title: Louvre Museum
text: I enjoyed watching it because there were many cultural properties, but recently, thieves have been forced to close in many places, so it was very inconvenient to move to other buildings. It is a bad decision to make ordinary people suffer.

title: Louvre Museum
text: One of the most famous and important museums of the world, entrance is 22 euros, it was very crowded and with some areas closed. Most of the elevators were not working and with a pregnant person it’s stressful and tiring. They should fix it.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
The mob of humanity coursing upstream like spawning fish to see the Mona Lisa, Nike, Venus di Milo, etc. is unbelievable! This is largest museum on planet Earth and most visitors only ran through in less than an hour (which is less time than waiting to get in!). The staff in general was helpful and organized. The signage (or lack thereof) was less than helpful. There has to be a better system than the one currently in place! We went the wrong way twice because it wasn't obvious there was a stairway around the back of the large stairway. A sign would've helped with that.

There was a line to stand in front of the Mona Lisa but you could skip it to look at the other art in the room. The staff in charge of the ML area got mad at visitors not in line for taking photos of the ML even though there was a good view. They kept yelling "no photos!" They should put up curtains or have the Mona Lisa in it's own room instead of yelling at visitors.

Overall it felt like a money grab with all the stores in the lower level, throngs of people paying to get in and head straight to the Starbucks, etc. The art was definitely not the priority.

title: Louvre Museum
text: We had tickets for 10:00. We arrived early and the lines were already very long. We were told that there were technical issues that made it so they opened the museum late. So all of the 9:00,  and 9:30 ticket holders still had not entered. (Meanwhile the entrance for groups bookings was allowing them through, perhaps they could have opened lines there to allow individuals in as well).  Then, after we finally entered the museum, the entrance to the Denon wing was closed so we had to go through the Sully gallery. The area with the Mona Lisa is packed. The museum director should consider a system to make it so people move through ( for example, the Tower of London has a slow conveyor belt that allows everyone to see the crown jewels up close and it moves people along. Something like this would be a great addition here.). Finding your way around is also confusing. Not all of the rooms are numbered so you have to guess where you are. Some hallways dead end and stairs don't go to all of the floors, so you have to backtrack to get around.  There are minimal signs at each piece, so you don't get any information about the artworks you are seeing. On the Louvre website, you can see which days each rooms are open. I specifically got tickets for a day that would have the rooms I wanted open, but, guess what, some rooms are still closed anyway. We did not get to see the Crown of Charlemagne in the Gallery D' Appollon, nor the Coronation of Napoleon, which were two of things we really wanted to see.  Overall, our opinion is that this museum is overrated and poorly run. After visiting many museums in Europe, I can say that this is my least favorite.  You will spend a lot of time here, and see very little. Disappointed! Nevertheless, there are some great things in the museum which deserve appreciation.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
We visited this museum over the weekend and it was very crowded. Unfortunately, when we got inside, we really wanted to visit the Egypt section, but after searching around for a while, we realized it was closed for renovations. Only a very small section with very few statues of the Egypt exhibit was accessible.
🤦‍♀️😒
When we purchased our tickets online from the museum’s official website, nothing about this closure was mentioned.
When we asked the staff about it, they responded rudely and impatiently. When we asked why it wasn’t mentioned on the website, they told us to “ask the website😕” and behaved very nervously. 😒We even contacted customer support to complain about their behavior, but received no response.😒
The Mona Lisa area was extremely crowded and chaotic. Sometimes the staff protecting the painting would stand directly in front of it, which was unpleasant. Some people were allowed to get much closer while regular visitors were not. When we asked about this, we were told it’s a personal matter.
What personal matters?😕
The ventilation in some sections of the museum wasn’t working and it was very hot. The museum shops are extremely expensive; we paid 6 euros for a single bottle of water, which is unfair. The restrooms were very dirty and didn’t have paper towels.
Overall, this was a very disappointing experience for us. I definitely won’t visit this museum again, and I hope others have a better experience.🙁

title: Louvre Museum
text:
What’s the point of buying tickets in advance if you have to wait for more than one hour outside in windy and rainy weather?

We arrived 30 mins in advance but were let in 40 mins after our designated time slot. Very poor organization overall - should either hire more securities to fasten the entrance or sell less tickets. At the end of the day we were left with very little time to explore the exhibitions, especially taking into consideration that some halls close 30 mins before the declared time, so you can only guess if you’ll be able to visit all the rooms you planned to visit.

The museum is fascinating though!

title: Louvre Museum
text:
A Majestic Experience with Some Practical Hurdles for Families

The Louvre is undeniably breathtaking—an immense facility filled with some of the world’s most stunning and iconic art. It’s a must-visit at least once in a lifetime for any art lover or curious traveler. We pre-booked a tour with the tour guy.

However, for families with small children, especially those using a stroller, the experience can be a bit challenging. The museum isn’t very stroller-friendly. On the day we visited, the elevators weren’t working, and we had to carry our (fairly bulky) jogger stroller up and down stairs—definitely not ideal. If you’re visiting with a baby or toddler, an umbrella stroller would be a much better option.

The museum was very crowded, which made navigating with a stroller even more difficult. We were told there is a baby room, but it was extremely hard to locate. We spent over 30 minutes asking multiple staff members, receiving different directions each time, and eventually gave up. We ended up breastfeeding and changing our baby behind a staircase after getting approval from a kind staff member. Definitely not what you expect from such a world-renowned institution.

Also, while the Louvre is mostly indoors, some rooms can get surprisingly warm on summer days. We were lucky to visit on a cloudy day, but even then, a few sections were uncomfortably hot.

All that said, the art and architecture are absolutely worth experiencing. Just be prepared if you’re visiting with young children—it’s doable, but requires patience and flexibility.

title: Louvre Museum
text: Amazing museum but poor coordination with vendors like Expedia since we've got Pariscity pass and museum barcode reservation but we were forced to buy tickets again. That was a cheating scam and many tourist were complainig about it.Hope they fix this inconvenience!

title: Louvre Museum
text:
I strongly suggest booking tickets online well in advance and making sure you arrive on time for your entrance slot. Unfortunately, we arrived 20 minutes late due to traffic and, even though our tickets allowed access through the Pyramid (which would have been a special experience), we were redirected to another entrance ‘because we were late.’ That line was at least ten times longer than the Pyramid entrance, which wasted even more time. This really matters when your slot is 13:30 and the museum closes at 18:00 on a Monday.

The museum is huge and definitely needs a full day if you want to see it properly. It’s very easy to get lost inside, even with the map, as room numbers are hard to spot and there isn’t much staff around to guide visitors. There also aren’t many explanation signs—mostly just small arrows pointing you through a particular section, which you don’t really need to follow. On top of that, there are many guided groups inside, and guides often don’t manage their groups well. They block passages, viewpoints, and photo opportunities, with little regard for other visitors outside their tour. It often feels as though they only care about their group’s experience, not about sharing the space respectfully.

Seeing the Mona Lisa (Gioconda) was also underwhelming: you can’t get close, there’s a big glass screen that causes reflections (even with just a phone), and you’re packed in shoulder-to-shoulder with hundreds of people. Staff don’t seem to manage the flow in a way that improves the experience, other than occasionally moving a barrier to let people exit. Strangely, they were strict about not stepping under the rope before it was opened, but not about visitor comfort.

Overall, I found it a bit overrated—many rooms felt similar to Versailles or other Paris museums. Some sections I wanted to visit were closed for works, and others were inaccessible by the time closing approached. In short: plan well, manage expectations, and be prepared for crowds.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
Over rated.
For such a huge name, you expect perfect experience.
We had to ask multiple times to find where we wanted to go.
Their map is very basic and not covering the areas properly.
Anyways, this is a must see place at least once in your life.
We loved what we saw, but still we had to spend more time finding what we were after, than actually enjoying them.

title: Louvre Museum
text: Overwhelming. We purchased the audio guides and barely used them as very few of the pieces had commentary associated. We found google lense and chat gpt were better sources. We all preferred the Musee d'Orsay. Despite this, I'm glad we went. Given the chance, you have to experience the Louvre once.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
The size of the of the museum is enormous, it is filled with lots of awesome artifacts.
Unfortunately, it is very poorly organized.
- It's hard to get from one place to another
- Rooms are labeled poorly, sometimes the label is missing but if it is there, there's only one that is always inside the room instead of at the entrance(s) of it
- The paper map is not very good, it shows rooms on levels that you cannot get from that level (only from another one)
- Sometimes you need to do unnecessary detours because a room entrance is closed or it's one-way only, the map doesn't show these
- Sometimes huge detours are need (going to another level) since sections are closed, the map doesn't show these and museum workers are not aware of what exactly is closed only that something is closed somewhere there
- Accessing food/water is hard, most of the places are outside of the entry gate so you need to get in line again or pay a lot for food inside
- The room of the Mona Lisa is extremely crowded, adding separators to form a single moving line might help

title: Louvre Museum
text: Visiting the louvre was one of the things on my wish list but unfortunately my visit was very disappointing, first thing i went on Friday and i found that the Egyptian great hall is closed every Friday and nothing was mentioned during my booking, nothing is really organized throughout the museum. I went before to the hermitage in Russia and i can say it is much more better

title: Louvre Museum
text: Went on Wednesday without buying a ticket in advance. The queue to security moved quite quickly but the line for buying tickets was very long and moving slowly as only 2 counters were open, so in the end we got tickets from the specially able tickets desk. Museum was overcrowded especially in front of the Mona Lisa in the Denon wing. We managed to do the Louvrelite and just wanted to leave as it was really hot and long day. Be prepared for loads of walking and stair climbing.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
A very big museum. You won’t be able to cover it in one day, so choose your favorite topic and go straight there. If you start at 9:00, you’ll have the chance to avoid the crowds.

You can get a map at the entrance, which is a good idea unless you enjoy getting lost. There are also restaurants and shops inside the building, but they are usually a bit overpriced.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
Absolutely nothing like it in the world! Expect very long lines. We have tickets for 10:30 AM in the morning and it took approximately 40 minutes to enter.

Rain and cold so definitely check the weather and dress appropriately. Take extra precautions and be ready for miles of walking. I really mean miles walking.

title: Louvre Museum
text: Buy tickets on line is the easier way. It is super crowded but still orderly. There are 3 main wings covering paintings and sculptures. If you don't have the whole day for this museum and wants to make sure you see Mona Lisa painting, go to "Denon" Hall right away. Same direction if you are keen only on paintings from France and Italy. It takes easily 1.5 to 2 hrs in this wing. The other 2 halls are mainly sculptures and antiques from reknown artists and sculptors. Good to engage a guide or get audio guide in advance so that there are highlights and commentaries making the visit more meaningful.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
Came on a sunday and this museum is really really big. It was pretty packed that day and a bit warm in the museum, so I suggest wearing comfortable shoes and clothing if deciding to explore around. There's a café within the museum for a quick coffee and a quick bite to eat. Its best if you get tickets in advance to avoid the long lines and wait times. A few buses do run in the area as well so getting there by public transportation wouldn't be too hard. Overall its a great place to go if you're into art and other historical events.

4/5

title: Louvre Museum
text: Despite the overwhelming crowds and chaos even in November the “low” season, very beautiful works and some quiet corners to slow down and appreciate the art.multiple legendary works worth  seeing- though the Mona Lisa is very overrated and can be skipped without missing a damned thing lol don’t waste your time waiting for hours, that one just google it.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
Do not trust the staff.....
I had a 9:00 a.m. ticket, arrived at 7:00 a.m. for a better viewing. I followed the instructions of the staff and got to the front of the line. An hour later I was informed that the opening time was delayed.
Then I was told that I would be given priority admission, but after waiting for 3 hours (I even arrived early just in case), there was already a long line.
It was crazy that I approached the same staff and offered to be given priority entry but was told to wait in the general line.

The collection is spectacular, and many of the staff in the building were nice and helpful. I will definitely come back!

title: Louvre Museum
text:
Do remember to reserve beforehand. Their educator ticket only applies to art, but does not restrict to Europe (US may also enter for free with identities).
It was just too big and crowded inside, especially when it came to Mona Lisa… there was no order. People in front row could stay as much time as they want and the whole line would not move. They should better organize the flow/crowd.

You got to see NIKE as well, besides Mona Lisa. It was also very stunning. Also the napoleon crowns and diamonds.

The inner decorations were amazing

Other than that and a few paintings, there was nothing so special. Probably I was not very good at appreciating the arts or neither do I have good knowledge.

There was special night admission on Wednesday and Friday and they are open until 9:45.

You can wait for sunset and get really good photo with dark blue theme and lightning pyramids.

It’s just too large and spread out (things mentioned above are concentrated though). Be prepared to walk a lot. Also, don’t get too ambitious in seeing everything. Do some work and plans ahead.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
Enormous. More steps than I have even seen in any museum, great exercise! Filled to the brim with artefacts.
I was hoping to see an Egyptian collection to rival the British museum, it did not.
The map does not even cover all of the rooms.

title: Louvre Museum
text: Even if you don't like museums, you must visit this one! Don't just look at the exhibits, look up at the incredible decor of this incredible place! The details in the ceilings and walls are alone historic and artistic treasures. Food is not so easy to get, so remember to eat first! Mona Lisa is there, but everyone is underwhelmed by its small size!

title: Louvre Museum
text:
I live in Rome, which is also a very touristy city, but I honestly didn’t expect such poor organization from the Louvre. We bought tickets for 11:00 a.m., arrived at 10:45, and only got inside at 12:20. All that time we were standing in line just to enter so buying a ticket for a specific time doesn’t guarantee admission at all.

We visited just one day before the jewelry theft at the Louvre ,  it’s absurd how they manage security and send guards in the wrong direction. We used the side entrance (which is supposed to be less crowded than the main one through the pyramid), yet we still waited for an hour and a half. The security check system is terrible ,  only one staff member and one scanner for such a massive crowd. Meanwhile, the thieves somehow managed to sneak in through an outside staircase.

Inside, it was just as chaotic,  way too many people and absolutely no organization. I’ve worked as a tour guide in Rome, so I know what crowds are like, and I was prepared for that. But what I saw at the Louvre was just a complete failure of organization.

title: Louvre Museum
text: It was worth it to see the Mona Lisa, but alas, I wasn't much interested in the other exhibits. Taking nothing away from the Museum though. It's a beautiful place and absolutely enormous

title: Louvre Museum
text: Lots of art, you can literally walk for hours and keep finding new stuff. The security staff needs to keep the lines organized to see the Mona Lisa. The food at the café was disappointing, better save that money and go to the bakery right next to the museum.

title: Louvre Museum

The museum is just so beautiful with benches for people to sit and enjoy 😉 the moment ❤️ and to take some snacks 😋 if you went with some sandwiches, nuts 😋 water, juice (little things like that) you'll be pleased 😌joined with a good weather I guarantee 💯

title: Louvre Museum
text:
The Louvre is always a good idea.
I booked my ticket a month in advance, and maybe that’s why everything felt so seamless—even the rain didn’t matter. I entered through a quieter side entrance, skipping the chaos at the main gate, and for a moment, it felt like the museum opened just for me.

Yes, it was crowded—tourists everywhere—and no, I didn’t see the Mona Lisa up close. I stood about 20 feet back, zoomed in with my camera, and took it in from afar. Too. Many. Tourists. !!!!! And still… it was enough.

Because the real magic of the Louvre is not in one painting—it’s in the endless corridors of history, light, shadow, and silence between brushstrokes. It’s the quiet awe that settles in your chest as you move from sculpture to canvas, room to room, century to century.

I spent four hours inside and could have stayed four more. The Louvre doesn’t just house art—it holds time, memory, and wonder. It’s not just a museum; it’s a feeling. One that lingers.

title: Louvre Museum
text: The Louvre is huge! I wish it had ac but understand can't happen. Lol make reservations ahead of time. Wear comfortable shoes. I'd get a guide if you want to know more history on the art. Mona Lisa is so packed so be careful as people push.

title: Louvre Museum
text: The most visited museum in the world, with encyclopedic collections spanning from Ancient Egypt to European painting. Seeing the Mona Lisa is iconic, though many, specificially me, every time, are surprised by its small size; and in my view some lesser-known works are even more impactful and finely executed. Overall, only the Hermitage in St. Petersburg, with its vast masterpieces, specifically painting, and imperial setting, surpasses it.

title: Louvre Museum
text: The museum is fantastic, but staff can be rude. Make sure to arrive early due to people traffic. Definitely recommend more crowd control and queue in some areas like the Mona Lisa exposition.

title: Louvre Museum
text: the museum is gorgeous and gigantic! the amount of art and history that is in one location is incredible. we got there 10 minutes before our timed tickets and waited for about 15 minutes before getting into the museum. people who got there at the time of ticket or after had to wait much longer. so make sure to go a little early. once inside, they have free lockers to put keep your belongings while you walk around. the reason why i subtracted a star was because of how crowded it was to see Mona Lisa. there is no order when heading to the room to see Mona Lisa, there is a very specific area for viewing and it was extremely crowded. staff does not guide people to move and there is no time limit to how long you can spend so you either have to be patient or shove your way to the front. i wish there was a better process and more efficiency when it comes to viewing the Mona Lisa. they needed to keep the crowd moving along so everyone can get a chance to view. other than that, i really enjoyed the museum and a whole day could have been spent there.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
This is a very touristy site ... even with tickets took 45 mins to get in. It is very nice inside with a large volume of items to view and enjoy however the sheer amount of people really takes away from enjoying the artwork.
Seeing the Mona Lisa was like a zoo, lots of shuffling to get a position, not a particularly fun experience especially if you're with a group of people. It felt a little like Disneyland.

title: Louvre Museum
text: This museum is full to the brim with art. I spent 5 hours wandering the halls, and walked past the majority of things within. The collection is very impressive. I docked a point because there is no clear through line on any of the floors. Also, it was difficult to find the room numbers on many of the floors, which meant that I got turned around repeatedly trying to follow the map.

title: Louvre Museum

The tour guide knows the highlights and moves you through them efficiently as navigating on your own is difficult at best as there are many  many rooms that create a maze like effect.

title: Louvre Museum
text: Tickets need to be booked well in advance, and it’s usually very crowded, so I recommend getting a timed entry ticket. There are so many paintings and artworks that you need at least half a day to really explore. The audio guide is also worth booking, as it helps you understand the history and background better. title: Louvre Museum
text: Truly beautiful but busy museum. Many tourists flock here to see the famous art pieces. Didn’t stay for very long as we were in a rush and had places to go but it is definitely worth a visit.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
We loved the museum. Every peace was exceptional would love to spend much more time to truly appreciate each and every one. We spent there over 4 hours and we truly only scratched the surface. It is a must for everyone.

The food however was passable at best. We stopped half way to get a very nice looking snack with coffee. Although, the pastries were nice, we found coffee burned and we decided not to drink it. Other food at the location was just OK. But for the price we recommend bringing your own foods.
We felt like this was a massive missed opportunity, as the English Museum had a very lovely English tea option on the menu. Which while expensive was worth the full experience.

title: Louvre Museum
text: Went here with a 14.30 prebooked ticket, despite arriving 30 minutes before the queue was long already. The queue moved relatively quickly once the allocated time occurred. Definitely recommend prebooking a ticket, but better to book early i.e. 9am or 5pm as it gets extremely busy and seating around the area is always occupied and many popular art pieces like the Mona Lisa are extremely busy as some people hog the area whilst taking hideous selfies.  Staff do try to usher people as best as possible. There is so much to see that it is recommended to get a map and highlight must see artifacts or use Chatgpt to raise an itinerary for the required duration of your visit. Many many beautiful pieces of mesmerising art pieces throughout the museum so be prepared to walk! Food within the canteen is expensive so be warned and seats are classed as hostile environment i.e. not designed to be comfortable for long periods of time. Make sure that you obtain a map at the foyer as staff anywhere else within the museum roll their eyes when you  ask them for one. The history of the Lourve was also actually very interesting and is worth it as it was very quiet and intriguing. This visit was on a Wednesday weekday and at 14.30 it was heaving!

title: Louvre Museum
text:
What to say about the Louvre. It’s the most famous art museum in the world. The building and art are overwhelming and you could spend days inside here.

However, over crowding is detracting from the experience. It is packed - lines for the toilets, Mona Lisa is 15 rows of people deep, people everywhere. When you have a 20 person line at every women’s toilet, you’ve let too many people into your museum.

Also, on a 75 degree day the museum was warmer than the outside and downright humid in places. The heat detracted from the experience along with the crowds.

Tips:
-Enter via the carousel entrance. It has a much shorter line than the pyramid.
-Purchase timed tickets for 9am and hope the museum opens on time (we were stuck in the plaza for 45 min due to a delayed opening) OR purchase the Louvre membership and enjoy a fast track queue and the ability to enter/exit exit throughout the day.
-Bring your patience, hydrate, and be prepared for the crowds.

title: Louvre Museum
text: You really need a lot of patience to stand in line and get inside, but it’s worth it at least once in a lifetime. The Louvre is unique and full of masterpieces — definitely an experience to remember.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
***** PRINT YOUR PASS! *****

Nobody wants to see you trying to get a signal while you're in the line and worse yet when you're showing them screen shots from booking or viator which are not the passes to get in!  See the pic, this is the only pass that will let you into the Louvre Museum.

Please be respectful and learn some basic French as the world comes to visit this location and the people trying to help you are just overwhelmed.

Culture, beauty, sensory overload!  We were only here for 3 days, coming back as this was only a taste of what this magnificent country has to offer.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
⭐ 5/5 — Iconic, Romantic, and Absolutely Magical at Night

Even without going inside, visiting Louvre Museum was an unforgettable experience. I didn’t enter the museum itself, but simply being outside and seeing the Louvre Pyramid was more than enough to understand why this place is so iconic. At night, the pyramid lights up beautifully, reflecting off the glass and surrounding structures in a way that feels elegant and timeless.

We visited while it was raining, and instead of taking away from the experience, it actually made it more magical. The reflections of the lights on the wet ground created a romantic atmosphere, calm and cinematic — truly one of those “Paris moments.”

The lighting, the symmetry, and the contrast between the modern glass pyramid and the historic buildings around it are stunning. It’s beautiful, peaceful, and captivating, even without stepping inside the museum.

A must-visit place in Paris, especially at night.
Iconic, romantic, and absolutely worth the stop — 5/5.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
25 years since my previous visit. Seems even busier now. Suggest pre-booking tickets and I would look to get a guided tour. We relied on my limited knowledge of the works 😅.
I spent the next day at the outside of the Louvre on Sunrise and had the place to myself, would suggest if the opportunity arises to head down early in the morning or later at night. We did both and it was magic.

title: Louvre Museum
text: A beautiful building filled with beautiful things! Spent over 3 and a half hours here and could have stayed longer. The free lockers for coats and bags was a brilliant addition although toilets can be a bit difficult to find.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
A Day at the Louvre

A single day at the Louvre is like sipping only a drop from an endless ocean. The treasures it holds cannot be embraced in so little time.

The Mona Lisa, though radiant, remained too far, watched from a distance, surrounded by a sea of admirers. Yet, my heart was captured by the sacred paintings of Jesus, Mother Magdalene, and St. Paul—their presence felt profound, and seeing St. Paul reminded me of the school in Darjeeling that carries his name and where my two boys studied.

Even the very bones of the Louvre whisper history. Marble and limestone, brass and terracotta, oak and walnut—materials chosen with such care—breathe life into its timeless halls. Every wall, every arch, seems to hold the echoes of centuries gone by.

The Louvre is not merely a museum; it is a cathedral of human spirit and faith, of beauty and resilience. One day was far too short—I left humbled, filled with wonder, and with a longing to return again.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
A great museum. Good structure. Good service. A day is not enough to visit everything. Recommend buying the tickets in advance and getting there on time. Nothing to complain.

The museum has expositions from a variety of fields. Asian, Egyptian, Italian, Greek among others cultures are shown.

title: Louvre Museum
text: A great organisation and a smooth operation. There are tons of visitors, but more antiques to be found. You will need days, weeks, months or even years to absorb what it offers.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
A Must-See, but Plan Wisely!

The Louvre is absolutely worth visiting — no question about it. But if you go in summer, be ready for crowds.

☝️Tips from my visit:
• Buy tickets online in advance (ideally for the morning).
• Arrive 30 minutes early.
• Bring your own water (inside, prices will eat you alive).
• Plan ahead which masterpieces you want to see first (Mona Lisa, Venus de Milo, Winged Victory).
• Travel light — no big bags, but free lockers are available.

Yes, the Mona Lisa has huge crowds (most just want a quick photo), but the Louvre is so much more than that. Don’t miss the Egyptian antiquities, Mesopotamian treasures, French paintings, and Napoleon’s Apartments — all just as impressive, with fewer people.

The Louvre isn’t just a museum, it’s another world.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
A Must-Visit in Paris!
The Louvre Museum is an absolute must-visit when in Paris. It’s truly worth spending a full day exploring — every corner offers something fascinating. The collection is incredibly rich, featuring art from all around the world. The Napoleon Apartments are stunning, beautifully preserved, and shine with royal elegance. Of course, seeing the Mona Lisa in person was unforgettable, but there’s so much more to discover beyond it. I especially enjoyed reading the stories behind each piece — it really brings the art to life. A wonderful experience overall!
I highly recommend buying tickets online in advance — it saves a lot of time.
Enjoy

title: Louvre Museum
text:
A truly amazing museum where to spend a whole day! Unfortunately the audio guide wasn’t available when we visited, but all key information are available next to paintings or can be browsed online.

Paintings are easy to find with clear visual instructions! The only room I found particularly crowded was were the Mona Lisa is displayed. But with some patience, you’re able to get to the front row in no time !

There are many snack bars and restaurants inside the museum that offer a wide selection of food and drinks.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
A truly iconic establishment housing a fantastic collection of art.
The various buildings are without doubt very stylish and a credit to the French nation and Architecture and Culture.
There are several very large plazas and gardens which are all well designed and maintained.
Definitely a must visit when you are in Paris.
You should spend the whole day to enjoy vast buildings, it's contents and the site as a whole.

title: Louvre Museum
text: A visit to the Louvre is a must. The architecture is amazing. The art offers something for everyone. Avoid crowds with an evening visit. Buying tickets in advance is recommended. title: Louvre Museum

title: Louvre Museum
text:
An unforgettable masterpiece of a museum

Visiting the Louvre was truly one of the highlights of my time in Paris. From the moment you step beneath the iconic glass pyramid, you feel the weight of history, art, and human creativity all around you. The museum itself is breathtaking — a magnificent former palace that is as impressive as the treasures it holds.
The collection is extraordinary in both scale and quality. Seeing world-famous works like the Mona Lisa, the Venus de Milo, and the Winged Victory of Samothrace in person was unforgettable, but what surprised me most was discovering incredible pieces in quieter galleries throughout the museum. Every room tells a story, spanning thousands of years and countless cultures.
Despite its size, the Louvre is well organized, and the staff were helpful and welcoming. I appreciated the clear signage and the many places to pause, reflect, and simply take in the beauty around me. It’s impossible to see everything in one visit, which only makes me want to return.
The Louvre is not just a museum — it’s a journey through art, history, and civilization. Whether you’re an art expert or a first-time visitor, it’s an inspiring, awe-inspiring experience that lives up to its legendary reputation. I would recommend it to anyone visiting Paris without hesitation.

title: Louvre Museum
text: As expected, it was spectacular. The museum is so enormous that you really need adequate time to go through it. We had a guide that took us to the highlights which I highly recommend. Whatever you do, purchase tickets ahead of time.

title: Louvre Museum
text: Be prepared to spend alot of time here, after all, it IS the largest museum in the world.  I'm not sure that even several months would be enough time!  The earlier you go the better. It's a museum experience like none other!!

title: Louvre Museum
text: Beautifully grand. De Louvre has multiple floors and separate “wings” that display seperate genres of artwork. You expect the artwork to be amazing, but the building itself was magnificent! You must purchase tickets in advance because they will be sold out, but if you have tickets there is little to no wait to enter. For easier navigation proceed to the information desk and acquire a map (available in multiple languages) and plan a route accordingly. Once you have visited one wing, proceed back to the center starting area to enter a seperate wing. Bring comfortable shoes as you will spend hours here. #1 museum in the world for a reason.

title: Louvre Museum
text: Being one of the largest, oldest, and grandest museums in the world, the Louvre hosts a treasure trove of artworks spanning diverse cultures and periods of history. The main attractions at the museum are the Mona Lisa, the Venus de Milo, Winged Victory, and stunning collections of prized paintings and Greek and Roman statues.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
I highly recommend buying tickets in advance. Even with pre-purchased tickets, be prepared to wait in line for about an hour before entering. The place is absolutely massive – you would need several days to see everything. One full day is enough to see the main highlights, but it requires a lot of walking and energy.

Security checks bags and backpacks at the entrance, but you are allowed to bring snacks and drinks with you. There's also a café inside, although we decided to skip it after waiting around 40 minutes in line – it might be worth considering adding more food options on-site.

If you want to see the Mona Lisa, head straight there. We visited outside of peak season, and the line was still huge. Once you manage to get close, you’ll have maybe a minute to admire the painting and take a photo. If you're into the truly great masterpieces, you'll find fewer crowds in other areas – and they are absolutely worth your time.

In my opinion, this is the best museum in the world. I could honestly spend years there, haha. As someone who loves history, the highlight for me was seeing the Code of Hammurabi. I was surprised it's not more crowded – it's an incredible piece of human history.

Highly recommended, and despite the queues, I will definitely come back. I’m curious to discover more hidden gems that will make me fall even more in love with this museum.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
I visited the Louvre — a place that feels less like a museum and more like a condensed journey through human history. You enter through the former royal palace of France and walk into corridors where civilizations sit side by side: Ancient Egypt with its mysterious statues and inscriptions; Ancient Persia, with Achaemenid reliefs and artifacts that reflect order, power, and grandeur; Greece with its balance and ideal beauty; and Rome with its sense of scale and realism.

In the European art sections, the paintings tell the story of centuries of thought, religion, and humanity, and the Mona Lisa — despite its small size — acts like a focal point drawing every gaze. The Louvre is vast and rich in detail; after a few hours, you realize it can never be fully seen in one visit. It ultimately reminds you that civilizations are not separate, but part of a continuous chain, each adding something to our shared human memory.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
It seems ludicrous to write a review about the Louvre, but I've done dumber things. Like writing a review about Nothing, Arizona, when there was literally nothing. The opposite is true here as the Louvre is everything an art museum should be. With over 35,000 pieces and over nine miles of exhibition space, it can be overwhelming, but some pre-planning helped make the most of the three-plus hours my husband and I spent here.

First things first, buy time-stamped tickets as soon as you know when you'll be in Paris. That's what we did, and it allowed us to go at the earliest time possible to avoid the worst of the crowds. Check to see if the Porte des Lions is open. Far less used than the glass pyramid, that's the side entrance closest to the Denon Wing where most of the famous works are. Currently Google Maps states it's temporarily closed, but it's worth checking.

Well before coming here, you should decide what the essential works are you want to see. Meandering the Louvre without a plan will tucker you out, and seating is at a premium. My husband and I wanted to see the acknowledged Top 10 works on a navigational path that allowed us time to browse with minimal backtracking. We started on Level 1 of the Denon Wing and sprinted to Room 711. That's where Leonardo da Vinci's "Mona Lisa" hangs.

It's small, under glass, and you can see it only from a restricted distance. But believe me, the crowd you see first thing in the morning reflects the best time to check this masterpiece off your list. Flip around and enjoy the museum's largest painting, "The Wedding Feast in Cana" by Veronese. Head to the back of the room and make a right to approach "The Winged Victory of Samothrace" in Room 703, a truly majestic sight.

This was where Audrey Hepburn floated down the steps in her red gown and gossamer shawl in "Funny Face". Walk beyond the mythic statue and make a right into the glorious Room 705 where you get up close to the resplendent French Crown Jewels. Walk back out and head to Room 702 where you see "The Coronation of Napoleon", a massive historical recreation by Jacques-Louis David. Continue to Room 700 and see two masterpieces.

The uplifting "Liberty Leading the People" by Eugene Delacroix and the darkly tragic "The Raft of the Medusa" by Theodore Gericault. Go downstairs to Room 403 on Level 0 still in the Denon Wing. That's where we saw the beautiful neoclassical sculpture,  "Psyche Revived by Cupid's Kiss" by Antonio Canova. Walk over to Room 345 and join the admiring crowd ogling the Venus de Milo.

At this point, we allowed ourselves to roam among the Roman, Greek, and Egyptian antiquities. It's an expansive space and hard to maneuver if you're looking for pieces in particular. We found the Great Sphinx of Tanis in Room 338 of the Sully Wing and eventually the Code of Hammurabi from ancient Mesopotamia in Room 227. It's as mythic as the Rosetta Stone. We recommend strolling through the sculpture courtyard.

It was quite a collection of magnificent pieces in an atrium-like setting. We weren't aware there was an Angelina on the premises, and it wasn't the zoo the original one is off the Tuileries Gardens. We managed to enjoy their fabled hot chocolate and a light brunch without waiting for a table. We did all of that in a little over three hours and still felt there was so much more to discover and explore. Yes, the Louvre is the opposite of Nothing.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
Visiting the Louvre Museum in Paris is not just a trip to a museum — it’s a journey through thousands of years of human history, art, and civilization. I spent nearly 6 hours here and still felt like I had only scratched the surface. Whether you're an art enthusiast, a history buff, or simply curious about one of the most iconic museums in the world, the Louvre is an unforgettable experience.
✨ Highlights:
Of course, the Mona Lisa is a must-see. It's smaller than expected, but the crowd and the energy around it speak volumes about its importance. Go early if you want a decent view without too many heads in the way!
The Winged Victory of Samothrace and Venus de Milo are breathtaking in person. The scale, detail, and emotion carved into these ancient masterpieces are simply beyond words.
The Napoleon III Apartments were a total surprise — extravagant, ornate, and transporting. If you ever wondered how royalty lived, this section of the Louvre is your answer.
The Egyptian Antiquities collection is massive and beautifully curated. Mummies, sarcophagi, statues, and ancient artifacts are laid out in a way that tells a coherent story of the time.
🏛 Architecture:
The building itself is as impressive as the art it houses. The Louvre Palace is grand, elegant, and immense. Walking through its vast corridors, marble staircases, and vaulted ceilings feels like being in a movie. Don’t forget to look up — many ceilings are painted masterpieces in their own right.
🖼 Layout & Navigation:
The museum is HUGE. It’s easy to get lost, but honestly, that’s part of the fun. Grab a map or download the Louvre app ahead of time. The museum is divided into three wings — Denon, Sully, and Richelieu — each with its own treasures. Planning your route ahead of time can help you make the most of your visit.
⏱ Tips for Visiting:
Arrive early. Lines can be long, especially during peak tourist seasons. Booking tickets online in advance is highly recommended.
Wear comfortable shoes. You’ll be walking a lot.
Don’t rush. Prioritize what you want to see, and be okay with not seeing everything in one visit.
Bring water and snacks. The café options inside are pricey and get crowded fast.
🎟 Ticketing & Entry:
Security was efficient, and the entrance through the glass pyramid is iconic and smooth. Once inside, everything is well-signposted. Staff were friendly and helpful, though it’s a busy place, so be patient.
🌍 Overall Impression:
There are museums, and then there is the Louvre — a living archive of human creativity and history. No matter how much you prepare, nothing compares to standing in front of some of the most famous artworks in the world and feeling their power in person.
I would return again in a heartbeat, maybe focusing on just one wing per visit next time. This place is truly a once-in-a-lifetime experience — or maybe a dozen times in a lifetime if you’re lucky.

title: Louvre Museum
text:
Visiting the Louvre Museum is truly a memorable experience—a must for anyone coming to Paris. But preparation is everything: tickets should be booked well in advance, and choosing the earliest time slot helps you avoid the huge crowds that start lining up before the doors even open.

Because the museum is enormous, it’s worth studying the map and planning your route ahead of time. There are also many apps available to help you quickly find the iconic masterpieces if your time is limited. If that’s your case, go straight to the Mona Lisa—and don’t miss the nearby sculptures around her. For true museum lovers, though, give yourself plenty of time; the Louvre is vast and deserves slow exploration.

At the exit, you’ll find museum shops and a few famous cafés. The shops are better for souvenirs than for art—don’t expect rolled protective packaging for paintings or prints, and options are limited. Drinks and snacks are available in the cafés, but note that coffee must be consumed in designated areas (no wandering the halls with it). Water bottles are allowed, and pastries make a nice break between galleries.

In short, plan ahead, pace yourself, and expect to leave with memories—not necessarily a painting under your arm.
