title: Tate Modern
text: Tate Modern is a unique destination for lovers of modern art! Located in a former power station on Bankside, the architecture alone attracts visitors: the enormous Turbine Hall and the panoramic views of the city from the Blavatnik Tower. The collection includes works by such icons as Picasso, Warhol, Duchamp, Pollock, and Rothko. It also houses the monumental spider sculpture "Maman" by Louise Bourgeois. Tip: If you see a long queue to get in, try walking around the other side of the building; there's usually never a queue there.

title: Tate Modern
text:
Tate Modern is a very nice place to spend a few hours, especially if you enjoy contemporary art and don’t mind that some pieces require a bit of interpretation. The museum is well structured and, even when busy, rarely feels overwhelming. Entry is free, which makes it an easy addition to any London day. The café at the top is a highlight, offering a relaxed break with impressive views over the city.

Quietly rated: worth stopping.

title: Tate Modern
text:
Tate Modern is absolutely world-class. I came alone and still easily spent several hours wandering through the galleries. The variety is incredible — from paintings and sculptures to large-scale immersive installations with sound and lighting that completely transform the space. Every floor feels like stepping into a new environment.

I went on a Sunday around noon and was pleasantly surprised: unlike the British Museum, it wasn’t overwhelming or overcrowded. I could move at my own pace, pause to really take in the art, and there was no wait to get inside.

The building itself is part of the experience too — the vast turbine hall is breathtaking, and the café at the top offers fantastic views across the Thames and the London skyline. It’s a perfect spot to take a break and soak in the city from above.

Whether you’re deeply into modern art or just curious, this is one of London’s must-visit cultural landmarks.

title: Tate Modern
text: Tate Modern is an electrifying hub of modern art. Its iconic Turbine Hall and sprawling, free collection are unforgettable. From Picasso to provocative new commissions, the curation is superb. Always buzzing with energy, it's a vital, must-visit London experience that continues to challenge and inspire.

title: Tate Modern
text:
Tate Modern is easily one of my top three art museums in the world — a bold, ever-evolving space that continually delivers thought-provoking exhibitions and world-class curation. Housed in an iconic converted power station on the South Bank, the museum itself is as striking as the art it holds — vast, industrial, and unmistakable in its silhouette.

Entry to the main collections is free, and the building is massive, giving you plenty of space to explore without feeling rushed or crowded. On my recent visit, I caught the Leigh Bowery show, which was absolutely compelling. His short but extraordinary life left a distinct and undeniable mark on the art world and beyond — a powerful tribute to a true icon of performance, fashion, and identity.

Another highlight is the Media Networks section, which explores how artists over the past century have responded to the influence of mass media and ever-evolving technologies. It’s a fascinating look at shifting cultural narratives through the eyes of artists like Roy Lichtenstein, Mark Bradford, and China’s Wang Guangyi.

Even on a Saturday, it didn’t feel too packed. Its riverside location, proximity to great restaurants, and sheer scale make it an ideal cultural stop in the heart of London. A solid 5 out of 5 — inspiring, dynamic, and always worth returning to.

title: Tate Modern
text:
Tate Modern is one of London’s most iconic museums of contemporary art 🎨✨. Housed in a former power station on the South Bank, its vast Turbine Hall alone is worth the visit — a dramatic space that makes every installation feel larger than life. From Picasso to Rothko to cutting-edge contemporary works, the collection is bold, challenging, and endlessly inspiring. 🌍💎

The riverside setting adds to the magic 🌆🌉. With panoramic views of the Thames, Millennium Bridge, and St. Paul’s across the water, it’s as much about the experience as the art itself. Whether you’re an art lover or just curious, Tate Modern is a must-stop for anyone wanting to feel the pulse of creativity in London. 🌸🙌

title: Tate Modern
text: Tate Modern is one of my favourite art galleries – it has a huge exhibition scale, updates its content really quickly, the staff are all incredibly warm and friendly, and the vegan ice cream in the restaurant is my favourite dessert.

title: Tate Modern
text:
Tate Modern offers a dynamic mix of contemporary and modern art in an industrial, unconventional setting. Some works are powerful and captivating, others more abstract and challenging — but that’s part of the experience.
It was my second time visiting, and once again it proved to be a pleasant and engaging stop. Even if not every piece resonates, the atmosphere and creativity make it well worth the visit.

title: Tate Modern
text:
Tate Modern stands as a breathtaking testament to the evolution of creativity, where the industrial echoes of the past meet the boundless innovation of the present. Stepping into the vast, cavernous Turbine Hall, you immediately feel the museum's core philosophy: art is not a static relic, but a living, breathing dialogue that requires space to flourish.
What makes Tate Modern truly exceptional is its unapologetic openness to the modern era. It strips away the stuffiness often associated with high-class galleries, replacing it with a democratic, high-energy atmosphere. The thematic displays challenge you to look at art through the lens of society, technology, and activism, making even the most abstract concepts feel accessible and urgent. It is a place where digital media, performance art, and traditional painting coexist in a seamless, forward-looking narrative.

title: Tate Modern
text: Tate will always be Tate; a must-seen, a must-visited museum for everyone, complimentary entrance for everyone. Except for the permanent exhibition which is a legacy for the people, the building itself is also astonishing, the shop is well equipped and the walks rounds are great, too. I’ve been there already 5 times.

text: The place is a bit big but it's possible to go through all exhibits in one day. Most exhibits were interesting, while a few were not to my taste. There's also a nice garden to enjoy a good picnic on a sunny day.

title: Tate Modern
text:
The Tate Modern combines contemporary art with a unique riverside setting and a layout that encourages exploration. Many areas of the museum are free to access, making it easy to move between the Turbine Hall, the upper floors and several gallery spaces, while selected exhibitions require a ticket. The mix creates a flexible and welcoming atmosphere for different types of visits.

During the day the museum is lively, with several school groups and guided tours contributing to the rhythm of the space. Despite the activity, circulation remains smooth thanks to wide corridors, clear signage and multiple viewpoints that allow visitors to pause and observe.

The terrace offers an impressive panorama over the Thames, St Paul’s Cathedral and the London skyline, adding a memorable element to the experience. The combination of architecture, public areas and changing installations makes the Tate Modern one of London’s most rewarding cultural stops.

title: Tate Modern
text: The Tate Modern is a truly special place—I spent my weekend exploring its inspiring exhibitions and left feeling uplifted and full of creative energy. Every visit reveals something new and thought-provoking, making it an essential stop whenever I'm in London. It's more than just a museum; it's a space that fuels the imagination.

title: Tate Modern
text: The Tate Modern is always a good choice. With interesting temporary exhibitions and permanent displays, you will always find something new to see. It’s worth buying exhibition tickets online to save your spot and make the visit run more smoothly, but you can also buy them in person. And for fans of the museum gift shop, there are at least 3 to stumble across!

title: Tate Modern
text:
The Tate Modern is an absolute must-visit for anyone in London, whether you’re an art lover or just curious about contemporary creativity. Housed in a former power station on the South Bank, the space itself is stunning: industrial, vast, and full of energy. The Turbine Hall alone is worth the trip, it’s an ever-changing showcase of large-scale installations that always impress and challenge the imagination.

The museum’s collection is both thought-provoking and diverse, featuring world-class works by artists like Picasso, Rothko, Warhol, and Hockney. I especially enjoyed the way the galleries are organized by themes rather than strict chronology; it encourages you to make your own connections between different eras and styles. The free entry makes art accessible to everyone, which is something I truly appreciate.

Beyond the art, the Tate offers incredible views of London from the top-floor viewing terrace, a hidden gem for panoramic photos of St. Paul’s and the Thames. The gift shop is excellent, filled with unique books and design items, and the cafés serve surprisingly good coffee and pastries.

Crowds can be heavy on weekends, I went on weekend and it was crowded. My experience was really good. I can't explain in words actually I enjoy it fully and I don't know how I spent 5 hours here. Overall, the Tate Modern is a perfect blend of culture, architecture, and inspiration, a modern masterpiece in itself. Highly recommended for visitors and locals alike!

title: Tate Modern
text:
The Tate Modern is one of London’s most iconic art galleries, offering an impressive range of contemporary and modern art. Located in the former Bankside Power Station, the gallery’s vast industrial space creates a striking backdrop for its diverse exhibitions. Whether you're an art enthusiast or a casual visitor, Tate Modern has something for everyone.

One of its highlights is the sheer variety of art on display. The galleries house everything from classic modernist works to cutting-edge contemporary pieces. Fans of Pop Art will enjoy works from legendary artists like Andy Warhol and Roy Lichtenstein, whose bold colors and playful designs capture the essence of consumer culture. For those who appreciate obscure and conceptual art, Tate Modern pushes boundaries with installations that challenge perceptions and invite deep interpretation. These thought-provoking pieces, often blending media like sculpture, sound, and digital technology, provide a unique experience that lingers long after your visit.

Families and younger visitors will appreciate the kids’ drawing gallery, a wonderful interactive space where children can create their own artwork inspired by the exhibitions. It’s a great way to engage young minds and introduce them to the world of art in a fun and creative environment.

Beyond the exhibitions, Tate Modern also offers breathtaking views of the Thames from its top-floor viewing deck, making it worth a visit for the scenery alone. Whether you're there to admire the masterpieces or simply explore, Tate Modern is an unmissable destination for art lovers of all ages.

title: Tate Modern
text: This is just an outstanding museum in an amazing building - a work of art itself. I came here when it first opened and have loved it ever since. The collections are quite extraordinary. I nice way to spend a rainy afternoon.

title: Tate Modern
text: This is my favorite place in London. The walk from the station to the museum is also nice. There are various modern art pieces on display, but it is not busy and it is good to enjoy the works leisurely. There are many works that give me a lot of insight into the modern era. In addition to the art pieces, the cafes at the top and in the middle are all nice, and the Thames River seen from the museum is also very pretty. It is a great place to enjoy the works while contemplating alone rather than with others.

title: Tate Modern
text:
This is one of the free art galleries to explore in London. I have visited a few times, and I commend the management team for welcoming visitors from all faiths. They have a dedicated prayer room, so if you are Muslim and visiting this place, don’t forget to take the time for your prayers.

To find the prayer room, take the lift to level 4. Once you exit the lift, turn left, then head to the right. As you enter the second room, you will see a sign on the door indicating that it is a multi-faith space.

title: Tate Modern

title: Tate Modern

title: Tate Modern
text:
Visited during half term for the family activities, and it was a great experience. Free entry is always appreciated, and the Tate Draw activity with the interactive screens was a real highlight — my child loved seeing his drawing projected on the wall. You can even buy the artwork on a T-shirt, which is a lovely way to support the museum.

The children’s play zone in the Turbine Hall was fantastic for pre-school and KS1 ages — fun, safe, and much calmer than other half-term options like the Science Museum or NHM.

If I could suggest one improvement, it would be a more curated children’s exploration of the art, perhaps a dedicated kids’ gallery or trail, as it was a little tricky keeping a four-year-old engaged beyond the organised activities.

The Corner Café deserves a mention too — great food options, excellent single-origin coffee, and plenty of seating even when busy.

title: Tate Modern
text:
Visiting Tate Modern is an unforgettable experience. The building itself, a massive former power station, is impressive and unlike any other museum. Inside, you’re greeted with a vast collection of abstract and modern art that feels both bold and captivating. From Picasso to Warhol, the variety is amazing, and even if you’re not a modern art expert, there’s something intriguing for everyone.

The standard collections are free to explore, so you can spend hours wandering through the galleries without worrying about a ticket. The space is open and welcoming, perfect for a relaxed afternoon soaking in creativity. Plus, the views of the Thames from the upper floors are a nice bonus!

title: Tate Modern
text: Visiting the Tate Modern is always a treat. Whether you arrive by strolling across the Millennium Bridge or wandering up from Borough Market, the experience begins before you even step inside. title: Tate Modern
text:
We were so delighted to visit Tate Modern on our recent London jaunt. From the moment you enter, it’s a full-on, jaw-dropping, inspiring place. Louise Bourgeois’s Maman is the first thing you see and I squealed with delight. The last time I had an opportunity to see one of her awesome spiders up-close-and-personal was back in the early 2000,s in Roppongi Hills, Tokyo; it always stuck in my mind and it was pure pleasure to view this again.

There are too many fantastic pieces to pick out, but I will say that even if you don’t want to pay extra to visit the featured exhibitions, the permanent free display is excellent, and you could easily spend many hours wandering blissfully. Highly recommended.

title: Shanghai Museum East Campus
text:
Absolutely amazing brand new, huge museum -5 very large floors -don't miss it if you are staying in Shanghai a few days. It opened in July 2024 and has been curated by enlightened archeologist and art curators, Bravo!
It's free to get in, but they need to see your passport. There's lockers for luggage too. Very busy on Sundays.
Take a Didi cab ( only about 5 £), or take the free shuttle from the older branch called Shanghai Museum in the centre ( 45 mn bus ride which is wonderful to discover the huge town).
There are fantastic permanent exhibition halls, beautifully lit and with explanations in English: Bronze, ceramics, sculptures, ethnic costumes, calligraphy, and paintings. It's all about the Chinese culture and artistic, historical origins of the artifacts and creations.
You could spend 2 full days there - there's plenty of café options and seating corners and shops for souvenirs. A very welcoming space.
Outside, lovely restaurants choices and gardens in an ultra modern architectural complex.
Fabulous side to Shanghai!

title: Shanghai Museum East Campus

title: Shanghai Museum East Campus
text:
After a disappointing visit on Sat to the main museum at Renmin Square, I decided to check out the East Museum the next day.

No advanced reservations required for this (except for special kids activities/ workshops). The queue to enter was crazy long but moved considerably fast.

Audio guide for ¥20 in Mandarin, ¥40 for other languages. 5 levels, 12 halls of exhibits from Bronze to Pottery, Calligraphy, Paintings, Archaeology and special exhibitions.

Special exhibit: Hongshan Culture 红山文化古文明特展. Part 4 of a series of 何以中国 exhibition, the Hongshan era dates back 5000-6500 years. Said to be where the first definitions of how dragons are imagined to look like, painted pottery, extensive use of jade. Always in awe of the skills in making such precise cuts and designs given the limitations of equipment. The video in the middle is a must see, as it shows how we learnt to draw squares out of circles.

Spent the day here, covering the Bronze Hall, Pottery Hall, Archaeology Hall, Jade Hall and the Special Exhibit. Despite the crowds, I think because this is a really huge museum, it doesn't feel as packed as the other one. I certainly enjoyed this much more.

Just opposite the museum exit is the 花木时光里 Time Edition mall with a range of food options and shops.

text:
Shanghai Museum East Campus

A museum that can impress.  There is a modern huge building.  There are several thematic exhibitions with thousands of exhibits
- Chinese bronze, jade products, writing and calligraphy, numismatics, paintings, sculptures, Chinese art, archeology, and others.  There are several cafes inside.  It is interesting to observe how restorers work - their laboratories behind glass.   As always, you will have to go through security at the entrance, just like at an airport.  This museum, like other state museums, is free.  Cool souvenir shops.   Plan at least 5-6 hours for this museum.  It's definitely worth visiting.

Wish to add - you are to register beforehand for visiting the Shanghai Museum, but it is not necessary to register for visiting the Shanghai Museum East Campus

Unfortunately, the nearby museum (of science and technology) is closed, may be for renovations (November 2024)

title: Shanghai Museum East Campus
text:

title: Shanghai Museum East Campus
text: The entrace ticket is free. Im shocked with how big this museum is. You have to spend 3-4 hours here to have full experience

title: Shanghai Museum East Campus
text: The main museum was fully booked so we ended up taking a bus here, and we're glad we did! Huge museum full of Chinese works from all time periods. title: Shanghai Museum East Campus
text:
The Shanghai Museum East Campus is a stunning, modern home for China’s most prestigious treasures. With world-class Bronze, Ceramics, and Jade galleries, the layout is open, English-friendly, and truly impressive.

While the Main Campus at People’s Square now focuses on bringing the world’s history to China, the East Campus is clearly designed to showcase China’s history to the world.

title: Shanghai Museum East Campus

Need a whole day here to cover the four floors of permanent exhibitions, which consist of the Chinese Bronze, Calligraphy,  Painting, and Seal gallery.
Register your booking in advance on their website before going.  Take note that Tuesday is closed.

title: Shanghai Museum East Campus
text: This is the art museum you don't want to miss out as a Chinese art lover visiting Shanghai.  There is a colossal exhibition of ancient Chinese works of art ranging from paintings, calligraphy and porcelain to bronze, furniture and local Shanghai art.  Be prepared to spend at least a whole day to view and enjoy the ancient Chinese civilization.

title: Shanghai Museum East Campus
text: This is the larger branch of the museum. This place is more recommended. One of the best museums I have been if not the best. You can walk in as individual without reservations. FREE ENTRY. Free lockers with digital control although you may need to be able to read chinese for the instructions. Clean toilets. Very high quality displays.  Descriptions in Chinese and English. High educational value with interactive displays. There are even people who constantly wipe the glass surface to remove finger marks and forehead marks, to ensure spotless viewing. Seating available when you are tired. Certainly beats all the museums I have been in Europe and Japan.

title: Shanghai Museum East Campus

The other galleries/exhibitions like the seal and numismatics gallery are very informative, and it's nice some find some Dutch history as well in the ceramics gallery.

title: National Gallery of Art
text:
A great place to view our national Art treasures.
The National Gallery of Art is a beautiful and impressive museum located right off the National Mall on the north side, directly across from the National Air and Space Museum, making it easy to find while sightseeing in Washington, DC. The entrance feels grand, with a smooth security checkpoint and a free coat check room that I highly recommend using this feature so you can explore comfortably without having to carry around your extra things. The main entrance opens into a massive rotunda supported by 16 large columns and topped with a domed ceiling, which really highlights the museum’s elegant design. As you move through the galleries counterclockwise from the north entrance, the artwork appears to be displayed in chronological order, making it easier to follow the progression of art over time. The collection features works from around the world, and there is so much to see that you could easily spend days exploring. One challenge, however, is the lack of clear signage and limited stroller or wheelchair access at the south entrance off the Mall, which can make entry confusing for some visitors. Despite this drawback, the museum is calm, well organized, and overall a lovely place to visit with an incredible amount to offer.

title: National Gallery of Art
text: always good to see whats up at the nga. big complaint is that the rothkos are covered in glitter… pretty sure he didnt make em that way. no reason that should be the case. clean them!!! also more postcards of the contemporary art showing please.

title: National Gallery of Art

I love how the gift shop has many little pins and magnets to collect, the easiest gift to take home if you are flying. The little pins are so cute from all the major artists ☺️
It would take you more than a couple hours to get through all the building and that if you're going fast through it as well.

title: National Gallery of Art

The interior, color combination is out of the world. Had high hopes for the sculpture garden. After searching for it so long got disappointed. Barely had any sculpture

title: National Gallery of Art
text:
It had been way too long since my last visit to the National Gallery of Art, so I made a point to squeeze in an exhibit before it closes in January. I was already in the area for another event, which made it easy to park once and enjoy a relaxed 10-minute walk over. It was one of those beautiful DC days that makes walking the city feel effortless.

I had almost forgotten just how stunning the National Gallery of Art really is. The museum spans two iconic buildings, the East and West Buildings, along with the Sculpture Garden. On this visit, I focused on the West Building, and it was more than worth it.

Admission is free, with the option to donate. The gallery is open daily from 10am to 5pm, except for December 25 and January 1. It is one of the most accessible and rewarding cultural experiences in the city.

The exhibit was excellent and surprisingly extensive, located on the main floor. The National Gallery of Art is massive, giving you the freedom to explore almost any style or era of art at your own pace. Whether you come with a plan or simply wander, it always feels intentional.

Beyond the galleries, there are several other spaces to enjoy. There are peaceful courtyards with fountains, a large and beautifully curated gift shop featuring art prints, books, earrings, and unique souvenirs, and a spacious cafeteria.

I arrived later in the day, so the cafeteria was closing, but I was still able to grab gelato, which felt like a small win. They even had seasonal flavors like eggnog and peppermint, which was a fun and festive surprise.

This visit reminded me that the National Gallery of Art is more than a museum. It is a full experience. Next time, I plan to explore the underground tunnel, the East Building with its contemporary art, and the roof terrace and atrium.

title: National Gallery of Art
text:
One of my stops during my "tourist-for-a-day" adventure in my own city was the National Gallery of Art. The goal? To fulfill my niece's specific request: she wanted to see "Greek art"--meaning mythology, of course.

When we arrived, I grabbed a map and started scanning for anything that might fit the theme. My thought process was: anything Italian, any century, might lead us to something mythological. As I stood there plotting our route, a friendly staff member approached and asked what we were looking for. I explained our mission, and she helpfully pointed out that classical mythology wasn't a major focus in the collection, but mentioned there was a bust of Aphrodite upstairs, near Room 26.

She was right--there was a bust. But I'm glad I followed my instincts and kept wandering. As we explored the Italian art rooms, we began spotting Roman depictions of the gods and goddesses we were looking for. It hit me then: unless you're familiar with mythology, you might not realize that Roman art features the same figures as Greek mythology--just under different names.

In the end, we saw exactly what we'd hoped for: plenty of mythological references. There were artworks featuring Calliope, cherubs, Venus (aka Aphrodite), Mercury (Hermes), Neptune (Poseidon), and more. Mission accomplished.

That said, the layout of the Gallery can be a bit disorienting. Even with a map in hand, it's easy to lose track of where you are. Though, to be fair, that might've been user error.

title: National Gallery of Art
text: The National Gallery of Art is a spectacular journey through art history, offering a breathtaking collection that spans centuries and movements. From the enigmatic smile of Leonardo da Vinci's Ginevra de' Benci 🖼️ to the fleeting moments captured by Edgar Degas and the luminous landscapes of Monet 🎨, the museum is a treasure trove for art lovers. It's a thrill to see such a wide range of masterpieces, including works by Picasso and other modern titans, all under one roof. The best part? Admission is completely free 🆓, making it an accessible and enriching experience for everyone. This museum is a must-see for anyone visiting D.C. 🤩

title: National Gallery of Art
text:
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

Our visit to the National Gallery of Art was absolutely wonderful. Before even stepping inside, we were struck by the beautiful architecture and the grand columns that make the building so iconic. It is a stunning sight right on the National Mall.

The experience inside is just as impressive. The exhibits are set up awesomely, making it easy to navigate through the different galleries. We especially loved the internal gardens and the peaceful fountain areas—they provide such a serene atmosphere to take a break and reflect on the art.

Highlights of the visit:

Stunning Architecture: The exterior design and the massive columns are a highlight in themselves.

Beautiful Indoor Spaces: The garden courts and fountains are incredibly tranquil and well-maintained.

Great Curation: The artwork is displayed beautifully, with plenty of space to appreciate each piece.

A must-visit for anyone in DC, whether you are a serious art lover or just looking to enjoy one of the most beautiful buildings in the city!

title: National Gallery of Art
text:
5/5 Stars: A Masterpiece of a Museum - Smithsonian National Art Museum_

I recently had the privilege of visiting the Smithsonian National Art Museum in Washington D.C., and I must say, it was an unforgettable experience. As an art enthusiast, I was blown away by the museum's impressive collections, impeccable maintenance, and thoughtful exhibit organization.

From the moment I stepped inside, I was struck by the grandeur of the building and the warmth of the staff. The museum's vast collections, spanning over 7,000 years of human creativity, are a testament to the institution's commitment to preserving and showcasing the world's most iconic works of art.

The exhibits were meticulously maintained, with each piece carefully selected and thoughtfully displayed to provide context and spark curiosity. I was particularly impressed by the West Building's impressive collection of European and American art, featuring masterpieces by artists such as da Vinci, Rembrandt, and Picasso.

The museum's organization and signage made it easy to navigate the vast collections, and the interactive displays and educational programs added an engaging layer of depth to the exhibits.

The facilities were immaculately clean, with comfortable seating areas and amenities that made our visit enjoyable and stress-free.

In short, the Smithsonian National Art Museum is a must-visit destination for anyone who appreciates art, history, and culture. Its world-class collections, exceptional maintenance, and thoughtful exhibit design make it a truly unforgettable experience.

_Rating Breakdown:_

- Collections: 5/5
- Maintenance and Organization: 5/5
- Facilities and Amenities: 5/5
- Overall Experience: 5/5

_Recommendation:_

If you're an art lover, history buff, or simply looking for a world-class museum experience, the Smithsonian National Art Museum is an absolute must-visit. Be sure to plan your visit in advance, as the museum's vast collections and exhibits can easily fill a day (or two, or three...).

title: National Gallery of Art
text: A gem of a collection! Excellent information desk staff gave good recommendations. Spent a mesmerizing half day looking at highlights of the collections and old favorites such as Durer,, Leonardo, Vermeer, and other Florentine, Rennaissance and Flemish and Dutch masters.

The interior architecture of the building is also quite exquisite. There were lots of people taking photos of themselves inside. It was a bit strange that they only allowed backpacks to be hanging on one shoulder, not two. Highly recommend!

title: National Gallery of Art

title: National Gallery of Art
text: Absolutely stunning museum with an expansive collection of art. The collection offered here is truly stunning and outstanding. All facilities, exhibits, and sanctuaries within the gallery are meticulously arranged and decorated beautifully. All staff I encountered were incredibly kind and knowledgeable. When I visited, certain rooms were being renovated (I believe the impressionist rooms). A very kind staff member at an information desk marked a map where certain pieces of art I wanted to see had been relocated. She also suggested I download the National Gallery of Art app which had information about all the art, and recommended free tours offered by the museum. Overall, this is an excellently beautiful museum in DC with an expansive collection that anyone who enjoys viewing art will absolutely love.

title: National Gallery of Art
text: Absolutely wonderful experience and what really made our day was meeting the distinguished and friendly gentleman with the salt and pepper hair and red bow tie at the information desk, he was very kind and informative, he highlighted for me and my husband the best route to travel through the gallery and where all the most popular artwork was located. He deserves a raise, he was so passionate and excited for us to see it all and it shows how much he enjoys his job. He made us feel inspired to be a witness to some of the most beautiful works of art. And on that day (1/7/26) we were feeling especially somber…walking around DC and being reminded of so much history (good and bad)- all this to say, it was really nice to encounter someone who remained steadfast in keeping his kindness and humanity intact.

title: National Gallery of Art
text:
An unforgettable experience for art lovers.

The National Gallery of Art exceeded all my expectations. The collection is extraordinary and curated with remarkable care and expertise. I was especially moved by the works of Claude Monet—his vibrant garden scenes filled with lilies radiate peace, color, and emotional depth. Equally captivating was experiencing Jackson Pollock's expressive energy up close—his drip painting technique comes alive through every chaotic, vibrant stroke.

Each gallery is thoughtfully arranged to create an immersive and educational experience, from the old masters to modern and contemporary art. The museum is free to enter, beautifully maintained, and the architecture itself enhances the artistic experience.

Without a doubt, a must-visit spot in Washington D.C. for anyone seeking inspiration, history, and beauty at its finest.

title: National Gallery of Art
text: Art is in the eye of the beholder. Some I don't like, some I like, some I can look at for hours. It's not recommended to go to the statues that look cold and tweak them. No rubbing, even on the bronzes. Just sayin'. Also if it looks like a water fountain, it is.

title: National Gallery of Art
text: As an art student and professional, I strongly recommend starting from the second floor. This way, you can see the entire development of art history. Medieval art may seem rigid and unrealistic, but it serves as a valuable reference for later Renaissance and modern art. You can compare aspects such as human body structure, facial expressions, and the expression of emotions. Many people mention Leonardo da Vinci; I won't elaborate too much, he was truly a genius. However, I still recommend seeing Botticelli's portrait in the same room—it's equally brilliant. You also shouldn't miss Vermeer, Rembrandt, and El Greco; they are truly remembered by history. Don't forget there's another gallery building , which I think requires three days to see completely and is worth visiting repeatedly.

text: Loved it. I have been here many times over the decades, but this time was very special. The "special" thing about this exhibit is that it has material from the Louvre which has never been loaned to another gallery before, the NGA is the only place it will be exhibited, and probably the only time it will leave the Louvre. title: National Gallery of Art
text:
My first visit to the National Gallery of Art was an absolutely elating experience—a true feast for the senses that left me breathless!

Stepping into the museum, I was immediately struck by the sheer brilliance of its layout. The division into the neoclassical West Building and the stark, modern East Building, connected by the dazzling Multiverse light installation, is a stroke of architectural genius. It made navigating the vast collection feel seamless and provided a natural progression from the past to the present.

The West Building felt like a grand journey through history, with its serene Sculpture Halls and rooms dedicated to European masters. I was thrilled to witness iconic works like Leonardo da Vinci’s Ginevra de' Benci and Vermeer’s A Lady Writing, which are meticulously displayed, allowing for genuine moments of quiet appreciation. The organization by period and national origin truly makes the sheer scope of the collection manageable and immersive.

But the real joy for me was exploring the incredible range of eras of photography and other media showcased in the collection, particularly in the East Building. From early American photographs like Alfred Stieglitz's work to the impactful contemporary art, the collection beautifully charts the evolution of visual storytelling. I was delighted to see not only classic paintings like Van Gogh's Self-Portrait and Monet's Woman with a Parasol but also pieces by modern masters like Mark Rothko and the beautiful mixed-media work. It felt like walking through a living timeline of human creativity.

The clarity of the exhibitions and the effortless flow between classical sculptures, Renaissance paintings, and cutting-edge contemporary installations made the entire day a profound and unforgettable art pilgrimage. I left the museum completely invigorated, already planning my next visit to delve deeper into this national treasure.

text:
recent visit to the National Gallery of Art in Washington, D.C., provided an opportunity to assess its role as a prominent cultural institution. While the beauty of the artwork is undeniable, understanding its historical context and the reasons for its widespread appeal offers a more analytical perspective.

The National Gallery's establishment in 1937, largely through the efforts and initial donation of Andrew W. Mellon, highlights a specific historical moment and a particular vision. Mellon's intent was to create a national art museum accessible to the American public. This founding principle of accessibility, being free and open to all, is a key factor in its broad appeal, especially for those traveling from places like Baltimore, where access to major art collections might be more limited.

text:
The National Gallery of Art has a stunning permanent collection spread across multiple floors. The main building alone contains so many rooms and artworks that it is simply impossible to fully see and absorb the scale and richness of the collection in just one visit. We were less impressed by the modern exhibition. Although it was recommended by guides and staff at the entrance—especially as a starting point for families with children—we personally found it unengaging and difficult to understand. For us, it lacked depth and meaning, and we felt that time could have been better spent elsewhere.
We came specifically to experience the permanent collection and to see classical masterpieces that are 300–400 years old or even older. If you truly value traditional, timeless art, I would strongly recommend focusing your visit on the permanent exhibitions.
That said, we will definitely return, as we were unable to see all the exhibits during our visit. The gallery is vast, and there is so much more to explore and appreciate.

title: National Gallery of Art
text:
The National Gallery of Art in Washington DC is one of the most beautiful and inspiring museums in the country. From the moment you walk inside you are surrounded by elegance, tranquility, and extraordinary works of art. The architecture of both the West and East Buildings is stunning, blending classical and modern styles in a way that feels perfectly harmonious. The grand halls, marble floors, and high ceilings create a peaceful atmosphere that invites you to slow down and truly appreciate each masterpiece.

The collection itself is remarkable and covers centuries of art from the Renaissance to modern times. You can admire paintings by Leonardo da Vinci, Raphael, Vermeer, Monet, Van Gogh, and countless others. Every gallery is thoughtfully arranged, allowing you to move effortlessly through different artistic periods and styles. The sculpture garden outside is another highlight, offering a beautiful open space where art and nature come together. It is especially lovely on a sunny day when you can stroll among the sculptures and fountains.

What makes the National Gallery of Art so special is the sense of serenity and inspiration it offers. The museum feels welcoming to everyone, whether you are an art expert or simply someone who enjoys beauty and creativity. The staff are kind and knowledgeable, and admission is completely free. Visiting the National Gallery of Art is a truly enriching experience that reminds you how powerful and timeless human expression can be. It is a place that nourishes both the mind and the soul.

title: National Gallery of Art
text:
The National Gallery of Art in Washington, D.C. is an incredible museum and one of the best art experiences I’ve had. The collection is world-class, featuring masterpieces from a wide range of periods and styles — from classical paintings to modern art.
The galleries are beautifully curated and well organized, making it easy to explore at your own pace. The building itself is stunning, with impressive architecture and lovely spaces to take a break and reflect.
I especially enjoyed the variety of artists represented — there’s something for everyone, whether you’re a casual visitor or a serious art lover. The museum also offers free admission, which makes it an absolute must-visit for anyone in D.C.
The staff were friendly and helpful, and there are plenty of resources available for visitors who want to learn more about the art and exhibitions.
Overall, the National Gallery of Art is an unforgettable cultural destination that I highly recommend!

title: National Gallery of Art
text:
The National Gallery of Art in Washington, D.C. is one of the most exceptional museums in the United States. Its outstanding collection is displayed across three adjacent areas:

• West Building: A Neoclassical structure housing the museum’s permanent collection of European and American art, including Medieval and Renaissance, Baroque and Rococo, 18th- and 19th-century French painting, Impressionism and Post-Impressionism, Early American art, and feature exhibitions.

• East Building: A striking Modernist building featuring works by the Modern Masters, Abstract Expressionists, and Contemporary artists, along with rotating exhibitions.

• Sculpture Garden: An outdoor space showcasing sculptures by artists such as Alexander Calder, Robert Indiana, Roy Lichtenstein, Louise Bourgeois, Tony Smith, and Katharina Fritsch.

Tips:
• I lived in Washington, D.C. for years and could never visit the museum enough. Its collection and staff are exceptional. I wish I could rent a room and stay at the museum for at least a month just to take it all in.

• Admission is free, and reservations aren’t required to view the permanent collection. However, some special exhibitions may have different guidelines. Donations help support the museum’s mission.

• Visit the museum’s website to browse its online collection—you can discover works that interest you most. Each piece is listed with its location and exhibition status, helping you plan your visit.

• I like to begin in the West Building. If you break for lunch the museum has restaurants in the West Building or by the sculpture garden. You can view the sculptures in the garden, share lunch and then explore paintings in the East Building.

• The museum offers recorded tours and provides accessibility accommodations.

title: National Gallery of Art
text:
The National Gallery of Art is one of Washington, D.C.’s cultural treasures—home to a world-class collection of Western art that spans centuries, styles, and continents. Located on the National Mall, the museum consists of two main buildings: the West Building, which houses classical and Renaissance art, and the East Building, which features modern and contemporary works.

The West Building is known for its impressive collection of European masters—think da Vinci, Rembrandt, Vermeer, Monet, and Van Gogh—as well as American artists like John Singer Sargent and Winslow Homer. The space itself is grand and elegant, with skylit galleries and quiet corners for reflection.

The East Building, designed by architect I.M. Pei, offers a striking contrast with its bold geometry and focus on 20th- and 21st-century art. You'll find works by Rothko, Picasso, Calder, Henri Matisse, and Louise Bourgeois, along with rotating special exhibitions that often include video, installation, and conceptual art.

Between the buildings lies the Sculpture Garden, a beautifully landscaped outdoor space featuring large-scale works and a peaceful atmosphere—perfect for a break or stroll. The two buildings are also connected by an underground walkway which you can access from the basement floor.

Admission is free, and the museum is accessible, easy to navigate, and welcoming to both art lovers and casual visitors alike. Staff and volunteers are friendly, and guided tours and audio guides are available for those who want a deeper dive.

title: National Gallery of Art
text:
The National Gallery of Art truly captivated me! With an extensive collection, the museum offers so much to explore that I highly recommend dedicating at least two separate days to fully appreciate every corner. The best part is that admission is completely free, and there's no need for reservations, making it accessible to everyone.

Once inside, you’re greeted by stunning architecture that adds to the overall experience. The spacious galleries showcase an impressive array of artwork spanning various periods and styles, ensuring that art lovers of all tastes will find something that resonates with them. From classic masterpieces to contemporary works, the museum’s atmosphere is both inspiring and tranquil, allowing visitors to immerse themselves in the beauty surrounding them. Overall, the National Gallery of Art is a must-visit destination that offers an unforgettable journey through the world of art.

title: National Gallery of Art
text: The National Gallery of Art was one of the coolest museums in DC. It has many paintings from different eras and regions of history.

title: National Gallery of Art
text: The National Gallery of Art was such an amazing experience! The halls are full of so many inspiring pieces from all around the world. There are countless forms of art from different artists and eras. You could easily spend hours admiring the pieces in this huge museum. What an inspiring place, and it’s even better knowing this is a free attraction! Put this on your list of must-see locations in DC and block off a couple hours if you can to just explore and take it all in.

Truly remarkable- I saw Monet, Picasso, Warhol and more. An outstanding gallery and experience. You could easily spent a day in each wing.

Their cafe has incredible coffee, cookies and soups. I highly recommend their roasted red pepper and gouda soup!

title: National Gallery of Art
text: This is a fantastic art gallery. There are both paintings and sculptures throughout. If you enjoy classic art, then this is the place for you. This is a must see if you are in Washington, DC.

title: National Gallery of Art
text: This is a fantastic museum and the fact that it is free is one of the best things our government is doing!

title: National Gallery of Art
text: This is a more formal serious art gallery. My kids enjoyed the interactive art boards that they could “paint” on in different famous artists styles. So many beautiful pieces of art!

title: National Gallery of Art
text:
This is a national treasure. Truly amazing collection and wonderful that it is free. Take time to walk through and see what catches your eye. No way you can do it all in a day but carve out 2 hours and enjoy.

My personal favorites are the American landscapes, French impressionists, and Italian masters. Also, don’t miss the 4-painting series called The Voyage of Life.

title: National Gallery of Art
text: This is an extremely expansive art gallery, with multiple levels and two different buildings. You could easily spend hours here and not see it all. There is really something for everyone who likes art or history. There are amazing paintings and sculptures from every time period. I appreciate that there are benches to sit many different places. There are fountains with plants, a water fountain that goes behind a glass wall downstairs in the Cafe, and twinkle lights over a moving sidewalk. Restrooms are available on each floor. Food and drink is limited to the cafes. You can bring your own food and drink if it's closed in your bag. You do go through a metal detector upon entry and are subject to a bag search, but that was a speedy process for us. I especially enjoyed the impressionists art- Degas and Monet in particular. There is currently a photography and a furniture exhibit as well. The sculpture garden is worth walking through outside, and it is fun to go to the top tower level and see the Big blue rooster and city view. It was not crowded, even though we were there on a holiday.

title: National Gallery of Art
text: This is an incredible art museum in DC and since it's free, if there's only one exhibit you're interest in you can go check it out just for that! I went right before it temporarily closed to check out the Photography and Black Arts Movement exhibit and it was stunning! I also went to this right after visiting the African American History Museum and there were certainly a lot of connections between the two which was cool to see. Would highly checking out that exhibit while it is still here!

title: National Gallery of Art
text: This is my favorite place in Washington DC.  If you've ever seen great art you can understand the power it can have on you.  With priceless portraits to incredible landscapes. The paintings and sculptures  history and world famous artist themselves are all on display in A never ending rotation that only gets better at every turn. Tours begin every half hour under the main dome and entry is absolutely free NO tickets needed.  I highly recommend the once in A lifetime experience for everyone visiting.

title: National Gallery of Art
text:
This is my favorite spot whenever I have free time. Especially when I can make it on a week day. No wait. Not too crowdy. It is simply the best. Like all other museums, they close at 5.00 PM. So I cannot wait too long to start my trip.
The museum is spacious with tons of artwork. Both classic and modern times. It easily takes you all day. My favorite is the Monet room. I am no art major nor know too much about art. But I just feel instantly at ease looking at his art.
The West Wing is truly interesting too. Sone artworks could be controversial but definitely worth checking out.
I attach here several interesting pieces but if it is not Okay, please let me know, I’ll take them off.
In one visit, we even see an interactive screen for kids to experiment different brush strokes, and recreate renowned artworks with their personal twist. It was really cool.

title: National Gallery of Art
text:
This is truly an exceptional museum. We visited the exhibition on the development of Impressionism Gallery 74, and the layout was thoughtfully curated.  If you go there early as 10am, not much wait time at all. When we leave at 1:30pm, waiting time is 45mins to get into the gallery.

It was such a privilege to see so many remarkable works of art. We also came across a local artist creating a live reproduction of a painting on-site. Watching someone so deeply immersed in doing what they love was heartwarming—even from a distance, their joy and focus were contagious.

title: National Gallery of Art
text: This is truly one of the must-see museums in all of Washington. I visited this past 4th of July holiday, and I was blown away by all the fantastic exhibits that they have on display. This place has a lot of pieces from well-known artists. They also have several cafes and gift shops to check out during your visit. Overall, if you are an art lover, then I recommend checking this place out. It is absolutely spectacular and the price of admission is even better.....free.

title: National Museum of Korea
text:
The National Museum of Korea makes a stunning first impression. The building is spacious clean and architecturally beautiful with wide halls and calm lighting that invites you to slow down and explore. It feels grand and important at first glance the kind of place you expect to leave with a deeper understanding of Korea and its unique history. But the longer I stayed the more confused and disappointed I became.

I had hoped to immerse myself in Korean culture to learn about its past dynasties its people its art and the journey of the country through time. Instead I found myself walking through exhibitions that felt oddly disconnected from Korea itself. There were entire sections dedicated to ancient Rome Greek mythology random global artifacts and even Japanese samurai armor. In fact there was an entire area focused on the samurai but surprisingly the word katana was never mentioned not even once.

While these things are interesting in their own right they felt completely out of place in a museum that calls itself the National Museum of Korea. I kept waiting for the moment where the story of Korea would unfold but it never really came. I saw endless variations of Buddha statues old porcelain from different parts of the world and an overwhelming number of scrolls and texts that were never clearly placed into context.

What I did not see was a clear timeline of Korean history no in depth section about the country’s own wars leaders traditions or even how the modern nation was shaped. It honestly felt like the museum had collected pieces from all over the world just to fill space. I left knowing more about Greek gods Roman culture and samurai armor than the place I had spent my whole afternoon in. I did not even come across a single map of Korea or an exhibit that tried to explain how the country came to be what it is today.

The only thing that saved the experience from being a complete disappointment was the building itself. It is a lovely space to walk through take photos and enjoy the peaceful atmosphere. But as a national museum representing an entire country it falls short in the most important way.

If it were simply called the International Museum of Art and History it would make more sense. But as it stands it fails to live up to its name. A beautiful space with little connection to the soul of Korea.

Two out of five stars. One for the building and one for the effort but the rest left me wondering what could have been.

This review came from my head. I locked my phone so that I can enjoy and focus on everything inside and it was boring, even without phone. Just imagine.

Overall it was okay, I wouldn't recommend any one to go inside.

title: National Museum of Korea
text: Excellent national museum for the Korean culture and history. Many artifacts mostly from Asia and eastern Asia in particular. Many exhibition halls were closed when I visited for renovation or for new exhibition build out. It is quite a big museum, and please bear in mind that this is not an art museum per se, there are sculptures and a few scattered paintings from around Asia, but there is not a specific exhibition room for paintings here. It is free to enter however, and the grounds are very photo worthy. Additionally, they sometimes have live performances under the big overhang outside the entrance. Finally, the exhibits and rooms themselves are really cool, very dark and well lit to highlight each exhibit. title: National Museum of Korea
text:
Visiting the National Museum of Korea should have been a grand immersion in history and culture, yet I left feeling curiously unengaged. The scale was undeniable—vast halls, extensive collections, carefully arranged artifacts. But the experience, at least for me, lacked the spark of dialogue that I had found in other institutions.

The galleries were filled with treasures—ancient ceramics, Buddhist relics, and finely preserved artifacts that testified to Korea’s deep civilizational roots. Yet the sheer quantity created a distance. Instead of being drawn into storylines or moments of reflection, I often felt as though I was walking through a catalogue. The displays were more about preservation than provocation. For an amateur art enthusiast with an economist’s inclination for narrative and structure, the absence of interpretive framing made it harder to connect.

The setting was impressive, with modern architecture and vast open spaces, yet the pacing was static. Visitors drifted through in silence, more as observers than participants. Compared with Seoul’s MMCA—where Ron Mueck’s sculptures unsettled and provoked—the National Museum felt distant, almost bureaucratic. It was conservation without conversation.

Though not engaging, the visit was instructive in its own way. I realized that museums, like markets, depend not only on the quality of their assets but also on how those assets are framed and mediated. Without interpretation, even the most valuable collection risks becoming inert. The National Museum of Korea reminded me that engagement is not guaranteed by scale or prestige—it requires translation, narrative, and connection.

title: National Museum of Korea
text:
Discover the Wonders of the National Museum of Korea (국립중앙박물관)

Nestled in the heart of Seoul, the National Museum of Korea stands as a captivating testament to the rich tapestry of Korean history and culture. As the largest museum in the country, it showcases a diverse collection that spans over 5,000 years, making it an essential destination for history buffs, art lovers, and curious travelers alike.

Historical and Natural Features:
The museum is home to a remarkable array of artifacts ranging from ancient ceramics and paintings to traditional textiles and tools, allowing visitors to journey through Korea's past. Noteworthy exhibits include the majestic gold crown of Silla, intricately designed celadon pottery, and exquisite Buddhist art that reveals the spiritual depth of Korea's heritage. The museum's architectural design, blending contemporary aesthetics with traditional influences, further enhances the visitor experience, offering panoramic views of the surrounding Yongsan Family Park.

Engaging Activities:
At the National Museum of Korea, exploration goes beyond the exhibits. Visitors can participate in a variety of enriching activities, such as guided tours led by knowledgeable docents, hands-on workshops for families, and seasonal festivals that celebrate Korean cultural practices. The museum also frequently hosts temporary exhibitions featuring international artists, ensuring that there’s always something new to discover.

Excellent Facilities and Services:
The museum is equipped with cutting-edge facilities to enhance your visit, including a spacious auditorium for lectures and performances, a well-stocked library, and a gift shop that offers unique souvenirs. Additionally, on-site dining options provide a delicious taste of Korea. The museum’s staff is dedicated to ensuring a welcoming atmosphere, with information desks available to assist visitors and accessibility features catering to all.

Parking Options:
For those visiting by car, the National Museum provides convenient parking facilities. There are both paid and complimentary parking options available, ensuring hassle-free access to this cultural haven. Take the opportunity to immerse yourself in Korea’s artistic and historical legacy at your own pace.

Conclusion:
Plan your visit to the National Museum of Korea and experience a journey through the heart of Korean civilization. Whether you are a seasoned art enthusiast or a first-time explorer, the museum invites you to discover the stories that shaped a nation.

title: National Museum of Korea
text:
I traveled from NYC specifically to see the Impressionism exhibition at the National Museum of Korea in collaboration with the Metropolitan Museum of Art, and it absolutely lived up to the journey. Seeing works connected to the Met presented in Seoul felt incredibly special and thoughtfully curated.

What moved me just as much, though, were the Korean history galleries. Each exhibit is presented with such detail and reverence that I found myself stopping often to reflect. The exhibition on Yi Sun-sin was especially powerful. I appreciated how it explored not only his achievements as a respected admiral, but also his humanity. It felt balanced, reflective, and deeply meaningful.

I also loved spending time in the typography and print sections. As someone who appreciates visual storytelling and design, seeing the evolution of written form and print culture was inspiring.

The museum itself is clean, beautifully laid out, and very foreigner friendly. The overall energy feels calm, intentional, and elevated. And the museum shop is honestly one of the best I have ever experienced anywhere in the world. The curation, design objects, and gifts are exceptional.

If you are visiting Seoul, this museum is an absolute must. It is world class in every sense.

title: National Museum of Korea
text:
If you are planning a visit to Korea, this is a place you absolutely must experience to see what Korea can offer the world. Most of the displayed artefacts are translated into English, so anyone who speaks English can easily understand and enjoy the exhibitions. However, that is just the beginning.
The digitalised displays are truly impressive—modern, immersive, and perfectly aligned with current trends. What stood out even more was the excellent system for engaging young visitors. Children are guided in small groups with dedicated guides and offered educational tutorials lasting two to three hours, giving them a meaningful introduction to Korean history.
Practical facilities are also exceptionally well organised. Water fountains are located on every corner, and toilets are available on every level. Most importantly, everything in this national museum is completely free—something you rarely see anywhere else in the world.
The highlight of the visit was the digital laser show just before closing time. It was spectacular and unforgettable. You really must visit to fully understand what I mean. Only then can you truly see the difference when comparing it to places like the Uffizi Museum in Italy.
Koreans are also outstanding at crowd control. Despite welcoming thousands of visitors every day, there were no chaotic queues or overcrowding—just smooth, seamless movement throughout the museum. The entire experience was remarkably well managed and thoroughly enjoyable.

title: National Museum of Korea
text:
It was one of those scorching summer days in Seoul—31 degrees and climbing, the kind of heat that makes the pavement shimmer and your clothes stick to your skin the moment you step outside. I needed a break, not just from the weather, but from the noise and bustle of the city. So I decided to take refuge somewhere cool—not just in temperature, but in spirit.

That’s how I found myself walking through the grand entrance of the museum, where the air-conditioning is crisp and the atmosphere is hushed with reverence. There’s something so comforting about wandering through corridors lined with history, where time slows down and the stories of past civilizations quietly unfold.

This museum has always been one of my favorite escapes. It houses an incredible range of historical artifacts—from delicate ceramics and ancient scrolls to royal treasures and everyday objects that once belonged to people just like us, centuries ago. Each room feels like a portal to another world, and I never get tired of losing myself in its quiet magic.

Even better? Admission is completely free every day, except for the occasional special exhibition. It’s rare to find such a rich cultural experience that’s so accessible to everyone. On a day when the city feels relentless, this museum offers not just a break from the heat, but a space to breathe, reflect, and be reminded of the long and fascinating human journey that brought us here.

If you ever need a break—from the weather, the routine, or the chaos—this place is a perfect little oasis.

title: National Museum of Korea
text: The Museum is an essential destination to explore the Korean history, art, and culture. it houses over 310,000 artifacts, with around 15,000 on display at any time, making it one of the largest museums in the world. The museum’s design is both symbolic and functional, with the left side representing Korea’s rich past and the right side symbolizing the future. Built with advanced features like earthquake resistant construction, shock absorbent display cases, and natural lighting, it ensures the preservation of its invaluable treasures. Exhibits are spread across three floors, featuring Korea’s journey from the Paleolithic era through the Joseon period, breathtaking calligraphy and traditional Korean paintings, and remarkable Goryeo celadon ceramics. The Asian Art Gallery highlights connections between regional arts through the Silk Road. Outdoor spaces, including gardens, waterfalls, and historical relics like pagodas, offer a serene and reflective environment. With its incredible collection, thoughtful curation, and peaceful surroundings, the National Museum of Korea is an enriching experience that is recommended to explore.

title: National Museum of Korea

The use of tech/digital media and lighting enhances the narrative and makes the experience engaging without feeling overwhelming. title: National Museum of Korea
text: The museum is well worth seeing. It has two floors. In the center is a bright, light-filled, covered atrium. Starting on the ground floor, there are several exhibition rooms on each side of every floor, each dedicated to prehistoric history. There are always one or two special exhibitions, for which an extra ticket costs 5000 pesos. Otherwise, the museum is free. The exhibition rooms are particularly pleasing and tasteful, each beautifully themed. Both the colors and lighting are perfectly suited to the objects. Truly very tasteful. There have been exhibits on topics such as India, Islam, Greece, Buddha statues, metalwork, porcelain, Japan, and so on. All the exhibition rooms are large and spacious. You should plan to spend at least 3-4 hours here. There is a subway station directly in front of the museum. There are restrooms on each floor, always at the beginning and end of the building. There are also designated taxi stands, for example, for Uber drivers. There is ample seating in the corridors leading to the exhibition rooms.

title: National Museum of Korea

Unfortunately, there are many inconsiderate visitors who neglect to read the rules and end up causing discomfort to others. Let us refrain from becoming one of them.

title: National Museum of Korea
text:
The National Museum of Korea is the primary institution showcasing Korean history and art in South Korea. Established in 1945, the museum has consistently dedicated itself to extensive studies and research in archaeology, history, and art while concurrently curating diverse exhibitions and educational initiatives.

The museum proudly showcases a diverse range of exhibits across six different galleries, including Prehistory and Ancient History, Medieval and Early Modern History, Donated works, Calligraphy and Painting, World Art, and Sculpture and Crafts Galleries.

The national museum is open to the public free of charge and features a vast collection of beautiful art, national treasures, and artifacts dedicated to Korean history and culture. During our visit, there was a special exhibition showcasing the exquisite Lacquerware of East Asia, which required an entrance fee. Nonetheless, the exhibition was certainly worth a visit.

title: National Museum of Korea
text:
The National Museum of Korea stands as a cultural gem, showcasing the rich history and artistic heritage of the nation. From ancient artifacts to contemporary masterpieces, the exhibits offer a comprehensive journey through Korea's past and present. The museum's architecture itself is a testament to modern design, seamlessly blending with the surroundings.

Visitors are treated to a meticulously curated collection that spans diverse eras and genres. The informative displays, coupled with engaging multimedia presentations, enhance the educational experience. The museum's commitment to preserving and presenting Korea's cultural identity is evident in every exhibit, making it a must-visit destination for history enthusiasts and art aficionados alike. The National Museum of Korea is a captivating exploration of the country's vibrant heritage, leaving a lasting impression on those who seek a deeper understanding of Korea's storied past.

title: National Museum of Korea
text:
The National Museum of Korea, located in Yongsan, is an impressive place where you can experience Korea’s traditional culture with your own eyes. The museum is massive, and aside from two special exhibition halls, most of the museum is free to enter.

Because of its large size, it can easily take around five hours if you want to explore it thoroughly. The gift shop is huge and filled with a wide variety of beautiful souvenirs.

There’s also a food court inside the museum. The portions are generally small, but the prices are very affordable. The menu includes Korean favorites like tteokbokki, ramyeon, bibim-guksu, pork cutlet, and hot dogs.

It’s truly a wonderful museum—I highly recommend visiting at least once!

title: National Museum of Korea

Also, don't miss the immersive digital gallery on the first floor. Good facilities here, including its own food court, lockers, and a large museum shop. Outside from the terrace at the far end, you have a good view looking north of the N Seoul Tower.

title: National Museum of Korea
text:
Visiting the National Museum of Korea was an incredible experience that completely exceeded my expectations. I’m someone who loves exploring history, and this museum truly immerses you in Korea’s rich cultural heritage. From the moment I walked in, I was struck by the sheer scale of the place—it’s massive, and the collection is unlike anything I’ve seen before.

One of the highlights for me was the Buddhist art section. The bronze Buddha statues were breathtaking in their size and detail, and the pagodas were beautifully intricate. I also loved exploring the ceramics and calligraphy exhibits—each piece told a unique story about Korea’s artistic and historical evolution.

The museum itself is so well-designed. The modern architecture, combined with the natural light pouring in from large windows, makes it easy to spend hours wandering through the galleries without feeling overwhelmed. The view of Namsan Mountain from the museum was a nice touch, adding to the overall experience.

If you’re in Korea, this is a must-visit spot. I spent several hours there, and I could’ve stayed longer. It’s a place where you can really dive deep into the history and culture, and it left me with a whole new appreciation for the country’s heritage.

title: National Museum of Korea
text: Wonderful museum showcasing Korean History and art including a children's museum next door. You will need lots of time here if you are absorbing and reading about the exhibits, enjoying the interactive programs and not just passing by. We spent  about 3 hours just on the first floor starting with ancient history through the end of the Joseon Dynasty and Korean Empire -- briefly touching on the annexation by Japan shortly thereafter. It's fascinating to learn about the different kingdoms, rulers and political intrigues over time as well as to see the crafts and tools from each period. It's free, so don't try to do it all in one go.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
We visited the Musée d’Orsay in Paris with my husband and overall had a beautiful and memorable experience. Before arriving, we planned our visit carefully since we knew we wouldn’t have time to see everything before closing. The most important areas for us were Level 0 and Level 5, especially the Impressionist and Post-Impressionist collections (Van Gogh, Neo-Impressionism, etc.), and this plan allowed us to truly enjoy the artworks without rushing.

We started on Level 0, taking our time with the temporary exhibitions on display at the time, then stopped at the café before continuing our visit on Level 5. The paintings and the atmosphere there were absolutely worth it and are the highlight of the museum.

Unfortunately, the end of our visit was disappointing. Around 4:45 pm, we lined up to take a photo at the famous clock on Level 5. The line was very long, as many visitors were taking a lot of time with photos. At 5:00 pm, an announcement was made that the museum would close in 30 minutes. When it was finally our turn, a staff member intervened in a very rude manner and did not allow us to take our photo, cutting us off abruptly. This was especially frustrating, as we had waited patiently and that photo was important to us after investing so much time, effort, and money to visit the museum.

Our advice for future visitors:
•	Take your photo at the clock on Level 5 early, as the line gets very long and people take their time.
•	Be well organized with your visit, as staff management near closing time could be improved.
•	It would be great if photo access at the clock were handled in a more organized and respectful way, especially for visitors who have waited in line.

Despite this, the museum itself and its collections are outstanding and truly worth visiting.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Among the most visited museums in the world and located in what used to be a beautiful Beaux-Arts–style railway station, the Musée d’Orsay has an extensive collection ranging from some of the finest Impressionist works to sculptures by Rude, Rodin, and Bartholdi. The curation is a little different from the typical American museum I’m used to, which at least for me made it a little more difficult to navigate with ease, but it is definitely worth a visit.

text: Beautiful museum with many works by some of the most famous painters in history including Monet, Renoir, Degas, and Cezanne. Incredible sculptures as well. title: Musée d'Orsay
text:

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: I wanted to give this 5 stars but I couldn’t just due to the system to enter. We waited out in the rain even though we had pre booked tickets and were drenched by the time we got in. title: Musée d'Orsay
text: It’s obvious that the museum’s collection is magnificent and absolutely worth seeing. The experience might be even better if visited off-season, when it’s less crowded. Personally, I found the number of visitors quite exhausting, and I wasn’t able to see everything I had planned. But on the bright side, now I know for sure that I’ll come back here again during a quieter, less touristy season.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Made use of free first Sunday to visit both permanent and temp exhibitions. It was gorgeous. The art was incredible, and the temporary exhibit on Sargent was a huge highlight. There were some cons, but the art made up for them for the most part. First con was that it was incredibly crowded, particularly in the impressionism sections. I understand that's what the museum is known for, but I also know some museums have done a great job in figuring out how to manage visitors so that you're not in danger of tripping over wild toddlers and into a masterpiece when you're just trying to push to see a painting. They even had some kind of management of the Sargent exhibit, staggering entry so that it didn't get so full that it felt like you had to wild west your way through.

For the most part, it was easy to navigate, though there were one or two occasions when I'd accidentally meander into a hallway and stumble on a painting I didn't expect but was very happy to see (Whistlers Mother, for example). I felt like because these paintings maybe weren't in dedicated rooms or well labelled, they'd be easy to miss if you don't search every nook and cranny.

My only other real observation is that I can tell the museum attempts to be well-planned out and accessible, but in particular the ground floor rooms with Millet etc only have stairs to get to the next section, and because of the dimness I caught myself almost tripping a few times. Not ideal. Too, certain lighting made the glare on the paint take away from the painting as a whole (I'll upload a few in particular, and no I wasn't using flash).

Besides all of that, the art was incredible. Don't miss this museum if you're in Paris.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Once inside it is very impresive. If you love impressionist art you will love it. Apart from that the architecture is amazing. The issue with this place is access, we had prebooked tickets and had to wait for 90 min in the line to get in. You should book tickets with a nominated time, but the issue there is you will still need to be there at least 30min early to ensure you get in. The other issue is there os limited information or assistance to work out what to do when you get there. You would think after centuries of visitors it would be a little easier. Still worth it.

title: Musée d'Orsay

Here you can admire iconic works by Monet, Manet, Degas, Renoir, Cézanne, Van Gogh, Gauguin, Seurat, Toulouse-Lautrec and many others. Highlights include Monet’s Water Lilies, Van Gogh’s Starry Night Over the Rhône, and Degas’ Little Dancer.

A must-see for art lovers elegant, atmospheric, and filled with masterpieces that shaped modern art.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
A must-see museum on your visit to Paris!
