title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
A must-visit if you want to dive into the fascinating world of Impressionism. This museum, housed in a former railway station built for the 1900 World’s Fair, offers an extraordinary collection of Impressionist and Post-Impressionist masterpieces. Works by Monet, Manet, Van Gogh, Seurat, and Degas are among its highlights, along with remarkable sculptures by Rodin.
Beyond the artworks, the architecture itself is impressive. The museum preserves the grandeur of the original train station, and its large clock and expansive windows provide beautiful views of Paris throughout the visit, adding to the overall experience.
As expected, some areas can become very crowded—especially the walls displaying one of Van Gogh’s self-portraits or Monet’s *Water Lilies*—so patience is required. I strongly recommend booking tickets online and choosing the first time slot of the day to enjoy the collection with a bit more space and calm.
There are also good dining options on site, including a café for light meals and drinks, making it easy to take a break during your visit. An affordable audio guide is available and well worth it, as it provides helpful context and insights that enhance your understanding of the artworks and the history of the museum.

An unforgettable experience for art lovers and anyone interested in the evolution of modern art.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: A place that has so many Gauguin's works will always have my heart. However, it's always crowded, which makes the experience more frustrating than enjoyable. Book your tickets in advance, otherwise you'll wait in a very long line.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: A stunning museum inside a former train station, filled with masterpieces by Monet, Van Gogh, Degas and more. Beautifully curated and truly inspiring – a highlight of Paris!

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
A visit to Musée d’Orsay is truly impressive. The museum beautifully combines history with an industrial architectural setting, creating a unique and memorable atmosphere. The way the museum is laid out makes it very comfortable to explore, with a clear flow that allows visitors to enjoy the artworks without feeling overwhelmed.

The sculptures displayed in the central hall are particularly striking and create a powerful first impression. The exhibitions featuring works by Claude Monet, Vincent van Gogh, and other renowned artists are extensive and truly impressive, offering an exceptional overview of nineteenth century art.

The view of the city from the top floor is also excellent and adds a special final touch to the visit, making the museum experience feel complete and well balanced.

However, it is an amazing collection and well worth the admission price.

title: Musée d'Orsay

title: Musée d'Orsay

amazing.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
As someone who isn’t a museum fanatic, this one knocked my socks. We went on a Thursday night as it was the only time slot left and it didn’t disappoint. It was still fairly busy at 730 but we were still able to enjoy the beautiful art displays and sculptures on various floors. We especially loved the Monet exhibition!

Highly recommend booking a ticket as soon as possible! It’s definitely worth visiting (at bare minimum you would spend 1.5-2hrs). They also offer free lockboxes for your coats & items which is a great added bonus without having to lug everything around for hours.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Beautiful 6 floor museum inside the old rail station.  If you're an Impressionist fan, it's a must-see!  Get a combo ticket at the D'Orsay or a Museum Pass to avoid the long lines.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Beautiful architecture & art. Impressionist floor (at the top) is definitely the most crowded, but the rest of the museum has much more space & art to explore. Definitely more manageable than going through the Louvre in a few hours/single day. Museum formatting (and navigating to elevator/lifts) was confusing, but all staff were very kind and helpful the whole way! Shoutout to Noel at the lost & found/accessibility counter who was especially gracious to us :) and another shoutout to the staff who helped me find a lost item!

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Beautiful museum with a good permanent collection. We had booked tickets online so we got in relatively quickly.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Beautiful museum with an incredible collection of Impressionist and post-Impressionist art. The building itself, a former railway station, is stunning and adds to the experience. Not as overwhelming as the Louvre, which makes it easier to enjoy at a slower pace. Definitely worth visiting.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Beautiful museum with friendly staff. The woman at check-in welcomed our two children in particular and asked about their stay in Paris.
They have neat little free lockers to safely stash your things in, which was a very good touch so we didn't have to lug our backpacks and coats through the museum.
This place is busy though. We went on a Tuesday in January. No holidays, not a weekend, and if it were any busier it would have been uncomfortable. I can see why though, the collection of Van Gogh's and Monet's is absolutely splendid. Seeing a Van Gogh in person is a whole different experience than a picture or a video. They almost come to life as you stare at them.
What is also nice about this museum is it is not overwhelmingly huge. You can see it all and take some time to contemplate pieces without having felt like you missed anything.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Beautiful museum, well worth the ticket price, so much to see, don’t miss a floor, or get lost. Sculptures & paintings are all unique, large size loved it.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Beautifully exhibited works of arts in the former train station. Such a beautiful building. The café upstairs was not to our tastes but then we realised there was also one downstairs that looked like it had what we where looking for.

Lots of interesting pieces and in a stunning building.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Best museum in Paris - it is a must see for anyone visiting the capital! It has been curated in such a thoughtful and beautiful way, with an exceptional experience on the 5th floor of Impressionism. The staff were lovely, and were very accommodating of our needs (we had a member of our party with mobility issues, and we were treated incredibly well).
Definitely worth the money and exceptionally great value, would recommend!!

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Big museum with nice art. Building used to be a train station and they converted it into a museum There are 3 floors and stairs are on both ends. There is a restaurant with an outside terrace to take nice pictures from. I'd recommend coming here if you're in the area.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Brilliant art museum with a lot of works by the famous impressionist artists. Worth getting an audio guide for the extra information on some of the pieces on display.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Came here with recommendations from a local guide since the Louvre was so busy!

It’s an amazing museum with art work from the likes of Monet and Vincent Van Gogh with pieces from Leon Cugnot, Victor Navlet, and Edgar Dégras .

My personal favourites from this was Pompon, the Polar Bear from the live art statue sections. As well as the ceiling from the original train station, the Vincent Van Gogh exhibit was quite moving as well - if you know anything about his previous experience, depression and mental health struggles alongside his work you can imagine seeing the all the appreciation in the exhibit was quite moving.

I would recommend if you’re an English or Spanish speaking tourist as translations are almost available at every piece as well as an audio guide. From a tourist standpoint it was less busy than the Louvre with half the price but double the experience with five floors. You only really miss out on a selected few pieces from the Louvre like the Mona Lisa. The experience and calm environment was a lot better as a lot of the artwork can be moving.

A girl called Stéphanie who was a helper was lovely and took some photos of us together as well! Merci beaucoup Stéphanie!!!

Also if you want a really weird fact to keep in mind whilst visiting - Dégras enjoyed women peeing apparently, you find this out right at the beginning of his exhibit so was hard to enjoy after that 🤣🤣🤣🤣

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: D’Orsay contains most of my favorite art. Some real big hitters in the art world and art history. They have the Renoirs, the Degas, the Loutrecs, the Monet’s, the Manets, Van Goghs, Bonnards, Gauguins, and some of their most important pieces too. I will continue coming back to pay homage to the impressionists i love and their circles of lesser known friends.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Definitely a must see place. Amazing display of impressionist art, featuring big names like Van Gogh, Gogain, Cezanne to name a few.. really impressive. There is free self-service locker where you can deposit your belongings as well as quite charming restaurant which surprisingly was not that expensive.
Better to book tickets couple of days in advance although they are more expensive when booked online (+2eur each). Don't worry if you see them sold out, though, you can still buy tickets on site

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Definitely worth the visit, it’s not as crowded as Louvre. Make sure you head to the fifth floor to start your journey as that floor has most of the popular paintings.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Do not miss this architectural and artistic gem! Do a bit of research first so you target the areas of greatest interest. Impressionists and Art Nuveau were a must for me. Meals are available in various places and they are good. Only downside is the layout of the building, it can be confusing. But the staff were helpful and we found our way at the end. (Uploaded photos are from two different trips to the museum)

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Doesn't need a review. But... it is niche art museum. Don't remember such kind in any other place. Paris. What can I say.
We were on patrimony days (hope I called it right). When we entered, paying full fee, girls we met told us, it should have been free. At about 15:00 they said the entrance was "temporarily closed", I believe it was the moment when the "free" was over. For others to know, just in case.
I am adorer of meticulous art. The paintings on the first level were just a feast for the eyes...

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Edited with photos.
There are many museums with amazing collections. This one is special. If you like impressionists, this is your place to be. I've visited more than six times and will always come back. Takes a while to see it. It is always crowded, so better to buy the ticket upfront.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Every time I find myself in Paris (and that happens quite often), I can’t resist returning to the Musée d’Orsay. No matter how many times I go, it never feels like enough — the world of Impressionism and Neo-Impressionism keeps drawing me back with its light, color, and emotion. Each visit feels like rediscovering beauty all over again.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Everybody knows how fantastic these places. The museum itself is just simply fantastic.  Well organized and clean easy to get around. Advice: get Timed Tickets otherwise  lines are really long. Audio guide well worth a little extra to learn about the paintings. Many different spots to take photos.

The card can get very big around famous artists and pictures.

5 start!! Bring comfortable shoes :-)

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Everytime its a wonderful experience, even when I had to wait in line for an hour in the rain.
It was not my first time here, if you think you saw everything you are wrong, its a huge museum, you can find something new everywhere. The collection of painting is so beautiful, but it has even more than that.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Fabulous building, excellent exhibition of John Singer Sargent's works in Paris. Absolutely wonderful morning spent there but very busy. We bought tickets for opening time and got there early to get into the special Sargent event and it was still quite crowded by the time we left.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Fantastic place with a lot of beautiful art. Legendary paints and amazing exhibitions. Must be in Paris

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
For me the best museum in the line up in Paris, try to reserve some weeks before your trip as the tickets run out quickly.
You can also book through booking but it is a bit more expensive.
Check out the free admision as many people could fit into its criteria!

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Free museum days are the first sunday id everything month and they require prior reservations, with no ability to secure a ticket the day of.

We made reservations and had a great visit. We spent about 2 hours here walking around. Highly recommend.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Go, if you love impressionists and sculptures.  Rodin's thinker is there, as well as other lesser known pieces. Many masterpieces of Monet, Manet, van Gaugh, Toulouse Lautrec and many more. Absolutely loved it.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Gorgeous renovated train station with an unparalleled collection of artwork after 1850. Van Gogh, Renoir, Monet, Pizarro, Sisley and others. Also a whole floor of Rodin sculptures. Not to be missed! And eat at the lovely cafe in the clock tower. Magnifique!

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Great museum to learn about art history and see a great variety of artwork and paintings! I would recommend checking this out.

Only downside is how busy it was which made it difficult to navigate. For popular artwork (like the van Gogh exhibit), we had to wait to see some paintings while groups of people took their photos and cycled through.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
he Musée d’Orsay is one of Paris’s cultural crown jewels, housed in a former Beaux-Arts railway station overlooking the Seine. Its setting alone is remarkable: the grand glass roof, iron framework, and monumental clock windows create a luminous atmosphere that enhances the artworks rather than overshadowing them.

The museum is world-renowned for its extensive Impressionist and Post-Impressionist holdings, featuring masterpieces by Monet, Renoir, Degas, Cézanne, Van Gogh, and Gauguin. Beyond these, visitors discover sculpture, photography, Art Nouveau decorative arts, and lesser-known treasures from 1848 to 1914. It fills the chronological gap between the Louvre’s classical art and the Centre Pompidou’s modern works.

The layout is clear and spacious, allowing a natural progression from large central galleries to intimate side rooms. The natural light from the station’s architecture plays beautifully on the paintings. However, it can get crowded, particularly in the Impressionist rooms, so early visits or late afternoon entries are ideal.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Highly recommend this museum, especially for Monet and Van Gogh. Multiple places to have a bite (cafes and full restaurant). Pay attention to do the day you book, when the Louvre is closed this place is busy.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Highly recommend!
We bought the tickets in advance.
At the entrance, we decided to get an audio guide — and didn’t regret it.
Every sculpture, every exhibit is truly impressive in its presence in this world.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: I absolutely loved my visit to the Musée d’Orsay, especially the 5th floor, which felt like a dream for an art lover. Seeing masterpieces by Monet, Édouard Manet, Paul Cézanne, Camille Pissarro, Edgar Degas, Auguste Renoir, and Vincent van Gogh in one place was breathtaking. The museum’s elegant architecture, transformed from a grand train station, adds even more charm. I honestly don’t understand why so many people line up just to take pictures in front of the giant clock when there are such extraordinary works of art to enjoy. One downside: the audio guides are quite expensive at €6 each.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
I absolutely loved the Musée d’Orsay and, honestly, it was my favorite museum in Paris. Everything is very well organized, which makes the visit smooth and enjoyable even when it’s busy. The way the museum is laid out makes it easy to walk around and actually enjoy the artworks without feeling overwhelmed.

I really recommend going in with the Paris Museum Pass, you don’t need to book a time slot, and the line to enter was super short when I went, which already puts you in a good mood. Another huge plus is that the museum has plenty of lockers for coats, bags, and even suitcases, all included in the ticket price. This is perfect if you’re arriving in Paris or heading straight to the airport. Overall, it’s an amazing museum and an absolute must if you’re visiting the city.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: I absolutely loved the Musée d’Orsay! The collection is incredible, especially the Impressionist works, which were my favorites. The museum itself is stunning, housed in a beautifully restored old train station with amazing architecture. We bought a timed-entry ticket and didn’t have to wait at all. Highly recommend!

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
I absolutely loved the Musée d’Orsay. Weeks later, I'm still taking in the amazing experience: the art, the history, the quaint atmosphere...the windows of the grand clock.  Even though I spent 3 to 4 hours there, it never felt overwhelming like some other big museums.
I'll definitely return when in Paris.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: I came to Musée d’Orsay and it felt like stepping into a dream. The paintings, the sculptures, the old train-station architecture everything is breathtaking. One of the most beautiful museums I’ve ever seen.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
I chose Thursday night to visit the Musée d’Orsay because the opening hours extend to 9:45 p.m. I visited from 6:00 to 8:30 p.m. with my Paris Museum Pass, and no reservation was required.

Before my visit, I was worried I couldn’t bring my bag, as I came directly from shopping. However, you are allowed to carry your bag with you, or you can pay for a locker. Both options work.

The paintings are famous, and the museum is easier to walk through than the vast Louvre. This allows you to enjoy each piece more slowly, which was exactly what I had hoped.

As expected, it’s a fantastic museum and I highly recommend it—a must-see in Paris!

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
I didn’t get the ticket in advance but it was very easy to get in. There was only a few people in front of me at purchase point and no wait to get in.

Quite crowded inside but if you navigate yourself well you can find moments where people passed by and the spots become quiet.

Insane collections. Love love love their pieces. Highly recommend coming here for the Paris visit.

I would plan at least 2 hours for being in here so you dont rush around. It was wonderful!

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: I had an amazing experience going there with a friend who had a member’s card. We went in 30min before the official opening, and we were alone inside this beautiful museum. I don’t know how much is this card but it definitely a more magical experience early in the morning.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: I had previously visited the Louvre in Paris, but during this trip I ran straight to the Orsan Museum and truly felt as if I had lived in a different dimension for three hours. The building, located in a magnificent spot along the Seine River, was originally an old train station. The museum’s architecture truly captivates you. The interior creates a powerful sense of spaciousness, and its windows offer an incredible view of Paris. The museum’s collection focuses especially on the years between 1800 and 1900. It presents one of the strongest collections in the world. Seeing masterpieces by giants such as Monet, Renoir, Manet, Degas, Pissarro, Cézanne, Van Gogh, and Rodin side by side feels like rediscovering art. The galleries are very intelligently designed, and at every step you encounter a new surprise. It is not only paintings; there are also sculptures, decorative art pieces, and a photography collection. There is both a café and a restaurant inside the museum, making it possible to take a break during your visit. I truly recommend it.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
I like this museum better than the Louvre. People flock here all year round and it’s a popular tourist destination. The Impressionism exhibits are well displayed with many famous works of arts from Van Gogh, Monet, Cezanne, Sisley etc.
The French exhibits are also vast as are the many sculptures.

Once you pass through security to enter you must leave all bags and belongings in the free lockers using a 4 digit code. There are toilets in the lower ground floor where is the theatre is. Here you will also find some small fa seating areas.

All floors are accessible by lifts. There are also escalators and stairs.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: I liked it more than Louvre. For a reasonable price, without crowds you can see the biggest number of masterpieces for 1 square metre.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
I love Musee d’Orsay, always one of my preferred!
Its architecture is wonderful as an old train station refurbished into a Museum,if you are into the impressionist art this is the place Monet, Cezane, Van Gogh, Renoir… etc

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: I loved going around this museum - especially seeing the Van Gogh paintings! Lots to see and explore. My boyfriend booked for my birthday so we managed to skip the queue. Recommend pre-booking

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: i loved the monet and van gogh collection. there was a large beautiful ballroom i’d recommend getting dressed up and taking cute pics!! busy but definitely recommend, we bought tickets online a few days before and got to skip the entry line.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
I loved this museum. Initially we went to see the despairing man from Colbert ( which is borrowed from the Qatar museum ). The painting in itself did not disapoint but neither did the staff, the exposition nor the arrangement of the museum.
The most popular part of the museum is the last floor with its Monets and Van Goghs but everything else is also beautiful.
During our visit, someone forgot their luggage and an evacuation was called as well as the cops. We had tl wait for ~40 minutes outside, but it did not affect our experience much.
I wholeheartedly recommend visiting, and also recommend doing the musée de l'orangerie which is not far.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: I really liked the Musée d’Orsay. The building is so beautiful and it feels really calm inside if you go in the morning. Walking around and looking at all the art was really nice. It’s very spacious so you can take your time, sit down and enjoy everything. It’s definitely one of my favorite places in Paris.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: I was positively overwhelmed taking it all in. I was surprised how Rodan & Camille Claudells sculptur moved me.  They are so amazing & alive despite being marble & bronze. Those likenesses are preserved for eternity long after their flesh have disappeared . The impressionists painters really speak to me & are encouraging me to paint with oil with every image I consume these impressions. Standing face to face with those paintings is unforgettable . As for Sargent’s  collection truely a crafts man whos painting are as vibrant today as when his brush left the canvas. The fabrics ,textures, the play of light & dark and the eyes of every subject reflect such individuality in each & every sitter. The staff here are so accommodating and friendly .I sketched in line while waiting to get in and the time flew by.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
I was surprised by the organizational measures. Either I was very lucky, or everything was really organized excellently. Reasonable lines were created at the ticket offices. Information about the further course. No need to worry about where to go, the employees tell and show everything, of course, if asked.
When going in the evening, you have to take into account that there will not be enough time for everything and you need to develop a sightseeing route with the places you want to see the most.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: I went here not long after it had opened. This visit was so much better. The galleries have expanded a d we spent over 4 hours here... we still left somethings to see next time we visit. This is a great place to go

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
I went on the Thursday evening one, arrived 10 mins early and waited about 10mins to get in once door opens.  You can buy online in advance using cheque vacances connect.
Quite an experience when walking along Seine at night after the visit, seeing the largest impressionism collections in the world, and all the beautiful paintings and sculptures, what a rich country!

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
I went to the museum in January on a Thursday around 11:30am. There was a steady flow of people, but it was not overwhelmingly busy.
The museum is beautiful and there’s so much to see! There’s art by Monet, Van Gogh, Renoir and magnificent sculptures displayed throughout the entire museum. The ‘La Petite Danseuse de Quatorze Ans’ sculpture by Edgar Degas was such a delight to see in person! The iconic clock is a masterpiece in and of itself. The view of the museum from the 5th floor’s observation deck was a real treat. And located at the back of the museum, you’ll find a glass floor with beautiful replicas like the Palais Garnier opera house underneath.
I spent about 4 hours at the museum, and I enjoyed every minute!

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: I will say that this museum, housed in a beautiful building that was once a train station, was my favorite stop of the four short days that we had in Paris. If you like Impressionism, this is the place for you. The works on the fifth floor were nearly an emotional experience, as it tells the story of how Impressionism began and evolved. The museum is well organized and easy to navigate. We were also thrilled to see Whisler’s Mother in the first floor as well as some early paintings of the great impressionists. To me this museum is much easier to wrap my head around than the louvre. If you come during the busy season, purchase your tickets in advanced for timed entry. I purchased a hardcover book recapping some of the works, for a very good value of 20 euro.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
I'm using Paris Museum Pass, just join the line via Entrance C.

This is one of the coolest art museums in Paris, it’s a must-visit if you’re Van Gogh's fan or simply into art. What makes it really special is that the building itself used to be a huge train station, it was the Gare d’Orsay, built around the early 1900s. Instead of tearing it down, they turned it into a museum, which means you get this amazing mix of old industrial architecture and art all in one place.

Inside, the museum is packed with incredible artworks, mostly from the late 19th and early 20th centuries. You’ll find tons of famous Impressionist and Post-Impressionist paintings here, artists like Monet, Degas, Renoir, Van Gogh, and Cézanne. If you love those bright, colorful paintings or want to get a feel for the art that changed the world, this is the spot.

Besides paintings, the museum also has sculptures, decorative arts, and photography. It’s pretty manageable in size, so you don’t get overwhelmed like in some bigger museums.

The museum sits right on the left bank of the Seine, close to the Louvre and the charming Saint-Germain area. So, it’s easy to fit it into a day of exploring Paris.

It's recommended to visit here. :)

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: If art is your thing, this is a terrific museum to visit. Highly recommend booking tickets in advance and arrive early for your allocated time. The queues do move quickly, but each person goes through metal detectors. The space is easy to move through and there is a cool insta opp looking out the large clock window.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
If you visit Paris and skip d’Orsay, then you haven’t visited Paris.

Get tickets in advance (we used Paris Museum Pass) and use the coat check lockers when you arrive. Audio guide highly recommended, and give yourself at least 2-3 hours here. You’ll still only see a fraction of the art on display in one visit.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: If you want to see famous works from Monet, Manet, Degas, Gauguin, Van Gogh, and others then you must go. It's also a beautiful building with multiple restaurants and cafes. Get advanced tickets because it gets crowded.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: If you’re a fan of Monet, Sisley, Van Gogh or Degas, this is a MUST visit. The overall experience was infinitely better than the Louvre — easier to navigate, less crowds, more helpful staff, you name it. Our tix came from a third party site which allowed us to skip the queue too, so that was a nice surprise!

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: In my opinion this is the better of the two main museums in the area. So many amazing paintings from all over Europe and a very interesting building, which was originally a mainline railway station. It was busy but not overcrowded and I will definitely go back when I next visit Paris.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: It was a great experience! The only thing you need prepared to spend a lot of time there, 3.5 hours was not enough for us. Otherwise all is good. And would great to have some coffee stop. Cause both restaurants were closed.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
It was my second time here because obviously one time is not enough. Go with at least 4-5 hours in hand if you want cover every single thing. Also you must visit this place if you are into Impressionism or Rodin. The collection is very vast and great.

And if you are 25 or under it’s free for you - but only downside is you cannot reserve it online, you will have to visit and show your ID proof in person to get a free entry.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: It was surreal seeing Van Gogh and Monet’s magic. And the rest is also fascinating. My recommendation is to go to the roof where Van gogh and Monet’s paintings are then going down. We couldn’t book online tickets so we thought it would be better if we go there early in the morning. There was a queue. However, when we were leaving the museum around 12pm, there was no queue for those who did not buy the ticket online. People were just entering like it was a mall. Little disappointed but couldn’t risk having more queue later in the day.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: It’s a great museum with a wide permanent collection, especially Impressionism. Really nice collection of classic Van Gogh, Renoir, Cezanne, Degas and Pissaro. The audio tour is probably enough. It’s well laid out and not as crowded as the Louvre. Probably had a better experience here than Louvre because of the crowds.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: It’s all about the impressionists!  The only museum in Paris that houses the largest collection of impressionists in the region. The selection is vast and organized beautifully. They even have an area with the few female impressionists. Along with that collection, we have amazing sculptures, featuring Rodin, Renoir and others. My only disappointment was the lack of Seurat!  One of my favs. Also, two dining areas in a beautiful old train station with plenty of historical significance. I enjoyed a small group tour with Viator using an amazing young art expert that shared her knowledge of the art, artists and significance of the pieces. What a great learning experience. Enjoy your might see visit.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
It's been several years since I've been to this museum, but it's easily one of my favourite places in Paris, and one I've longed to visit again in the future (whenever that may be).

The museum had a booklet detailing the history of museum in many different languages for customer convenience, and also a picture of each painting and artwork in the museum and where you coud find it so that you could plan your way around accordingly.

I hope that when I next visit, I will be as pleased as I was the first time!

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
It’s lesser visited than Louvre, but the exhibits here are valuable pieces of art.
They have a kids activities room as well.
Getting online tickets will let you beat the queue.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Just 12 shots to give an idea of what the museum offers.  Although, I believe most of these are John Singer Sargent, a temporary exhibit-- one of our favorite artists.  There's a lot more to see, though! 😊👌

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Just a simply amazing place you must See it!! This museum is a must-see while in Paris. Historical pictures from the 17th century and beyond are the backbone to Art and our history around the world. Monet, Manet, Renoir, VanGogh... Just breathtaking.  This building was an old train station that France decided to keep and convert holding some of the most historical and well-known artworks of the world

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Just saw this museum for a couple of hours during my first visit and I was overwhelmed by it's beauty.
Even the temporary exhibit of John singer sargent was breath taking.  If some one would tell me they were pictures from camera I would have believed them.
This museum is unbelievable.  Worthy of multiple visits .

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Just worth it all to see the architecture, the sculptures inside and important painter's paintings especially the room for Van Gogh, Monet. Loved the English information audios and spent time very efficient. I bought ticket for full day so I entered very easily with no queue at all.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Like many visitors before me, I found myself doing the very tourist thing and walking into the Musée d’Orsay, half-smiling at my own predictability. Yet once inside, that irony quickly faded. The collection is genuinely extraordinary — from Impressionist masterpieces to delicate sculptures and beautifully curated spaces. The former railway station itself adds a quiet grandeur that enhances the experience rather than overwhelming it. Even after having seen art in many cities, this museum still managed to create moments of calm, reflection, and unexpected emotional warmth. A place that reminds you why museums still matter, even when you arrive as “just another tourist.”

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Lines are insane! But buying tickets in advance is a good thing. Absolutely stunning art, worth visiting.

Some pictures are unreachable to take a look at because of the crowds and endless photoshoots, like I don’t get why would people go to museums nowadays. Or one has to be really pushy to get to Starry Night, so may the luck be with you😅

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Lots of different collections of art master pieces in Musee d'Orsay! The building is well restored and decorated, it was an amazing experience to witness these artists' breathtaking masterpieces! A little crowded in all the famous pieces but others were easy enough to get close view and relaxing to walk through. Definitely worth to visit when in Paris. Booked tickets in advance is advisable.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Lots to see besides their Impressionist painters, when we went there was also an exhibition of John singer Sargent, paintings and drawings from his many travels in his younger years before he became well known for portraits. Muder D’Orsay entrance fee covered the extra exhibitions. Book ahead so that you can get in when you are in Paris.
Restaurants nice settings but expensive for small portions and think my small bottle of water was 5.70 Euro.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Loved this museum.  My kids had a great time here as well. The section 5 that has the famous paintings was busy but the rest of the museum was manageable.  We went late August so the summer rush had passed. We had the paris pass so included this in our stay. We however did make reservations for the time slot. Glad we did it on a different day and before the Louvre as it was good segway for the kids.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Maybe my favorite museum in Paris. Less talked about than the Louvre but the Monets, Van Goghs as well as many other impressionist paintings are awesome. Also don’t miss all the sculptures. We didn’t have tickets so we showed up before open on a Saturday and got in 10 minutes after the doors opened.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Maybe overseen by the cultural importance of Louvre's centuries old collection, d'Orsay shines for its amazing collection of mostly 19th and 20th century French artworks. It holds one of the, if not, the biggest collection of impressionist and post-impressionist art in the world. The uniqueness of the setting adds a whole lot of magic to the artistic experience in itself, the old Gare d'Orsay train station is wonder by itself.

Amazing experience ;)

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Musée d’Orsay
There’s not much to say: beautiful, fascinating, and very well maintained. An outstanding collection set in a truly unique and iconic building, worth the visit to Paris on its own.
The only downside, unfortunately, is the staff: I found them quite rude, which feels out of place in such a magnificent museum.
That said, it remains absolutely unmissable. Highly recommended.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Musee d’Orsay is one of my favorite art museums in the world. The variety of art works was wonderful. As an artist, I hope to have one of my paintings to be displayed in a museum one day. I get inspired by viewing the works of the masters, and this museum really inspires me by visiting in person. The displayed art and the building itself is amazing. We really enjoyed our visit.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Musee d'Orsay was a highlight of my recent trip to Paris. We had 1:30pm entrance tickets and went right to the cafe for lunch which was very nice. We did not do the self-guided audio tour but walked around at will. The museum was crowded but you could get around to see and take photos easily. There are so many works by well known artists here. I'm no art major but was impressed with the collection. We took a lot of pictures of the giant clock faces which are so iconic. I would rank this above the Louvre.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Must see in Paris. We had better experience than even with Louvre. The first thing you notice is the location, which is inside the old train station. The interior is beautiful and the collection of art is exceptional. A lot of celebrated paintings from 19th- early 20th century are brilliantly composed to submerge the visitor into the era of Impressionism. Without pre orders tickets we waited around 1h in queue on Saturday.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: My favorite museum in paris. Absolutely incredible collection of sculptures and impressionist artworks. Generally, the museum and art is so thoughtfully imbedded into the old train station building that it makes the space have a special feel to it. However queues are crazy, so make sure to book your tickets online (said by a person who stood there for 40 minutes).

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
My favorite museum!!!!!
Love all the paintings! Highly recommended to see the masterpieces of Van Gogh and Claude Monet 😍😍😍😍😍
I visited every time I traveled to Paris, they also had a temporary exhibition about Van Gogh paintings ✨✨✨✨
Free entrance for students (no need to book in advance for this type), need to book in advance to avoid the long queue

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: My favourite museum in Paris. It gets quite busy so I recommend going during the week and early in the morning to avoid crowds. Also book tickets online to avoid queues

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
my favourite place. i spend 6 hours in this museum and i would do it again.
there is lots of famous paintings and sculptures, but also smaller ones but wonderful btw. it can be tiring because its a 5 floor building full of things to visit, but if you love art you cant miss this.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Nice collection of art. Very affordable/fair prices compared to many other museums and galleries. The collection wasn't as large as I imagined, especially being such a large building. But it's worth a visit. There are some great photo opportunities from above, and the impressionists art is fantastic.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Nicely spaced exhibits. Open and airy atmosphere. Plenty of amenities with a cafe/restaurant inside. Price was reasonable. Lots of famous paintings and sculptures to see. Well worth a visit.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Not so expensive (14€ directly at the enterance) but you have to wait in queue outside for half an hour at least.

Very vast interior of an ex-train station so there's a lot to see. If you want to see most of it with a common sense than be prepared to have at least 3 hours of free time to see everything without rushing.

Besides popular items from Monet, Manet, Van Gogh you can also see the original Statue of Liberty and the famous painting "Whistler's mother" which made more famous from the movie "'Bean" 😁

title: Musée d'Orsay

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
soul of French art!
The Musée d’Orsay is an absolute must-see in Paris. Housed in a former railway station, the architecture alone is stunning — and then you step inside and are surrounded by the world’s greatest Impressionist and Post-Impressionist masterpieces. Monet, Degas, Renoir, Van Gogh… every gallery feels like turning another page in art history.

The layout is easy to navigate, and the natural light in the upper floors adds so much beauty to the experience. I also appreciated the friendly staff and well-organized entry, even during busy hours.

If you love art, culture, or simply want to feel inspired, the Musée d’Orsay should be at the top of your list in Paris. Truly unforgettable!

title: Musée d'Orsay

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
Spectacular riverside museum spread over several floors of a historic train station. There is a picturesque vaulted central corridor through the station, dotted by sculpture and overlooked by a large clock at one end. The museum has several transparent clocks offering unique views across Paris. The standing collection is one of the greatest in the world, focusing on painting and sculpture. The museum can command some of the best touring exhibits as well. When a large work in the collection needs to be restored, the work is often done where visitors can watch.
Long lines outside the museum are a problem, especially for Paris museum pass visitors who are barred from booking an admission time slot. Unfortunately the museum seems to hope museum pass holders will instead visit other smaller museums. This is especially surprising as patrons in the museum are frequently either sitting and starring at their phones or forming crushing hordes of picture takers around a handful of key art works.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Spent my day there yesterday and it was sooo lovely, they have a café/bistro area for lunch or afternoon tea where I got myself a vegetarian wrap and a muffin for a total of 14.50€. Also a bonus, if you're a student between th ages of 18 and 26 you can get a free entry, with proof of ID of course. The layout is very nice and not overwhelming. Highly recommend if you're looking for something cultural other than the Louvre.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: Stepping inside the Musée d’Orsay feels like walking through time — surrounded by the genius of Monet, Van Gogh, Degas, and so many others who painted emotions in colors words could never express. Every corner holds a story, every painting whispers a piece of history. It’s not just a museum, it’s a journey through light, beauty, and imagination.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
The best museum I have ever visited!
The building itself is as much of a masterpiece as the collection it houses. Renovated from a former train station, the architecture inside fits beautifully with the sculptures, creating a truly unique and harmonious atmosphere.
I visited on a Thursday evening at 6:00 PM with an e-ticket and only had to queue for about 10 minutes. I headed straight for the 5th floor, where I happily lost track of time for over an hour, admiring the spectacular Impressionist collection. Seeing works by Monet, Degas, Renoir, Van Gogh, and Camille Pissarro up close was breathtaking. The ground-floor sculptures are equally impressive.
I would highly recommend that anyone visiting Paris put this absolute gem on their itinerary!

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
The Louvre is a manic mess and this is the opposite. Sure, the entry is poorly planned, but that just prepares you for the museum layout which will you lead you to back track multiple times. Perhaps, that's just me preferring a more linear, IKEA, art experience.

Beside that there's adequate space for people besides the welcome known Van Gogh paintings. There's the usual tour groups speed-running the place, but this was minimal late on Thursday afternoon.

Speaking of visiting times, tour groups tend to go early, but if it works for you, go late on Thursday when it's open late. While you can get slightly cheaper tickets starting from 6pm, that means those entries are full, so avoid them and go at 5:30pm. That will get you ahead of the human wave that starts afterwards.

While there's a restaurant on the top floor, venture outside to the balcony for some great photos of the Louvre. If your budget allows, buy an expensive champagne or beer in the roped off section.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: The Musée d'Orsay is a museum in Paris that specializes in 19th-century art and houses a large collection of impressionist and post-impressionist works. The building was originally a renovated Orsay station, which was built for the Paris World's Fair in 1900, and its distinctive large clock is one of its remaining highlights.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
The Musée d’Orsay is a truly breathtaking institution, housing an extensive and exceptionally well-curated collection of art. During my visit, I was particularly drawn to the paintings. From the very moment one enters, the grandeur of the main hall is striking; the building’s origins as a converted railway station lend it an atmosphere that is both majestic and distinctive.

The museum showcases remarkable works by a wide range of artists, and it is utterly mesmerising to observe the paintings at such close proximity. Being able to study each brushstroke, the varied textures, and the true scale of the artworks is a rare privilege. Some pieces are far smaller than one anticipates, while others are surprisingly vast—and it is precisely this contrast that makes the experience so extraordinary.

One of the greatest pleasures lies in discovering new paintings and witnessing their quiet majesty. Works such as *Woman with a Parasol* by Monet exemplify this perfectly, revealing a presence and elegance that is truly striking when seen in person. The museum is replete with exceptional paintings and offers an outstanding representation of Impressionism, with an entire floor dedicated to the movement.

I would strongly recommend setting aside at least half a day—no less than five to six hours—if one wishes to wander unhurriedly, truly appreciate the collection, and take in the museum in its entirety. I tend to absorb and process visual information slowly when standing before a painting, noticing details gradually and thoughtfully, and for such an approach, generous time is essential.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: The Musée d'Orsay is a wonderful art museum within an old railway station built in 1900. While it is best known for its large collection of of impressionist and post-impressionist paintings on display on the 5th floor, it also has some magnificent neoclassical, romantic and realist paintings found on the 1st floor. The floors in between had some interesting art-deco era artifacts. The building itself is well laid out for its current purpose and it only really gets crowded on the 5th floor. The line to get in wasn’t too long and there are some nice food options inside. I recommend planning on staying around 3 hours to fully appreciate everything Musée d'Orsay has on display.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
The Musée d'Orsay is located in the heart of Paris, on the left bank of the Seine, opposite the Tuileries Garden, and occupies the former Orsay train station, which was built for the 1900 World's Fair. The architect Victor Laloux designed it as a symbol of technological progress and modernity, with a grandiose iron and glass vault that allowed for abundant light and air. However, after World War II, the station became outdated, and trains could no longer use its platforms, leading to the gradual decline of the building. In the 1970s, it was planned to be demolished, but thanks to the efforts of the public and the French government, it was decided to turn it into a museum dedicated to the art of the period 1848-1914, which includes paintings, sculptures, photographs, decorative arts, and architectural projects. The museum opened in December 1986 and has since become one of the most significant cultural centers in France. Today, the museum's collection includes masterpieces of Impressionism and Post-Impressionism, including works by Monet, Renoir, Van Gogh, Degas, Gauguin, Courbet, Seurat, and many others. The building itself is a work of art, combining the industrial power and elegance of late 19th-century architecture. The museum's location is deeply intertwined with the history of Paris, as it stands on the site of a former swamp and an old monastery. The soil is composed of alluvial deposits from the Seine, making it soft and requiring reinforcement, especially for massive structures. The geological features of the area played a role.
As always, the museum is wonderful: the paintings, exhibitions, and sculptures are all very unusual, interesting, and exciting. The only downside is the long lines in the restrooms, especially in the women's restrooms. The administration should pay attention to this and actually help the women.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
The Musée d’Orsay is one of my favorite museums in Paris.

Housed in a beautifully converted former railway station, the building itself is already worth the visit. Unlike the Louvre, which spans multiple eras and civilizations, the Orsay focuses primarily on Impressionism and Post-Impressionism, making the experience feel much more cohesive and immersive.

The exhibition layout is thoughtfully designed along a chronological timeline, allowing visitors to clearly see the evolution of artistic movements and styles. I especially appreciated how intentional and well-curated the flow of the museum felt.

I would absolutely visit again.

The only downside is the waiting time — the line for on-site tickets was extremely long (around an hour). I highly recommend purchasing tickets online in advance to avoid wasting time in line.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
The Musée d’Orsay is one of the most beautiful museums in Paris, especially if you’re a fan of Impressionist and Post-Impressionist art. I visited it on the first Sunday of the month, when entry is free — but be aware, these free slots go fast! I recommend booking your ticket at least a month in advance if you want to visit on a free Sunday. Otherwise, you can easily buy regular tickets on their official website.
The museum itself is stunning — located in a former railway station, it has such a unique charm. You’ll find masterpieces from artists like Van Gogh (yes, “Starry Night Over the Rhône” is there!), Monet (including his Water Lilies), Manet, Renoir, Degas, and Cézanne, as well as works from naturalist and symbolist painters. The collection is incredibly rich and beautifully curated.
One of my favorite parts is the giant clock on the top floor — not only is it a gorgeous photo spot, but you also get a breathtaking view of Montmartre and the Sacré-Cœur through it.
Whether you’re into art or just want to explore one of the most iconic museums in Paris, Musée d’Orsay is definitely worth the visit.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: The Musée d’Orsay is one of those places that quietly steals your breath. Housed in a former train station, it feels like time itself paused between iron and glass. The light spills gently over the Impressionists Monet, Degas, Renoir and every brushstroke seems alive. You wander through rooms that smell faintly of history and oil paint, until Van Gogh’s colors pull you in completely. It’s less like visiting a museum and more like stepping inside the heartbeat of art itself

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
The Musée d'Orsay was easily my favorite museum in Paris. Housed within a stunning former Beaux-Arts railway station, the building itself is a masterpiece, perfectly setting the stage for the art it contains. The central clock and open architecture create an atmosphere that is both grand and comfortable.

What truly elevated the experience for me was the art, particularly the Impressionism collection. Being surrounded by masterpieces from Monet, Degas, Renoir, and Van Gogh was breathtaking. I loved the Impressionism art the best; seeing the vibrant colors and revolutionary brushwork up close truly made the artists' works come alive.

For anyone who loves 19th and early 20th-century art, this museum is an absolute must-visit and a genuine highlight of the Parisian cultural scene.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
The Musée d’Orsay, opened as a museum in 1986, is a brilliant example of how a former railway station was transformed into a cultural masterpiece. The architecture alone is stunning — elegant, spacious, and very French in style.
Inside, the museum offers a complete and fascinating journey through the history of Impressionism, with works by some of the world’s most outstanding artists. The collection is beautifully curated and inspiring.
There is also a stylish café inside, perfect for enjoying a coffee, tea, or even a light meal while soaking in the atmosphere.
Overall, a truly memorable experience that leaves a very warm impression.👍 Highly recommended

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: This former railway station was reborn as a cathedral of light and art; its great nave, once for trains of the Compagnie du chemin de fer d’Orléans, now a sculpture aisle beneath the iron-and-glass vault. Galleries flanking the old platforms reveal Gauguin, Van Gogh, Redon, and Degas, displayed in soft light against muted walls. Van Gogh’s Starry Night Over the Rhône glows beside masterpieces of Symbolism and Post-Impressionism, while Degas’ Little Dancer still startles in her fragile pose. Monumental clocks frame views over Paris, making time itself part of the collection, a portal between the city’s age of steam and its age of art.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
V & I were lucky to be in Paris for three days on a stop over on our way home from our anniversary in Italy. I had not been to this museum in 25 years and it is everything I remembered and more.

If you like the French Impressionistic period this is your place. All the masters including Picasso, Van Gogh, Monet, Renoir, Cezanne, Manet, Seurat and the list goes on and on. And these are not the “also ran” pieces, the number of true masterpieces here is incredible in such a (relatively) small location. It’s not as grand or immense as the Louvre and not the wow factor of Monet’s largest works like in the l’Orangerie, but there are SO MANY masterpieces and with the right guide (Highly Recommended!), you will learn how the period evolved and who was involved and why they were involved and how they painted and where they painted and how it all came to be! There are few things in life as beautiful as art and to see these paintings IRL, it’s breathtaking.

Don’t miss this beautiful place and its history and evolution because it more than just impressionist art. The sculptures are amazing - maybe not as mind blowing as Rome or Florence - but each as meaningful and amazing as the pieces you see elsewhere, and some you can’t see anywhere else.

Don’t forget to invest in a guide. There are some great ones you can find in these reviews along with recommendations in certain very well known discussion forums that I have read (it) about.

Last advice, avoid Tuesdays. The Louvre and l’Orangerie are closed in Tuesdays so this museum is poppin’ on Tuesdays. It is closed itself on Mondays like most every museum thus Wednesday and Thursday are likely the best time to visit.

Enjoy!

title: Musée d'Orsay
text: We had a very pleasant visit to the Musée d'Orsay and enjoyed the experience overall. The museum is housed in a beautifully converted train station. title: Musée d'Orsay
text: We spent a wonderful day at the Musee d'Orsay. Very busy so I would recommend going early, nice restaurant, limited bathrooms. Amazing artwork. I would recommend getting tickets in advance.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
We visited the Musée d’Orsay in May and had an incredible experience! The museum is beautifully organized and filled with masterpieces — especially the Van Gogh and Impressionist collections, which were the highlights of our visit.

We bought our tickets online in advance from the official website for €16 per adult, and it was definitely worth it to skip the long lines at the entrance. The museum also accepts the Paris Museum Pass, which is convenient if you plan to visit other museums.

Inside, the atmosphere is calm and inspiring. Photography without flash is allowed, and the lighting makes the paintings even more impressive. The Café Campana on the top floor has delicious food , but costly — prices are a bit high, but the quality and location make it worthwhile.

The building itself, a former train station, is stunning — the grand clock and glass ceiling add to the experience. Staff were friendly, and everything was well signposted in French and English.

Highly recommended for anyone visiting Paris — a perfect place to spend a few hours surrounded by art, history, and beauty. 🎨🇫🇷

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:

We loved the sculptures and all of the impressionists.  The great hall in the middle was impressive!  The building itself is a piece of art.  Then you have Monet, Manet, Seurat, Van Gogh... So many!  We had some time on a Thursday night to do something and fortunately they have late hours that night with a reduced ticket price.  It was worth it!

If you like art of that time period and want a different experience than the Louvre, I highly recommend it.

title: Musée d'Orsay
text:
