title: Musée d'Orsay
text: You might enjoy this museum more than the Louvre, especially if you are into impressionism. I found this museum much more accessible and enjoyable. The clock is of course iconic and it’s past as a train station is very well preserved.  The crowds are manageable compared to the Louvre and the dining options looked wonderful even if we did not protect during our last visit.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
We visited the Museum today, and despite the „must-see-hype“ we were greatly disappointed. A lot replica, barely anything’s in English and it was sometimes so dark, you had to use the phones flashlight to read the signs. Furthermore, no guidance or map was handed to us and there is literally no organised way to visit the museum without a tourguide. We did not see any information about tours or/and guides as well. Website was down for the last two days as well.

Visit only if you are a hardcore historian AND speak Spanish, otherwise every simple stroll around beautiful CDMX will provide you a better experience.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Great collection of Mexica history but weirdly not tourist friendly. Some displays have English translations but most do not. If you can not read Spanish you are out of luck. You are just staring at an object with no story, no context.

Also, no audio headphones that can explain what you are seeing in your language.  Something common in many museums around the world.  Something they could make money selling. But nope...speak/read Spanish or you're out of luck.

Shameful for the elite museum in a world class city.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: I enjoyed the museum but 210 pesos for a museum and has about 50 exhibits and can be viewed in less than 90 minutes. seems a bit steep.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: I have a question for everyone: Is a 500 Mexican peso banknote acceptable? Can it be used? If so, why did a female staff member at the museum ticket office throw my 500 pesos on the ground? (November 18th, morning) Her behavior was extremely rude and disrespectful! She has absolutely no manners. This is completely out of place in this beautiful museum!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
I see why this museum is a must visit for tourists, but I was underwhelmed by how inauthentic some of the displayed pieces were and the lack of explanation of the collections.

I literally left with way more questions than answers. Definitely work with a private professional tour guide if you truly want to learn.

Lots of walking and far away bathrooms/water access so make sure to wear comfy shoes.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: What good is a museum if it takes an hour of waiting in line to even enter? Why can’t Mexico’s govt do anything efficiently? Why is it so difficult to buy tickets online? Why does the wifi barely work on the lobby?

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Fantastic museum with an endless array of spectacular and flawlessly preserved artifacts. Quite a few tomb displays with complete skeletons, and also outdoor areas where you can get some fresh air in between exhibits / buildings.

Fair entry cost at around $5 USD.

⚠️🚨⚠️🚨⚠️

Dropping 1 star due to the sacrifice pit, I mean egregiously poor design layout in the front of the museum. Inebriated adults and small children take note - if you don’t watch your step, you could easily fall into the pit of doom and land in the water basin below where you will likely meet an early demise! I’m not being dramatic either, see pic 1 below.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Great museum, covering evolution of humankind and Meso-American cultures. Of highest interest are the Teotihuacan and Aztec rooms, but many other areas of Mexico are covered with many artifacts. Don’t miss the beautiful gardens outside each room which display a few artifacts in a more natural setting.
There are many items to see, some of which have placards. I think it best to go with a guide for greater context. Also enjoy the modernistic central courtyard.
We spent two hours, but only saw a third of the museum. Highly interesting!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Huge museum with a lot of interesting history in it. The layout isn't the most user-friendly to navigate so, take a look at the itinerary maps before entering the room.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Huge place and well laid out with each area covering a different part of Mexico's history. The displays and exhibitions are very well done with my only negative point being that a lot of the information boards are in spanish only. Strongly recommend getting queue jumpers on line as the queue for tickets was enormous (cheaper for locals on a Sunday apparently).

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: I do enjoy history but i feel this place was over hyped and was a bit underwhelming for me. The Teotihuacan gallery is the best part. The place is massive. Plan to spend at least 3 hours here.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: I’m not a museum person, but the ticket to this one is only like $5 USD per person. It was definitely cool to see so many artifacts, but it kind of felt like the same thing over and over in every room. We honestly buzzed through it in under two hours but were happy that we quickly checked it out.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
It was very informative! 400 pesos for 4 people... not bad! No backpacks. You have to check them in free to your left. They have a lot of thought provoking pieces and they also thought about putting sit-down places wich is nice because the museum is huge!!! You will need a day especially if you read and watch the videos.

The restaurant is incredible. They told us 15-20 min wait and I was please to only wait 5 min for a table for 4. The food was excellent and the desert was just awesome. I recomend the chicharon ribye and the narangada with mineral water. And for desert I recomend the crembrule with sweet potato. So bomb! Sorry didnt get a lot of pictures cause I scarred it down 🥲 #nojudgement

Now continuing the museum... idk who curated it but the flow of the museum needs work. You constantly run into walls and have to backtrack exit and go into the next gallery.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Love the fascinating history of Mexico.   Very well displayed in Sectional themed gallery rooms.  I wish more of the artifacts had English descriptions.   It would also be nice to have a personal or audio guided tours available in other languages.    I would highly recommend spending 3-5 hours here.   Also had free check bags and a nice gift shop.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: National museum exhibiting the history and culture of Mexico. Informative and interesting, displaying the profound depth of backgrounds and the richness of Mexico. Much to see, learn and appreciate. $100 pesos, about $5 dollars  to gain priceless insights.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
NEW PRICE 2026-
210 pesos for adults (non-Mexican)
I was annoyed that the new cost (which is more than double what it was before) is not currently posted online. So if you are going to visit now in 2026 just know it has increased!!

The museum itself is wonderful. Amazing collections, and very well organized. Give yourself enough time to explore it. I liked that it is indoor and outdoor, and there is just so much to see. A lot of the information is translated to English as well. Beautiful place!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Please note: This is the most visited museum in Mexico City. Meaning, you have to wait to get in, wait to get a ticket (I waited 30 minutes), and then wait again to have your ticket scanned to be able to enter the exhibitions. Just like with other museums of this size, there is also large crowds and people of all ages.

That said, the art and history inside is well worth it. You learn a lot and get to see some of the oldest pieces know. It’s huge and there is a beautiful garden as well.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: The museum is really great and with very rich exhibitions on all the Mexican, Aztec and Maya cultures and their development. Perhaps the only drawback is that it's almost too much: too much information, too many exciting things. After a few hours, we felt slightly overwhelmed and couldn't take in any more - even though there were still the upper floors to explore... Still, definitely worth an extended visit!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: The museum provides an excellent overview of Mexican history and society. Some additional bilingual exhibit signage would be appreciated for those not fluent in Spanish.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This a nice museum. Recommend if you’re in the area. This place will get busy very quickly, so get your tickets early.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
This is definitely a magnificent museum of the history of the peoples of this land, however, the facilities in January 2026 are not perfect. Many of the toilets have no lock and no seat, the cafe had a 45 minute wait for a table and the food was “adequate”.
However the biggest issue today was that the rooms themselves on the ground floor all had their doors open to the outside. It was 10c outside. Despite keeping our coats and 2 long sleeved tops on as well as a scarf we were absolutely freezing with running noses for the entire time we spent looking around. This definitely shortened my visit and significantly reduced my enjoyment.
It is sad that so much money is being taken in places like this and chichen itza but it does not seem to be filtering through to the facilities

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Very big.  Nice decor.  Lots of stuff to visit but a bit of a maze and no maps to give out.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
A fabulous, very large museum, that went to a lot of trouble to put visitors in the shoes of the ancient people. Highly recommended. Allow about three hours to see a fair amount of it. Great to see a lot of info in English; given the number of English-speaking visitors here every year.

The only odd thing was the lack of interesting features and seating in the huge internal courtyard. Given the money and time spent on the halls and exhibits inside, surely they could have created some gardens and put more (comfortable) seating in place.

See my photo.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: A huge art collection.  What a beautiful place. Totally recommended. if you're an art and history lover, this te right place is for you.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: A huge museum that is recommended. We should have a tour guide next time, there is a lot to learn. Great scenery!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: A HUGE museum with so much history and art that it can be stunning. It took us 3 hours and we cruised through everything but without going deep. A good place for history and art lovers. Wouldn’t bring kids - they’ll get bored and ruin your experience and everyone’s else.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: A little overwhelming in its size and scope. I think the experience would be helpful with a guide, however exploring independently also allows you to linger wherever you fancy. Vast collection of artifacts, which could take two visits or more if you want time to appreciate everything. All of that being said, it's one of the best museums I've ever visited - so don't skip it!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: A must see in Mexico City. The indigenous cultures of Mexico are represented in minute detail, the most splendid being the Aztecs (Tenochtitlan, los Mexicas) but plenty of attention given to Maya, Toltec, Zapotec Teotihuacán, and many other cultures. Thousands of artifacts are on display with ample explanation. Not all explanations are translated to English, but many are. There are also exhibits on contemporary indigenous cultures including a fascinating one on how spinning cotton and weaving into beautiful textiles has played an important role in indigenous culture and religion throughout Mexican history across its many indigenous peoples. Tickets were 100 pesos. We spent 20 minutes in line for tickets, but not a big deal considering all the great stuff we fot to see once we went in. Don't plan on spending less than two hours here. We spent three and a half and wanted more but they were about to close. This was probably the fourth time I've visited this museum and it won't be my last.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
A MUST SEE VISIT!

well located, this museum is attached toa park and has covered a good part of Mexican history.

The rooms are divides work civilization, displaying multiple artifacts, stones, thumbs , among others.

They are all well preserved and you can also gore local guides to guide you around.

I strongly recommend going to Teotihuacan to complete understanding their culture.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: A must-go anthropology museum if you arrive at CDMX. It offers a wide range of permanent exhibitions related to Mexican history, plus temporary special exhibitions. (When I was there, there were two: one about Côte d'Ivoire and another about an ancient Chinese dynasty.) Expect a bit of a long line to buy tickets, as this museum is always popular with both locals and travelers.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: A must-see in Mexico City. The museum is incredibly rich in pre-Colombian history. I missed some more information under certain artefacts, such as maps and explanatory notes, which would have helped with the enormous amount of information during our visit.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
A must-visit cultural landmark in the heart of Mexico City

Located within the stunning Bosque de Chapultepec, the Museo Nacional de Antropología is one of the most impressive museums I’ve visited in Latin America. The building itself is massive and beautifully designed, housing an extraordinary collection that spans Mexico’s rich pre-Hispanic history.

I visited on a Sunday, and I strongly recommend arriving early if you plan to go that day—especially since admission is free for nationals, making it one of the busiest days. I arrived shortly after opening and only waited about 10 minutes in line. However, by the time I left, the line had grown significantly, likely with quite a bit of waiting time.

Inside, the museum is thoughtfully organized, with each hall offering a deep dive into a different culture or historical period. If you want to read the displays, absorb the information, and truly enjoy the full experience, be prepared to spend several hours here. Rushing through would not do justice to the magnitude and quality of the exhibits.

Although I didn’t eat there, the museum also has a restaurant on-site, which looked modern and inviting—definitely a convenient option if you plan to stay for a few hours.

Whether you're a history buff, a casual visitor, or someone looking to learn more about Mexico’s ancient civilizations, this museum is an essential stop.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: A must-visit place! I learned so much about Mexican history and culture in just a few hours. This museum is definitely underrated. If you’re curious about the formation of civilizations and the cultural diversity of Mexico, you’ll truly enjoy a visit.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: A VERY cool museum. Full of artifacts and exhibits - a great prequel if you plan on visiting the pyramids. Has a distinctly “Indiana Jones” feel to it. Tickets are cheap and entry doesn’t take long. A pass to record video costs a little extra. Expect to store your backpacks and bags in a secure area with a claim ticket. The only long line was waiting to get my backpack at closing time (15 min) but if you stay until they close you’ll get to see a ceremony lowering the colors they fly outside.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: A wonderful anthropology collection and a great place to learn about the Mayas and Mexicas before Spain conquered Mexico.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Absolutely expansive, you would need days to see everything properly. Sad that the first section was closed but there was still plenty open! No metal water bottles allowed, you will have to rent a locker.

Information was beautifully presented and many plaques had English translations.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Absolutely wonderful experience touring this museum recently. So much history. Such beautiful culture. So many artifacts in such a small space. Hands down one of the best museums I’ve ever toured. Highly recommend a visit!!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Absolutely worth a visit when you're in CDMX. The history from this region is so deep, and there's a large amount of archaeological artifacts in this museum from all different periods. It takes a while to make it through and you may even visit more than once. Bags have to be checked, and I'd recommend taking a bottle of water with you because it wasn't easy to find any within

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Amazing and extremely large museum!
You need way more than 2 hours. :) 4 hours just to check what they have...
There are also free guided tours in Spanish at certain hours, make sure to ask at the entrance.
There's a souvenir shop inside but quite expensive though

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Amazing collection of things arranged by region and tribe. You could spend the full day and still not see everything.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Amazing cultural center for Mexican history. My family and I were in awe the artifacts and the vastness of them! Recommend everyone to visit this museum!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Amazing display, you will need several hours to see the entire museum. Museum offers audio guides in several languages. There is no need to have a tour guide unless you want the full understanding of the artifacts. There is a sit down restaurant on the premises offering local and favorite dishes.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Amazing displays, some large structures outside.  We spent 3.5 hours here with a break for a delicious lunch on site.   You could spend all day.   Outside the entrance there are traditional performers and of course vendors selling, snack, drinks, souvenirs.  Beautiful park around it, too, with many other sites to visit.  This place is huge and seems to accommodate any number of people.   It was free for Mexicans that day and it never felt crowded.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Amazing museum and gift store. Definitely worth a visit when in CDMX yo understand it’s history

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Amazing museum if you looking for an experience learning all about Mexico's vast archeological history!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Amazing museum showing the history of Mexico's first nations. Arranged by culture and region. It's huge and we only saw the first floor. It's also wonderfully curated with many explanations in English though you'll need a translation app occasionally.

Ive been to Mexico a dozen times but this was my first visit to Mexico City so it was great to put many of the ruins sites ive seen into context.

One of the world's great museums and a "must" when visiting.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: amazing museum with some great architecture as well - needs multiple visits but worth every time

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Amazing museum. Need to go early in the am. To really truly enjoy it. The restaurant has great food, but pricy!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Amazing place but you have to go earlynin the morning to give yourself enough time.  There is just so much to see.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Amazing, really incredible. Cafe was waaaay too busy. It’s very small for such a massive place. Recommend eating before you go or bringing snacks. It’s enormous and you will be there a long time. You have to queue for a bit to get in even if you have tickets, but it moves fast. Staff were friendly. I used my google translate image feature to read the text. Worked well.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
An absolute must-visit in Mexico City — the Museo Nacional de Antropología is a world-class museum that leaves a lasting impression.

The architecture alone is stunning, but it’s what’s inside that truly captivates: an incredible collection of artifacts from Mexico’s rich pre-Hispanic civilizations, including the Aztec, Maya, Olmec, and more. The iconic Aztec Sun Stone is a highlight, but every hall offers something fascinating — from giant stone sculptures to delicate jewelry and detailed models of ancient cities.

The exhibits are thoughtfully curated, informative, and visually powerful. Whether you're a history buff or just curious, you’ll find yourself drawn into the stories and cultures that shaped Mexico. Give yourself plenty of time — you could easily spend several hours here and still feel like there’s more to see.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
An absolutely incredible museum and one of the most impressive cultural places I have ever visited. Here you truly dive into the ancient civilizations that lived on the territory of Mexico long before the Spanish conquest. Despite attempts to destroy these cultures, their spirit, knowledge, and traditions have survived and are still alive today.
There is something mysterious and powerful about these civilizations — even now, no one fully understands all aspects of their origins, beliefs, and achievements. The museum preserves enormous amount of fascinating artifacts that make you feel deep respect for this heritage.
The museum itself is very well designed, comfortable, and extremely engaging. The central courtyard with its massive, unusual fountain is mesmerizing and unforgettable.
And a special highlight — the gift shop. Honestly, it is probably the best museum souvenir shop in the world. Compared to it, the Louvre in Paris or the British Museum in London feel very modest. A huge selection of handmade items, authentic pieces, and surprisingly affordable prices — even cheaper than many souvenir shops outside.
I left absolutely impressed. A must-visit in Mexico City.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: An absolutely incredible museum! One of my favourites I’ve been to in the world. So cool, especially the lower level archaeology stuff. Definitely worth spending 3-5 hours in.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: An absolutely world-class museum and a must-visit in Mexico City. The exhibits are beautifully curated and incredibly informative, covering Mexico’s pre-Hispanic cultures in impressive depth. Highlights include the Aztec Sun Stone and the stunning Maya galleries, but every room offers something remarkable. The architecture itself especially the central courtyard with the iconic umbrella fountain is unforgettable. Plan to spend several hours here, it’s impossible to do justice to everything in a short visit. Wear comfortable shoes and bring a sweater. Also visit the Sala Gastronómica Restaurant that’s in the museum.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: An amazing experience for anyone interested in history and culture. This is an extremely popular and highly visited museum. Mornings are going to be generally less crowded as the lines can get quite long as the day progresses. Pro tip: save yourself one more line by buying your ticket in advance rather than just turning up. The ticket purchasing line looked longer that the queue to walk in the door, and it seemed to move much slower as well. You can easily spend an entire day looking at every exhibit, so pace yourself and take a break in the middle to rest so you can fully enjoy the entire experience. You won't be disappointed!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: An amazing museum of the history of Mexico. Well laid out. So many artifacts. Only downside is most of the plaques are in Spanish only. I used Google translate to read them. Well worth the admission, around $12 US.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: An amazing place to learn about the history and culture of Mexico! Everything is beautifully curated with incredible details, stories, and exhibits to explore: room after room filled with treasures. You could easily spend an entire day here.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
An oasis in the city, and the sculpture garden feels like a portal to another time. This museum can't be simply experienced in a single day. We were here for several hours and didn't even see half of everything. One of the best and most important museums in the Western Hemisphere, if not the world.

There are few places to sit inside, though, and no water is allowed so you can get dehydrated or go to the restaurant.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Awesome museum and definitely worthy of its reputation.  Great layout and collection, and they also did an excellent job with landscaping around.

Spent 3 hours here (skipped most of top floors)

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Beautiful architecture and great exhibitions. Don't miss out on the exceptional restaurant food.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Beautiful museum displaying ancient artifacts of Mayan, Aztec and Spanish colonization culture meshed together into what is now Mexican culture.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Beautifully designed and incredibly rich in history and culture. The exhibits are well-curated, immersive, and easy to follow. But it's a shame that there's not enough English interpretation and it's very troublesome to translate everything into English. Definitely not the way if you want visitors to better understand your culture. It would be so simple and easy to translate but I guess the museum is just too lazy to do this. The staff doesn't have knowledge of the timing of guided tour. Seems very unorganized. But still this is a must-visit amazing place.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Best musem in the world. Highly recommended. Be prepared to spend 6hrs or more.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Best museum I’ve been too. amazing historical artifacts. Although I would appreciate more respect to Pakal the great for he holds major significance in Maya history and it doesn’t feel right to be able to take pictures of someone that at one point was considered sacred to a nation. As well as the fact that his body is in the replica and not in his original tomb. All the dead that make appearance in the museum deserve a better delivery of respect. Other then that great place. I recommend going early to avoid lots of foot traffic.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: By far a must see while in Mexico City. It’s a vast museum, so give yourself a whole day because there are multiple buildings and floors. But we found it to be exceptional and worth it. We also took a break halfway through and enjoyed an amazing lunch at the museum restaurant. Wear your comfortable walking shoes and take as many pics as you can!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Came here 2 years ago but did not have enough time, so we wanted to come back. Spent the entire day in the museum and treated ourselves to a delicious lunch. Must see place, definitely!!!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Came to this museum after a short 10-15 mins walk from the Chapultepec Castle. Read so much about this #1 spot in TripAdvisor museum and boy, this museum really lives up to its spot. Well worth the entrance fee of M210 pesos ($12), increased from M100 pesos from 1 Jan 2026. Do allow 2-3hrs for a quick visit or 3-5hrs for a more detailed visit.
Good: Location is excellent, just an easy walk from the Chapultepec Castle. Museum is spacious. There are more than 10 exhibition halls, ranging from different era and civilisations from various parts Mexico and neighbouring countries, over a 2-floor square shaped complex. Museums displays so so so many artefacts, both indoors and outdoors.
Bad: The main overall displays are in Spanish and English, but the individual artefacts are mostly in Spanish.
Overall: A must-see when you’re in Mexico City. Photos online does not do justice to this museum. You have to experience it in person. The sheer size of the museum and the humongous number of artefacts displayed are beyond words. Highly recommended.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Definitely one of the best museums I've ever been too. The architecture, the way it's organized, the contents, everything is so carefully done. The history is fascinating. There are recreated temples, countless artifacts, and even tombs. Sometimes you're walking to a temple in a jungle, and sometimes you walk into a basement for a section on the underworld and a buried tomb.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: During the week we only waited 5-10 mins to get in. The museum is definitely a must as the Stone of the Sun is there and so many other important cultural pieces. I don’t recommend the restaurant and go somewhere before or after your visit. You will need at least 2 hours to see most of it at a quick pace. The gift shop here is also very reasonable and lots of local art to buy.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Enormous, interesting and educational. Very good restaurant with selection of regional foods.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Excellent experience at the museum, a must for anyone wanting to learn a dimension of Mexico history! Great artifacts and visuals that kept the visit stimulating and educational. Great spot for all ages, and the stage performances added a cultural.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Excellent museum! There are so many exhibits and so much history gathered in one place. Even several days wouldn’t be enough to see it all. It’s very well organized — there are lines for buying tickets, but the machines usually have no line at all; I have no idea why people wait. The building itself is worth mentioning — a grand and impressive structure. The price is reasonable. A wonderful experience overall. I’d highly recommend it to everyone.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Extremely impressive museum covering a diverse range of Prehispanic history and culture. Definitely worth visiting with free entry for students on Sundays. I recommend visiting earlier in the day to avoid crowds. Because this museum is so huge you definitely need to dedicate a few hours to see everything.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Fabulous!
I love a museum,  but this is the best I've visited throughout my years of travel. Seeing this countries earliest evolution, history and coulture come alive through archaeology (relics), sculpture, art, fabrics, prints, murials, jewellery, photographs and curated reenacted installations. The Mexican people have preserved their history well.
This is a large museum which has plenty to interest everyone
Mexico
As I'm not Spanish speaking, the only downside is that technology has not been introduced here and so there was a lot of time spent Google Translating.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Fantastic museum,  well worth a visit. Cheap entry, not too busy and lots to see both outside and inside from a wide range of different areas and eras. Would highly recommend visiting.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Fantastic, can't say enough good things about this museum. It is incredible.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Finally making my way through my list of museums to visit in CDMX—and this one did not disappoint. I was VERY impressed by both the artwork and the architecture. Walking through the main entrance and into the outdoor area, you’re immediately met with a massive pillar cascading with water—easily my favorite feature, along with the second-floor view facing the entrance. The outdoor seating area was a great spot to take it all in as well.

It took almost 30 minutes to get in (I went on a Sunday when admission is free), but it was well worth the wait. I’d recommend setting aside at least three hours to really absorb all the history—there’s a LOT to see!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Gorgeous museum grounds. Exhibits are well put together, spacing is great, and very informative. Need more than one day to get through everything and give exhibits the time they deserve!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Great experience over all. English version of descriptions would help, as there was hundreds of foreign tourists like us

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Great impressive museum. Super interesting, full of artifacts and history. My full recommendation. Only possible issue is that it potentially holds too much information. So visitors may apply certain strategies to cope with that, like just strolling around the exhibitions and see a bit of everything, or just focus on one culture and keep other exhibitions for later. Or get prepared by reading about the respective cultures and then not be overwhelmed by the sheer amount of information or all strategies combined. I was just totally overwhelmed by learning so much about the mesoamerican cultures, I am still digesting a week after my visit.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Great place! If you can visit definitely do it! Be prepared to be here all day, just because of everything there is to see!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Had an absolute blast. Always learned about Mexican culture so to be here in Mexico and see these artifacts in person!! 🔥🔥🔥

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Huge and wonderful! Takes several hours and comfortable shoes to explore even a part of it.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Huge museum! Prepare for several hours. Mostly divided by four big areas of Mexico. Lots of text in english and amazing amount of artifacts. Must visit museum.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
I found it a very interesting afternoon and admission was very cheap.

I struggled to understand what I was looking at a lot of the time as I only speak English and a lot of the signs weren’t bilingual. That’s my bad!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
I have been to many museums around the world and am a huge history enthusiast, and I think this the best museum I have ever been to.

The design with 24 different rooms for each subject, which have both indoor and outdoor areas, made the massive subject easier to comprehend.

We arranged an independent tour guide for a very reasonable cost, which we were very happy with, but the explanations in Spanish and English would have made it enjoyable on our own.

We visited with our two kids and were there for 3-4 hours, making it to 6 of the rooms. Plan multiple days if you want to take it all in.

Wish I could give 6 stars!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: I really liked the museum. The exhibitions and halls are properly set via the time periods and the culture of Mexico can be seen during the whole day. Definitely recommend you spent a few hours here.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: I recently was in Mexico City and I went to this museum. I have to say that I really enjoyed going here because it tells how Mexico came to be in the present from the past. It does an excellent job with the displays and very interesting. When I arrived here, I didn't have to wait but 5 minutes in line and you're allowed to buy food here. However you can't bring it inside the museum with you. There's an area in the lobby where you can leave your belongings that you don't want to bring inside the museum with you. The price was ok for a large museum. I was here for 4 hours and there's lots to see here.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: I spent 6 hours here and I need to come back because I missed a ton. An amazing museum. Not only for its collection but the way the museum continuously pulls you from outside to inside is incredible. I could stay here for a month.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
I think there's soooo much info and history in this museum.

We used AI to guide us through the different rooms and create a bespoke journey. Learned loads and had a good time

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: I waited in a long line because I forgot to buy the ticket in advance. My bad. The museum is large and well laid out with literally thousands of artifacts. You need to wear proper shoes to walk around for hours. I think it's best to go there and take a break after a few hours and have lunch at the restaurant which has pretty good food. I would definitely go back again.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
If I could give six stars, I would without hesitation! This museum is absolutely one of my favorite places in the world. The collection of art, culture, and historical artifacts is truly awe-inspiring—so extensive and diverse that it could easily take an entire day or more to explore everything thoroughly. Every exhibit is beautifully preserved, allowing visitors to truly appreciate the craftsmanship and historical significance behind each piece.

The layout of the museum can be a bit challenging to navigate at times, especially when trying to follow the chronological timelines and regional sections. However, I found that doing a little homework beforehand—such as watching documentaries or reading about the exhibits—really helps to fill in the gaps and enriches the overall experience. It’s well worth the effort, as each corner of the museum offers something fascinating and unique.

What stood out most to me was the depth of the collection and the care taken in its preservation. The staff was friendly and knowledgeable, adding to the welcoming atmosphere. Whether you're a history buff, an art lover, or simply curious about different cultures, there’s something here for everyone.

In summary, this museum is a true treasure trove of history and art, and I highly recommend it to anyone visiting the area. It’s a place that inspires awe, curiosity, and appreciation for the richness of human creativity and history. I can’t wait to return and explore more in the future!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: If you are visiting CDMX this is a must. The back with the ruins and the upstairs area which featured celebration costumes and artifacts were my favorite! So much to see and very pleasant layout. As I was leaving there was a long line so head there early if you can!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: If you can only go to one museum in México City (of the approx 180!), go here. The building itself is magnificent, and the various exhibits and artifacts are very well arranged.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: If you have a big impressive story to tell,you need a big impressive building to tell it from. The entire history of Mexico is here for all to see,grouped in chronological order of human development. If you like stone carvings this is the place. Really well presented in a fabulous setting. Go. You won't be disappointed.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
If you like history, then you have to visit this place. It has tons of artifacts and you can spend all day here if you want. If you casually like to learn about history then it’s a good couple of hours visit.

It’s big, clean, and worth checking out. Oh, and it has some of the cleanest bathrooms!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
If you only have time for one museum while you're in Mexico City, make it this one. It does an amazing job of capturing the history, culture, and spirit of Mexico all in one place. From ancient artifacts to modern design, every section has something striking to see.

The building itself is beautiful and worth exploring on its own. There’s so much to take in, so give yourself plenty of time if you can. Easily one of the highlights of our trip.

We had to stand in line to buy tickets at the entrance, which took a little while. It might be a good idea to buy them online ahead of time if you want to save time and get in faster.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
If you're in CDMX, this museum is absolutely unmissable. It’s one of the best anthropology museums in the world, showcasing Mexico’s incredible indigenous history and pre-Hispanic cultures like the Aztec, Maya, Olmec, and more.

Plan at least 5–6 hours to explore it properly. The museum is massive, and every room is packed with fascinating artifacts and stories. You could easily spend a full day here and still not see everything.

Highly recommend going with a guide. There’s so much rich context and symbolism that’s easy to miss without someone to explain it. A good guide brings the ancient civilizations to life and makes the experience much more meaningful.

Highlights: The Aztec Sun Stone (often mistakenly called the calendar), the replica of the Tomb of Pakal, and the impressive outdoor exhibits, including the giant waterfall fountain at the entrance.

Tip: Wear comfy shoes, bring water, and go early to avoid crowds!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: If you're in Mexico City, do yourself a favor and visit here. Just over $200 pesos per adult tkt. Spend at least 3-4 hours. Very enjoyable experience! Gracias

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Impressive museum, both in terms of architecture and it's collections. Definitely lives up to it's reputation as a world class facility. The ground floor contains historical artifacts from the various civilizations that inhabited México. The second floor focuses on modern anthropological topics including community, food, identify, and so on. Lots of public restrooms throughout the museum. The restaurant is quite good and is located behind galleries 10 and 11 on the lower level. The indoor/outdoor nature of the museum is fascinating and very unique. I highly recommend visiting.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Incredible value, world-class museum but bring a translation tool!

The Museo Nacional de Antropología is easily one of the best museums I’ve ever visited  and the entry fee is shockingly cheap for what you get (~$5) The scale, the depth of information, and the sheer richness of the exhibits make this a must-visit in Mexico City.

From the Stone of the Sun to massive Olmec heads and full-scale temple replicas, every hall is packed with artifacts that bring ancient civilizations to life. The building itself is a masterpiece, especially the central courtyard and its iconic waterfall pillar.

Important tips:
- purchase tickets online or be there early in the morning. It gets really crowded during the day.
- all exhibit information is in Spanish, with almost no English signage. If you're not fluent, make sure to bring a guide or use an AI image translator app it makes a huge difference in understanding the context and significance of what you're seeing.

A must-see for history lovers, especially those interested in Mesoamerican culture.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Incredibly big, incredible stuff to learn so much about this beautiful country and others. One of my top anthropology museums in the world.

It's big and they don't let you bring in water or food. You can buy once your pass the entry but just know if you brought your bottle they'll make you toss the water out .

Beautiful museum with tons to look at but your feet will tire out. Loved it.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: It was a very nice museum and there is so much to see. Honestly you can spend hours here. It's a lot of walking so definitely wear comfortable shoes. So much history. A little hard to follow the maps. But recommend.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
It was one of those Mexico City days with a soft rain that makes everything feel cinematic--until my Uber got into a minor accident on the way over. Everyone was okay (thankfully), but I showed up a little rattled.

Then the Museo Nacional de Antropología did what great museums do: it reset my brain. This place is enormous, and the exhibits are genuinely stunning--so much history and so many artifacts that you could spend all day here without getting bored. It's the kind of museum where you keep saying, "Wait, there's more?"

Bonus: I had a surprisingly great lunch on site, which made it easy to settle in and keep going. Rainy day, weird start, incredible finish.

Absolute must-visit in CDMX.

PS: don't touch the exhibits! Especially if your name is bad bunny

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Just exceptional. The stones are huge! I had the chance to be one of the first into the museum, we walked the entire thing and had breakfast and lunch at the restaurant inside the museum. Beautiful. Very little cell signal in the museum and the WiFi spots are not working. FYi.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Loads of information. Not a quick stop. Take your time to learn. The restaurant here is also very good.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Lots of history and cool artifacts.. plan for atleast 4 hours for the ground floor rooms

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Museo Nacional de Antropología – CDMX 🇲🇽
⭐ Rating: 10/10

An absolute must-visit in Mexico City — this museum is a true national treasure. 🏛️ From the moment you step in, you’re surrounded by the rich history and culture of Mexico, with incredible exhibitions ranging from Mayan and Aztec civilizations to fascinating artifacts from indigenous cultures across the country.

The architecture is breathtaking, especially the iconic central courtyard with the massive waterfall pillar — it’s one of those places that leaves a lasting impression. The displays are beautifully curated, and you can easily spend half a day or more exploring without getting bored.

💡 Tip: Go early, wear comfy shoes, and don’t miss the Aztec Sun Stone — it’s even more impressive in person.

#CDMX #MuseoDeAntropologia #MexicoCulture #MustVisit #HistoryLovers

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Museo Nacional de Antropología is absolutely worth a visit. Most of the information is in Spanish, but Google Translate works really well — especially the camera and audio transcription for the videos. The museum is huge; I spent a full two hours there and still didn’t manage to see everything, so allow plenty of time.

A nice practical touch: they ask you to store larger bags for free when you enter, which makes walking around much easier. If there’s a long line, you can book tickets online — I’d definitely do that next time to skip the wait. Overall, an impressive, immersive museum and a must-see in Mexico City.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Must see. The collection is amazing and the layout allows a comprehensive chronological flow OR to move to regions and eras of interest - don’t miss the second floor.

Highly recommend the Resturant if you’re not able to go to other areas of Mexico - it provides an excellent sample of regional dishes.

HIGHLY Recommend getting there early or getting tickets in advance otherwise a large line awaits you

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
My main complaints are that it's too big to see in one visit and they don't let you bring in water, but you walk a lot. I didn't see any water fountains either, but I needed it with all the walking. I spent 3 hours here and couldn't focus on anything anymore, I was hungry and thirsty, and still had 4 more rooms left to see.

The exhibits are nice, and some weave outdoors also, so don't leave your sunglasses/hat at the bag check. Bag/water bottle check is free.

Everything is in Spanish and some signs are in English. If you read all the Spanish signs, you'll be there forever. I recommend reading the main signs that have both languages, and then read only the descriptions of the pieces that you find most interesting.

Buy tickets at the white kiosks to the left (toward the bag check) if you can pay with card to avoid the wait at the manned ticket booths.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: My visit to the National Museum of Anthropology in Mexico City was an absolute marvel! From the moment I stepped into the grand central patio, I was captivated. Each hall was a treasure trove, brimming with artifacts that whispered tales of Mexico's rich pre-Hispanic past and vibrant indigenous cultures. The sheer scale and meticulous detail of the Aztec Sun Stone left me awestruck, its intricate carvings a testament to ancient astronomical knowledge. I spent hours admiring the delicate jade masks, the powerful Olmec colossal heads, and the fascinating reproductions of Maya frescoes. The dioramas brought ancient life to vivid reality, while the ethnographic exhibits provided invaluable insight into contemporary indigenous communities. Every turn offered a new discovery, a deeper appreciation for the ingenuity and artistry of these civilizations. The museum's layout guided me seamlessly through millennia of history, and the profound sense of connection to Mexico's heritage was truly unforgettable. It was an enriching and deeply moving experience that I'll cherish.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
National Museum of Anthropology is simply overwhelming in the best possible way. This is not just a museum with impressive artifacts — it presents the entire depth, scale, and continuity of Mesoamerican civilizations in a way that feels both monumental and coherent.
Walking through the galleries is a humbling experience. The scope of history, the sophistication of the cultures, and the clarity of the presentation make it impossible not to feel awe. It fundamentally changes how you understand Mexico’s past and its cultural foundations.
This is a world-class museum by any standard. Even if you’ve visited major museums around the world, this one stands out as truly unforgettable.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
One of the best museums I have seen in my life. A spectacular collection of historial indigenous artefacts. The Mexica room alone is worth the trip, and the museum is next to the wonderful Bosuqe de Chapultepec.
Please go if you are in CDMX!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: One of the best museums I’ve ever visited.  Book a tour guide, there’s so much to see and the help is worth it.  Midday seemed to be less crowded than I would have expected.  So good, I visited twice during my week’s stay in CDMX.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: One of the best museums I’ve ever visited. The collection is impressive, from the Aztec Sun Stone to the Mayan artifacts and the great Teotihuacán. It’s a deep dive into Mexico’s ancient cultures. Although government could improve the signs with a QR code or translation available.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
One of the best museums in Mexico City, the Museo Nacional de Antropología is both vast and fascinating. The exhibits span Mexico's rich pre-Hispanic cultures, from the Olmecs to the Maya and Aztecs, with incredible artifacts like the Aztec Sun Stone and giant stone heads.

The museum is well-organized, and the architecture itself is impressive—especially the central courtyard with its iconic umbrella fountain. Many displays have English translations, but having a guide or audio tour helps you appreciate the depth of information.

You’ll need at least 2–3 hours to see the highlights, but history buffs could easily spend the whole day. There's a café inside for a quick break, and the surrounding Chapultepec Park is a bonus.

Overall, a top-tier museum experience—don’t miss it while in CDMX

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: One of the best museums..but its huge so takes more than a day to see it properly.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: One of the most amazing museums I have ever been to. Stunning 60s architecture with a collection of Mexica and other American cultural artifacts that just completely overwhelmed me. I normally don’t revisit museums on a first visit to a city, but I might have to go back before I leave. Highest recommendation.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: One of the most beautiful museums I’ve been to ,truly impressive and well curated. If there’s one thing to improve, I wish there were more English explanations. They have a great overview in English at the beginning, but it would be nice if the smaller details inside were also translated.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Plan for an day to visit the museum if you want to see everything, or almost. Incredible display of ancient civilizations and their culture.

I wish there was more labels on items and their dates to better understand what they are and their cultural contexts. Main large texts include an English translation but smaller labels are Spanish only. I felt like I was missing out and also feeling overwhelmed by the many artifacts. It got increasingly difficult to stay focused trying to decipher and follow history across the different rooms. We were told the most important ones were the Mayan and Mexica/Mexicana/Aztec exhibitions.

Get a guide well-versed in history/anthropology if you can for your visit. Otherwise you may start feeling like you're missing information while admiring what will feel like more and more priceless rocks.

Buy tickets in advance and it's best to arrive around 15 mins before opening to avoid long lineups. Buy tickets in advance and avoid checking in your jacket to avoid additional lineups, especially to retrieve your stuff afterwards. There is a bag check and magnetic inspection at the entrance. No food is allowed inside.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Plethora of various artefacts from pre-hispanic civilazations of Mesoamerica and i guess that is what most if us come to Mexico for. You will find them here. Not sure why some descriptions are in spanish only though. Also during my visit some of the outdoor areas  were closed. I've been twice here, and not sure why but there was a day with free entrance.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Probablu the most gorgeous museum I’ve ever been to. It is amazing when it rains as natural waterfalls form with the structure of it. The content of the museum is very interesting and impressive

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Probably the best museum in all Mexico. Unfortunately most texts are in spanish but there are some English explanations also. Don't forget to watch introduction video in the hall in English before entering museum.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Probably the most fascinatingly designed museum I've visited. Really enjoyed the layout, the in and outdoor spaces blending together. Seamless flow throughout and remarkable history to peruse.
We were in a time crunch, but were first to arrive (arrived 15 minutes before entry and were first in line but that could be due to the holidays) and did a speed-run of the museum with what felt like the place to ourselves. Spent an hour, could've spent 3. Lots to see, lots to learn. Can buy tickets just as easy as the counter if online gives you trouble.

**Also recommend not buying until you see the line, we bought in advance for the day before, and when we saw the lineup at 12pm, decided to forget it and come back for opening****

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología

Really lovely museum! It's beautifully designed, and has an enormous amount of exhibits – you won't be able to see everything in one visit!

The process of getting tickets is quite challenge; the website seems to not work very well, nor does the WiFi on-site. There are several lines inside, none of which have signs, and stretch for long periods of time.

The museum itself was quite enjoyable, but I'd love to see them improve the entrance process.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: So beautiful with some amazing works of art. A must if youre in Mexico!

the best museum-worthy artifacts, and it has the right movement. I wanted to visit every day.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
The collections here are almost overwhelmingly large! We spent almost 3 hours and we feel we didn’t even see half of what was on display.
Our favourite was the more contemporary collection of textiles and clothing on the second floor.
Museum itself is nestled in the beautiful Chapultapec park; and we were grateful that the cloakroom was free.
We realised towards the end of our visit that there were recorded guides that we could have used to enhance our experience - many of the signs have Spanish and English, but the majority of the smaller signs have only Spanish, so an additional English language guide would have been helpful.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
