title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: The iconic Museo Nacional de Antropologia is one of this best place to go if you want know vast history of mesoamerica ancient, fully incredible pieces that portray fidelity the civilization that lived there.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: The most amazing museum of any kinds. It would take days to visit it entirely. Yet, unlike many large institutions of this type, it is never boring, always fascinating and well organized. The ticket price is also very cheap (7 USD) if compared to the Met or the Louvre.The architecture of the place is also captivating. It is quite interesting that among the nations that destroyed their native cultures, like Brazil and the US, Mexico is the only one that recognizes their importance and gives them such a true homage.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
The Museo Nacional de Antropología in Mexico City is truly one of the best museums I’ve ever visited—an absolute must for anyone coming to the city. This was my third visit, and I am still just as impressed and in awe as the first time. The museum is incredibly well designed, with collections thoughtfully laid out by region, making it easy to follow the cultural and historical context of each exhibit.

Every gallery feels intentional and immersive, offering a deep appreciation for Mexico’s rich and diverse heritage. The iconic fountain and waterfall in the central courtyard are absolutely breathtaking, and on a hot day, it’s the perfect place to pause, cool down, and take in the beauty of the architecture.
One of the standout highlights for me was the recreation of the burial tombs. Seeing them up close offers a perspective that photos simply can’t capture—detailed, atmospheric, and wonderfully presented.

Whether it’s your first visit or your fifth, this museum never stops inspiring. Highly recommended to anyone interested in history, culture, or simply experiencing one of Mexico City’s true gems.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: The Museo Nacional de Antropología is a powerful journey through Mexico’s ancestral heritage. Its indigenous history, sacred artifacts, and monumental Aztec Sun Stone reflect the depth and richness of Mesoamerican civilizations. An essential cultural experience in Mexico City.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
The Museo Nacional de Antropología is an absolute must when visiting Mexico City. It’s the largest and most visited museum in Mexico, and for good reason — the collections are incredible and showcase the richness of Mexico’s history and culture. The entrance fee is only $100 MXN, which is an amazing value for the experience you get.

If you are a fan of history, archaeology, or simply want to understand Mexico’s deep cultural roots, this museum is a must-do. Plan to spend several hours here — there’s so much to see and learn!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
The Museo Nacional de Antropología is an absolute must-see when visiting Mexico City! This museum is massive and packed with history—so many incredible artifacts all under one roof.

One of the highlights was seeing exclusive pieces from the Mayan ruins, including the iconic Mayan calendar. Every exhibit was thoughtfully curated and rich in detail.

If you plan to visit, make sure to give yourself plenty of time—we got there right when it opened and had no wait, but it got much busier as the day went on. There’s also a restaurant/café on-site if you need a break, which is super convenient.

Highly recommend adding this to your itinerary!

#MuseoNacionalDeAntropología #CDMXCulture #MexicoCityMustSee #MayanArtifacts #TravelMexico #MuseumDay #HistoryLovers #CulturaMexicana #CDMXMuseums #AnthropologyMuseum #MayanHistory #TravelTipsMexico #ExploreCDMX

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
The Museo Nacional de Antropología is an absolute must-visit. The galleries are extensive and filled with amazing artifacts. I regret not coordinating a guide, as there is a lot to learn, discover, and read.

The museum is laid out in a semicircle with a central open courtyard and pond. Best to plan your visit, as you will not see it all unless you plan to spend more than 1/2 a day.

Several exhibits also feature outdoor elements, which are a nice break after viewing a gallery of pottery. Personally, I was most impressed by the recreation of burial tombs and the various tricks that went into the afterlife for the deceased.

If you come early and realize that you MUST eat, there is a lovely restaurant on the lower level with an outdoor, covered patio. The food is ambitious and organized by region, which makes it fun to explore. I would say the restaurant offers much more than the small café, but the food is terrific. Since you didn't plan, I am not sure I would come here specifically for the food. It's good, but CDMX has excellent food everywhere, and the bar is high.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
The Museo Nacional de Antropología is incredibly interesting and easily one of those places where you can spend an entire day without getting bored. The exhibitions are extensive and well maintained, and the overall conditions of the museum are very good, both in terms of space and presentation.

There are no official audio guides, but I actually turned that into a plus by making my own with ChatGPT, which worked surprisingly well and let me go at my own pace. The Maya section stood out the most for me, especially the dedicated building, which is rich, dense, and genuinely impressive.

Another big advantage is the atmosphere. It was busy but not overcrowded, and there were not too many annoying tourists, so it was easy to focus and really engage with the material. If you are even remotely interested in history or culture, this museum is a must and absolutely worth a long, unhurried visit.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: The museum is amazing! So many artifacts and historical items. Felt so good connecting to the culture and soaking it all in. Definitely recommend visiting this place. You will need plenty of time to explore this place, we couldn’t see it all in 1.5hrs.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
The museum is very large and incredibly rich in information. It offers an impressive overview of Mexico’s history and cultures and is beautifully designed, with many well-curated rooms.
Unfortunately, a lot of the information is only available in Spanish, which is a bit disappointing for international visitors. Aside from that, it’s a fascinating and visually stunning museum and definitely worth a visit.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: The National Museum of Anthropology (Museo Nacional de Antropología) in Mexico City is the country’s most important museum, showcasing the rich history and cultures of Mexico’s Indigenous civilizations. Its highlights include monumental artifacts like the Aztec Sun Stone, Maya sculptures, and immersive exhibits that trace Mesoamerican societies from ancient times to the present, all housed in a striking modern building centered around a vast courtyard and iconic umbrella fountain.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: The National Museum of Anthropology in Mexico City is truly world-class. I spent three hours there and only saw a fraction of the exhibits. The history of Indigenous cultures is absolutely fascinating, with artifacts dating back to 10,000 BCE. The Mayan and Aztec exhibits were especially impressive and beautifully curated. There are so many original historical pieces that bring the past vividly to life.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
The reviews did not disappoint, and I can understand why this museum is one of Mexico's national treasures!

We had a tight schedule in Mexico City, and on day 1, we visited the museum after erroneously missing our bus stop.  As a result, we didn't have time to prepare, but the timing was perfect.  We visited on a Friday morning when the museum had just opened and was pretty much empty.

The architecture of the museum is very interesting, and I really enjoyed the balance between being indoors and then going outdoors to cross over to the next exhibit.  There's also access to outdoor gardens which are quite beautiful and relaxing.  The exhibits are spacious, everyone is helpful, and the museum is very well maintained.  During our visit, we weren't able to visit the second floor, I wasn't sure why it was closed off or if it's private.

The entrance price was 95 pesos, I believe.  The only regret I have was not having booked a tour.  I didn't realize it was an option until I saw a group of people with a tour guide.  I couldn't tell if it was a museum-guided tour or a private tour.  Nonetheless, it's a lot to see and read.  We stayed at the museum for roughly 3 hours trying to capture as much as we could, but I think in the future I would prefer a tour guide because there were many questions still unanswered.  Most of the museum is in Spanish, but there are some descriptions and videos in English.

Overall, I highly recommend visiting this museum if you're visiting Mexico City.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This is a beautiful collection of buildings housing a huge collection of artifacts from all of Mexico.  Each area is broken up into south, west, Baja, etc. The lobby level has included English in the descriptions but the 2nd floor does not. Still, you can get a good idea about the subject even if you don't speak Spanish. There were thousands streaming into the building, quickly and efficiently. It was a crowded Sunday afternoon but not wall-to-wall people somehow! There was a great restaurant with indoor and outdoor space for lunch. The menu was broken into sections of Mexico with specialties  from thos regions. And it's delicious!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This is a cultural landmark that lives up to the hype. Don't miss it. Also of the line for the tickets is long there is a self service line opposite from the booths. Plan to spend a lot of time here, it is huge.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This is a must see when in Mexico City.  The entrance fee is minimal and the history of the different eras are amazing.  You could actually do more than 1 day here to take it all in and discover all the areas of interest.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This is a must visit in Mexico City, it is an amazing museum and one of the best I have visited in the world. When you are going expect to walk a bit, we allowed 2 hours but we were short of time so I would say a minimum of 3 hours and give yourself at least 30-45 min for shopping.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This is a vast collection of anthropological delights, with carvings and much much more. Put on your comfy shoes and take in some real historical artefacts and cultural information.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This is an absolute must when you are in Mexico City. Make sure you budget at least 3/4 hours to do it properly (you can spend more time in here for sure!). Not only the building and facilities are amazing, but you can learn so much about Mexican history. Please do yourself a favor and visit this museum!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This is an exceptional cultural art experience and one not to be missed. Take your time. I just did the ground floor over about three hours. Exhausting but definitely worth visiting. Will have to return to explore level one when I return to CDMX.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
This is not just a museum, it's a true educational experience that will fundamentally change the way you understand Mexico, especially if you know little about its complex, ancient past.

The museum is beautifully structured, starting with a primer on general anthropology and ethnographic studies and then precedes in a chronological fashion to detail the ancient civilizations that settled Mexico, going into rich detail about society, culture, everyday life, religion, history, diet, living arrangements, etc...

I HIGHLY recommend the restaurant as well, very good food with an upscale tranquil atmosphere!

I have a video on my YouTube travel vlog @thewholetravel that covers this museum in greater detail with recommendations and other major sites at Chapultepec Park.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This is one of the best museum I have been too. Super clean and organized very detailed alot of information around and very unique pieces.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This is one of the best museums in Mexico. The museum is extensive, you will need several hours to view the different cultures areas; but if you have no much time to visit, you cannot miss the Mayas. That is simply my favorite area.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This is the museum you cannot missed when you're in Mexico City. 100 pesos entry fee don't need to book advance.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This may be one of the best museums I have ever been to.  The collection across preMex civilizations is fantastic.   Reserve several days to get thru it.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This Museo is a must see in CDMX, both for the spectacular exhibits and the architecture. We recommend arriving early - we arrived at 9 AM just as the museum opened, and we found it pleasantly uncrowded, though by the time we left, it was starting to get very busy. We hired a guide, (which I will rate separately) and that’s probably a good idea as there is just so much to see.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This museum has incredible variety, indoor and outdoor, and massive artifacts and entire rooms that transports us through history. When I went it was also free entry. Deserves more than 5 stars for all of the reasons above! Prepare around 5 hours if you want to see all of the rooms in detail

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This museum is absolutely incredible the collection of Aztec and Mayan artifacts is world-class and a must see in CDMX. However, the experience of waiting in line to get in felt like a trip back to the Stone Age. Maybe they kept the ticketing system ancient to match the theme of the museum? Everything else was perfect, but be prepared to practice some "ancient" patience at the entrance.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
This museum is an absolute must-visit in Mexico City. The moment you step inside you can feel the depth of history and culture that Mexico has to offer. The exhibits are beautifully curated — from ancient Olmec and Maya artifacts to detailed explanations about Aztec life, language, and cosmology. The layout is logical and easy to follow.

The centerpiece, the Aztec Sun Stone, is breathtaking in person — far more impressive than in photos. Every room offers something new to learn, and the artifact preservation is exceptional. The outdoor gardens and courtyard are also lovely places to take a break between galleries.

Highly recommend for history and culture lovers — or anyone visiting Mexico City who wants a deeper understanding of the country’s rich heritage!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This museum is one of the best spots in Mexico City if you’re into history and culture. It’s packed with amazing artifacts from ancient civilizations like the Aztecs, Mayans, and Olmecs — including the famous Aztec Sun Stone. The building itself is beautiful and the exhibits are well laid out and informative.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
This museum is so rich in culture. A must visit in Mexico city. Only 100 Mexican pesos. The museum is huge and you will
spend easily 2 to 4 hours there.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This museum was absolutely beautiful. It does a great job of education on all the indigenous cultures to Mexico and acknowledges the way colonialism has impacted the loss of culture and language. It was wonderful to see so many artifacts recreations and beautiful pieces of art

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This place is amazing. The exhibits are beautiful. The museum is so huge that you need more than one day to visit. Also the restaurant at the museum has delicious food.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This place is massive and $100 pesos per person is not bad. Also, kids can go in for free and on Sundays if you're a Mexican citizen (most provide an ID) your entrance is free as well.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
This place is nestled in a beautiful area of Mexico City. The museum is huge! We spend 3 hours here and didn't even see everything. You could easily turn this into an entire day if you really wanted to stop and read everything.

It is important to note, though they do have English text on some displays, most of the writing is in Spanish so take some sort of translator if you can't read Spanish.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This place was insane!!! It was massive and we only completed half of it and stuck to the first floor. It’s a well curated museum with so many places to read and explore. Learning about the full history of Mexico through this museum was really enlightening and fascinating. It was such an our ish space that we didn’t get into a crowd once and there was plenty to see.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This was a fantastic museum.  Worth the time and money.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
This was definitely one of the best museums I’ve ever been to. It was a really fantastic activity that me and my friends are able to go to where we got the chance to learn about their history and culture… The museum was bigger than I expected it to be with different sections and we enjoyed every bit of it. The collections were so amazing… from Aztec, to Mayan, to Teotihuacan… amazing artifacts to stand infront of. We unfortunately went before it was about to close, but I definitely plan on coming back to this museum next time I’m back in Mexico City so that I can explore everything here further than I was able to this time.

We highly recommend that everyone goes to this museum during their visit to Mexico City

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: This was one of my favorite places to visit while in the city. There is so much to see and learn, we didn’t feel the time go by. It’s so surreal to be able to stand in front of such monumental pieces of history and culture.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Though a bit English explanation might lack, so many things to see and learn there. A well made museum, with area corresponding to different era. A must see in Mexico City

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Truly incredible experience. Unless you go from open to close, you won't get a chance to dive into absolutely everything.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Truly, the biggest gem Mexico City has. The hype is real, people!! It's a behemoth so plan on a whole day or 2 days.  Each piece holds so much ancient history of the people of the meso-Americas. A well organized museum but you'll have to ask dossiers as i could not find a map. Every staff member i spoke was pleasant and helpful.  If you can get your tickets ahead of time,  I would as there were lines.  Their restaurant is superb and a nice break from the craziness of the first floor.  Don't forget to explore the garden areas outside each hall. It was the best experience I have had in a museum in such a long time.  My only issue is that only some plaques are translated into English and lots of artifacts have no explanations at all

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Very nice museum with lots of info on Mexico old history. I found the description lacking in many cases, where you are left wondering what is what. One example is the ancient capital that is now the center of Mexico City: you would not know it by simply looking at the maquette and reading the legenda.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Visited 2-5:30pm on a Thursday and there was a distinct lack of student groups then, so recommend afternoons for perhaps a quieter experience if you can't get there first thing. The museum was not crowded at all. If you do not speak Spanish, a tour guide is highly recommended, as there is very little in the way of English signage and you will miss a ton of context without one. I booked the "small group" Airbnb Experience tour with Raul and it was a phenomenal, insightful way to spend 3 hours.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Visited on Sunday and it's free for locals and residents.
Entry was easy and fast.
Well organized. The gift shop was good.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Visiting the anthropology museum was a great experience. There is so much to see, it feels endless! The museum holds an immense collection of relics, and even its architecture is remarkable. To truly appreciate everything, you should plan to spend at least 3–4 hours. It’s a truly unforgettable experience that no one should miss. Highly recommended.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Visiting the Museo Nacional de Antropología was one of the highlights of my trip to Mexico. The museum is not only architecturally stunning, with its iconic “umbrella” fountain at the entrance, but also incredibly well-curated and rich in history.

Each hall takes you deep into the world of Mexico’s ancient civilizations—Olmec, Maya, Mexica (Aztec), Teotihuacan, and more. The displays are thoughtful, detailed, and visually impressive. You don’t just see artifacts; you feel immersed in the culture they came from.

One of the most impressive pieces is the Sun Stone (often called the Aztec Calendar), which is absolutely massive and mesmerizing. I was also blown away by the Olmec colossal heads, the Mayan stelae, and the recreations of ancient temples and tombs inside the museum.

The museum is huge, so give yourself plenty of time—at least 3–4 hours if you want to do it justice. There are also modern ethnographic sections that show the diversity of indigenous communities still living in Mexico today, which adds a powerful, contemporary layer to the historical narrative.

Pros:
•	Spectacular architecture and layout
•	Incredibly rich and well-organized exhibits
•	Clear explanations in both Spanish and English
•	Affordable entrance fee

Cons:
•	It can be overwhelming due to its size, so wear comfortable shoes
•	Gets crowded during weekends and holidays

Whether you’re a history lover or just curious about Mexico’s roots, this museum is absolutely worth the visit. It’s more than just a collection of artifacts—it’s a living story of a country’s cultural heritage. Highly recommended!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Visiting the National Museum of Anthropology in Mexico City was an amazing experience. The museum is huge, beautifully designed, and full of stories that help you understand Mexico beyond food and sightseeing. From traditional weaving looms and Indigenous homes to colorful ceremonial dresses and everyday tools, everything reflects deep cultural roots. What I loved most is that it never feels boring. The visuals, colors, and craftsmanship keep you engaged throughout. Entry is only 100 pesos per person and absolutely worth it.

Some of the houses even reminded me of old Bangladeshi village homes. The mud walls and simple structures felt familiar and nostalgic, showing how cultures far apart can still share similar ways of life.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: We arrived today 45 minutes before closing and got in for free! If you don’t plan to stay for a long time, I’d try this an hour before closing. The locker rental was also free. We really enjoyed walking around with less people in the place as well.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
We booked the "Amigo" guided tour and were helped by this awesome Leonardo dude. This is such a great experience where we learned so much about the amazing history of Mexico, about its culture and traditions, and it's definitely a blast!

Would absolutely recommend this museum, this guided tour, and Leonardo the perfecto, to everyone who's interested.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: We bought tickets ahead of time, so we had no wait time.  The main signs have English.   The artifact signs did not.  Used Google translate to get the artifact signs translated.  Excellent place to get an inkling about the mexican culture.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: We came here on a Friday afternoon and there was a 15 minute line to get in to buy tickets. The museum is very large. Give yourself multiple hours to complete. It starts with the history of humanity as a whole, then goes into the development of the Mexican communities across Mexico and how they were different. It continues with the Aztec and Mayan empires and massive relics from from those eras. I would definitely recommend as the museum is beautiful and informative.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: We spent probably 9 hours there total, a day in a half. We read some things but not all. One of the best museums we have ever been to and locals said its the best in the whole country. We found things here that they talk about at the ancient sites, some very famous pieces and good replicas. We gains a solid education by reading the signs and watching the videos. The museum it self is beautiful, comfortable tempature. Getting your tickets online saves you probably on average 30 mins of lines. If the line outside is past the shops then its about a 30-40min wait to get inside and then another 30-40 wait inside to get tickets. Go early! There is a restaurant but no food or water allowed inside. Plenty of restrooms. 2 stories. Public holidays they double the price.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
What a complex! We took the Turismo bus (yes, they are wheelchair accessible!) to it, and lo and behold we didn't need our portable ramp to get around this huge set of museums. We tried to hit all the major parts of the museum, but you really need to take more than one day to grasp the full scale of the place. And for essentially $5 US dollars per person, it's pretty much a steal!

As you can see, we mostly stuck to the sections with older artifacts, basically triaging our time so we could get the most out of it. The wheelchair accessibility is pretty good, save for the back of buildings that led out into gardens and a tomb replica.

As for getting something to eat, there are vendors strung along in a row right in front of the main entrance plaza, and the food is cheap and delicious. Sit and eat in the adjoining park area and enjoy dogs and children playing in the fountains.

And of course, last but not least, there is a gift shop, where you can buy all sorts of books and other items to take home with you.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Wonderful pieces and majestic architecture,  although some lighting is needed to read certain texts. Would appreciate to be more insightful in certain items if the visitor wants to dig deeper.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
Wonderful place to come with an awesome architecture. So many beautiful pieces and lots of information, everyone who works there is incredibly nice and helpful. Not very expensive, and definitely can take a while should you choose to read everything, lots of small and large pieces alike.

They do have a bag / umbrella check, as you will need to check the umbrellas. Not a hard process.

The main area has a beautiful courtyard and huge water fall structure in the middle, great for pictures.

Would recommend on a visit to Mexico city.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: World class museum based on Mexico’s ancestry. the museum is very well laid out and is incredibly comprehensive. I went on a tuesday morning and for whatever reason more than half of it was closed. the entirety of the upstairs was sectioned off and about 2 exhibits on the first floor. I will have to come back. they also open at 9 but didn’t start letting people in until 920. when i arrived at 915 there was already a healthy queue, but the line moves quickly

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Wow oh wow is this ever a beautiful museum. Was here for a few hours and could still spend more time. Wait is a bit long, but the line moves fast

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Wow this place is massive! This is a two level, U-shaped museum offering thousands of unique historical finds from all over Mexico. They also have foreign exhibits seasonally. You start on one end, work your way through the halls, and end up at the end of the building.  Plan on spending at least 2-3 hours exploring all the great things to see. There is a little park and courtyard right outside where you can grab a quick snack before going in.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Wow this was an amazing museum! Absolutely massive though so don’t get too bogged down at the start. I don’t speak Spanish but there was still plenty in English but not everything. Really impressive architecture. You can also leave your bag for free in a locker.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Wow. Simply amazing. Huge. You’d need a whole day to really take it in. Learning about indigenous Mexican history and anthropology is a must. The restaurant was really nice and served interesting and delicious food from all regions of Mexico, paired with excellent service.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: Yes, there are long lines to get in and the exhibits can be crowded. That said, this museum has one of the best collections of ancient artifacts in the world and is absolutely a must-visit in Mexico City. The Mexica and Maya exhibits are outstanding. There’s also an on-site restaurant, so you can take seat and enjoy some food & drinks without leaving the museum. 100% recommended!

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: You will need a week to fully cover your visit at the museum.  There are a lot of intact artifacts and history to see and discover.  Although,  most of the info written in Spanish,  you need an app to translate.  No food or beverage is allowed.  Overall, very pleasant experience.  A must see !

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text:
You'll need either a full day or two half-day sessions to explore the museum - This is one of the greatest collectioms of art, culture, and artefacts that I have seen, and the Museum curators have done a wonderful job of showcasing Mexican history and heritage throughout.

Truly an amazing experience.

title: Museo Nacional de Antropología
text: You'll need some time for this one! As one of the largest museums in Mexico City, it's a true gem of mostly original works. Lines were a bit long but we were part of a tour so things moved quickly to enter. Atmosphere was welcoming, the artwork was phenomenal, and the place was super clean and easy to maneuver from gallery to gallery. Highly recommend spending some time here to soak it all in.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: A bit too posh, too much gold everywhere, cannot recommend

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: It is an amazing museum, but because of the staff and behavior this museum doesn’t deserve even one star. First they asked for a ticket from my six-months old daughter. This is incredible and never seen anything like this anywhere in the world. They were very rude in the entrance while they were asking another ticket for my baby. They stole my wallet in this museum, just in the entrance before jordan staircase. The security did nothing, they didn’t check the cameras and they asked us to go to police. I emailed to Museum Directorate but they didn’t respond. There is an organized crime in this museum so please be careful during your visit. Always check your belongings, pockets, bags etc. Lastly, museum staff should learn to speak English as soon as possible. If you want tourists in the country, you should teach your staff how to communicate.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text:
Yes, you definitely have to visit it. But be prepared for these:
1. At least 4 hours line, that might be stoped at any moment because they say: “the museum is fulfilled with visitors, we are closing entrance till further instructions”.
2. There are no toilets. So while you’ll be waiting to enter the museum buy an adult pumpers or something like that. Do not take coffee or tea! Remember, there is no place to pee.
3. Also you have to know, that you might buy tickets at e-desk (at right side of courtyard). These tickets will allow you to enter the museum without the line, until at any moment  they say: “the museum is fulfilled with visitors, we are closing entrance till further instructions”.
4. At all this moments and time - if you are a part of a group, you may enter (WTF?!?) at anytime.

Btw, there where +3 Celsius and windy and snowy.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: Far to busy. Couldnt realy enjoy visit as it was so packed.  Went on a organized excursion from cruise ship with Cruise Excursions.  Would not recommend that company. title: State Hermitage Museum
text: A large museum with many antiques and artifacts, but in reality I felt bored. It needs to be developed in a way that makes the place more enjoyable and organized.

title: State Hermitage Museum

title: State Hermitage Museum
text:

Pain in the arse Chinese tourists though

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: As a museum, it's spectacular and fantastic. The rooms, the hallways, the stairs, the ceilings, every square foot is a piece of art. Needless to talk about, the number of art masterpieces presented in this museum: the paintings, the sculptures, the decors, the ancient items, and more, all that are fantastically amazing and gorgeous One can spend a week coming to this museum every day and taking time to enjoy every piece of art. title: State Hermitage Museum

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: Hermitage Museum, commonly known as the Hermitage, it is a museum of art and culture on the Neva River in St. Petersburg, Russia. It is the largest art museum in the world by exhibition space . It is one of the most popular museums in the world, along with the Louvre in France, the British Museum in the United Kingdom, and the National Palace

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: Hermitage Museum. Commonly known as the Hermitage, museum of Art and Culture on the Neva River in St. Petersburg, Russia.Only a small part of the collection is on permanent display, and more than three million artifacts are preserved. The complex occupies an entire block of three-story buildings on a grand scale and lavishly decorated. A visit to St. Petersburg is a must-see

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: How not to be scammed into extra payments.. Excellent place but reach at 10am. And NO need to Buy Skip the line as there are no real lines worth skipping by paying extra. Read on.. Do not buy entrance tickets from street vendors, tour operators and Internet bookings. Buy them ONLY at the ticket counter INSIDE the Museum. Pay for WHAT U WANT. Other Vending machines will give u 700 Rubles tickets for 4 Museums including Hermitage, State, Porcelain and another. Some of these are like 10kms away and you may not even have time to see them. Plus certain rooms within Hermitage like Gold Room have extra payments needed in ADVANCE. So I recommend you BUY ONLY THE BASIC TICKET COSTING 400RUB and then add on whatever you want rather than end up buying a 700 ruble ticket and end up not using most of it and then paying extra for the gold room etc.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: I am running short of words after exploring the Hermitage museum. I could not complete it but what I witnessed is beyond explanation. You need to complete one day to see the whole museum. You will find long queues of people waiting to explore it from inside. It is worth watching and tickets usually start from 700 rubles. If you want to skip lines tickets can cost you between $40-60. This place is highly recommended to visit but be ready to exhaust one day especially if you are on a short trip.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: I'm giving it 4 stars because they didn't need an AC; the weather was freezing. Beautiful place, though.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: Loved it. Too much to see In one visit but what we did was amazing. Only down side to many people visiting at the same time.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text:
Museum is a must visit in Saint Petersburg. It's amazing place, amazing  structure and exhibitions and a great experience. The reason I took a star and almost caused me to give up the visit was the ridiculous unecceptable long line to get in. The line to get tickets was around 75 minutes, and  my mom in law was the only reason we staed.
BUY TICKETS ONLINE.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text:
Nice copy, but as nice as the original...

Growing up, I had always dreamed of visiting this former palace/ museum because of of a book I owned as a child about the wonders of the new world. It looked like it came right out of a fairy tale in those pictures. Later, I read the story of its construction along with that of the entire city of St-Petersburg. Apparently, Peter the Great decided to build a city that could rival the likes of Paris, London and Vienna to his honor. Understandably, the city's architecture was heavily influenced by that of those western European cities. Although I found St-Pete very beautiful, I personally felt that it just looked like an attempt to "copy" Paris or Vienna. Unfortunately, a copy can never be as good as the original and I really noticed that there. This Hermitage Museum/ Winter Palace confirms the pattern. Indeed, although I found it beautiful overall, I couldn't help but notice that it looked like a cheaper version of the Palace of Versailles. Besides, I noticed that the building wasn't as well preserved as I thought it would be. Every here and there I would notice small cracks or chipping paint on walls, humidity stains etc. This didn't fit the fairy-tale image I had from my childhood.

Nevertheless, it was a very pleasing visit. The palace/museum is incredibly vast and every new room you step into feels like its own new magical universe. t is easy to be transported into the grandeur of that Russian imperial era. However, I'd say you'll  definitely need at least 3 to 4 hours to really enjoy it...

title: State Hermitage Museum
text:
Normally I'd rate this outstanding museum 5+ stars, but having no English option on a paid guided tour is unacceptable.
The second largest museum in the world, set in one of world's most beautiful palaces is a must on every traveler list. The collection is incredible - spread in several adjoining buildings beside the splendid Winter Palace, and at the General Staff bldg as well. World history art biggest names are here.
Covid rules - TIPS:
- BUY your tickets ONLINE - due to Covid the volume of daily admittance is limited and I am not sure if they sell tickets at the counter at all !!
- Tickets are on a time slot basis with 15 min tolerance.
- You have TWO fixed routes to choose from - you can NOT combine them, nor you can take the other when you finish the first (unless you have purchased both tours with different timings),
- You can buy the guided Diamond room or guided Gold room (neither of them you can visit alone). They last 1 hour after you're left alone to walk yourself one of the routes. Tickets are almost double the fixed route, but still much cheaper than in Europe (around 15$) for such a museum,
- I chose the Diamond room tour and after 20 min was very bored and after 40 min I left the tour. The guide spoke only Russian (there was NOT an option online for English tour, so I thought it'd be bilingual), which is quite unacceptable for such a world class museum and therefore I deduct one star

title: State Hermitage Museum

title: State Hermitage Museum
text:
The State Hermitage Museum in Saint Petersburg, Russia, is an absolute must-visit for anyone traveling to the city. It’s not just a museum — it’s a journey through art, history, and beauty all in one place. Founded by Catherine the Great in 1764, the Hermitage is one of the largest and most impressive museums in the world, with over three million works of art and artifacts. From ancient Egyptian relics to masterpieces by Leonardo da Vinci, Rembrandt, and Van Gogh, there’s something here to amaze every visitor.

The main building, the Winter Palace, is a work of art itself. Its grand halls, golden staircases, and lavishly decorated rooms will leave you speechless. Walking through it feels like stepping straight into the era of Russian emperors. Every corner is picture-perfect — a mix of stunning architecture and world-famous paintings.

One of the highlights is the European art collection, especially the Italian Renaissance and French Impressionist galleries. You can easily spend an entire day exploring and still feel like you’ve only scratched the surface.

Whether you’re an art lover, a history buff, or just someone who appreciates beautiful places, the Hermitage Museum will absolutely captivate you. It’s more than a museum — it’s an experience that will stay with you long after your trip. Don’t forget your camera and some comfortable shoes — you’ll need both!

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: The State Hermitage Museum is truly grand — more like a royal palace than a regular museum. If you are a real museum lover, then this place is absolutely worth visiting because there is so much to see and explore. The collections are huge, the rooms are beautifully decorated, and you can easily spend hours walking around. But if you don’t enjoy museums or get bored quickly, then you might find this place tiring because it is very big and has a lot of historical artworks and displays. It definitely requires time and interest to appreciate fully. There is an entry fee, but for museum lovers, the experience and the beauty of the palace interiors make it totally worth it.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: Visited during the pandemic. There are 2 routes to choose from. Each "session" is for 2 hours. I took a wrong turn and ended up straying from my route. I was guided back by a nice staff lady, and ended up spending more than the two hours in the museum  (luckily no one checked my ticket at the exit). The collection is incredible and enormous. One of the best in the world. More Rembrandt in one place, than I've ever seen in European museums, enamel from Limoges, pre-rennaissance Italian icons, DaVinci, Italian ceramics, great Egyptian collection, entire hall with Greek vases. You can take photos without a flash. Small-medium backpacks are allowed. Water bottles are not allowed. I was paranoid about pickpockets and thieves, and clung to my backpack the whole time, but everything was ok.  Unfortunately, it's impossible to see the entire museum right now, because of the pandemic, but you would need a week to see everything, anyway.

title: State Hermitage Museum

title: State Hermitage Museum

My girlfriend and I bought the 2-day tickets online through the Hermitage website (not through skip the line), and the lineup for online ticket holders only took about 5 minutes!! If you’re interested in impressionist paintings (Van Gogh, Picasso, Matisse, Gauguin etc) they’ve been moved temporarily to the General Staff Building across from the Hermitage - so make sure your ticket allows you to enter both buildings. We didn’t get an audio guide or a private guide as we felt that it would take up more time, and there’s too much to see. Anyway we loved it!

title: State Hermitage Museum
text:
After that, I went to the Winter Palace, and honestly, I think it was the most impressive site I saw in Saint Petersburg. I haven’t been to Versailles in France, but this place felt like the ultimate display of wealth, power, and artistic taste.

The decoration and aesthetic were absolutely jaw-dropping — it felt like every corner was designed to overwhelm you with beauty and grandeur. It’s truly a miracle of human history. I couldn’t stop thinking: How can people be this rich?!

And the best part? The entrance ticket was only about 50 RMB. For such an iconic and stunning place, that’s unbelievably affordable. If you’re in Saint Petersburg, you can’t miss it.

The State Hermitage Museum has millions of artefacts, we spent 6 hours and browsed through most of the rooms and galleries. I measured that it was over 22 kilometres in walking distance. I was glad that I could do it while I still have the health and mobility. The eye opening knowledge we got is just priceless.
There will not be the same exhibits in many rooms for every visit, as they change them from time to time.
I would highly recommend any one to go no matter you love history or not, because the Hermitage has everything that would suit your interests. Daily artefacts, arts, paintings, sculptures, manuscripts, photos, you just name it. We found it so awesome that the building itself was a piece of art at the age of 300 years. Just this is already older than most modern cities in many parts of the world.
St Petersburg itself is already an art piece, building from scratch on a swampy land ! Come and see for your self, I couldn't think of other advice that is more practical, hah.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text:
During Catherine the Great’s rein she had her eyes set on the west moreso than the east and brought back a vast collection of souvenirs from her travels. As a patron of the arts, literature and education, Catherine the great’s personal collection of art and artefacts became, in 1852, what is now known as the Hermitage Museum.

Today the Hermitage Museum occupies the Winter Palace and a number of other connected buildings overlooking the River Neva and is recognised as one of the largest collection of art anywhere in the world. It would take many days or weeks to observe each piece of art and you’ll find the most popular pieces, like an original Da Vinci, with the appropriate crowds gathered in front.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text:
For me, the building was the most impressive part of the museum. When you think you saw the top showpiece hall, there are twenty more to follow.

Then the collection is of course substantial as well. It consists mainly of 18th century works, and works that were famous at that time, and early 19th century works. There is hardly anything (if anything at all?) from past 1850. The collection therefore documents wonderfully the artistic taste of the Enlightenment and Empire epochs.

Many information texts are in Russian and English, but many also are in Russian only, especially the more recent additions.

For tourists without a bank card, the only way to buy tickets is at the cash desk inside, and that takes about an hour and 15 minutes.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text:
Having traveled extensively round East Europe I ,without any doubt, believe that just behind The Louvre Museé en Paris, The British Museum en London, El prado in Madrid, The Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York and the Pergamo in Berlín ,The Hermitage does get a place as one of the most beautiful museums in the word displaying a wide array of art.

Undoubtedly visiting the Hermitage is but a must when coming to Russia. I’ve visited twice Russia though the first time I didn’t visit it and. If you are  going to Russia do not miss it.

As with large museums around the world, you may need to come round more than twice to get to see and appreciate the vast array which is displayed here, however, I have a few mentions which I personally find amusing and appealing.
1. Go and admire the Cameo Gonzaga from Alexandria, Egypt, corresponding to 3rd century BC. This unique double portrait was presented in 1814 to Emperor Alexander I by Josephine, wife of Napoleon Bonaparte, as an act of gratitude for the Russian tsar’s personal visit to her palace near Paris to guarantee her safety while the victorious allied troops entered the city. It is on display in the Cameo Gallery on the first floor of the New Hermitage building.

2.Aphrodite (Venus Tauride). Ancient Greece,  from the 2nd century BC.This 167-cm high marble statue was a gift to Peter the Great from the Vatican (other sources say it was exchanged for the relics of St. Bridget of Sweden). The "pagan idol" was initially put on public display in the Summer Garden, before being moved to the Tauride Palace, home of Prince Potemkin. The statue was transferred to the Hermitage in 1852 to mark the opening of the New Hermitage (where it still stands in Room 109).
3. Statue of Jupiter. Ancient Rome, late 1st century AD.This colossal sculpture of the ancient God was acquired for the Hermitage in 1861 by Alexander II from the bankrupt Marquis of Campana. One of the largest statues in the museum, it is on display in Room 107 of the New Hermitage.

4. Leonardo da Vinci. Madonna Litta, 1490s. One of the first works of the High Renaissance, this painting was created for the rulers of Milan. The Hermitage bought it in 1864 from the Duke of Litta, a member of an aristocratic Milanese family, whose collection had held the painting for several centuries. The work is exhibited in Room 214 of the Large (Old) Hermitage.

5. Raphael. Conestabile Madonna, 1504 This early work by the great Raphael was in the collection of Count Conestabile della Stafa in Perugia, Italy. In 1871, the painting was bought by Tsar Alexander II for his wife, Maria Alexandrovna. The work was moved to the Hermitage in 1881 at the behest of the empress. See it in Room 229 of the New Hermitage.

6.Michelangelo. Crouching Boy, 1530–34. Sculpture by Michelangelo in Russia. Boy was bought by Catherine the Great in 1785.In 1851 it joined the museum in honor of the opening of the New Hermitage, where it is on display in Room 230.

7. Caravaggio. The Lute Player, 1596–96 The picture exists in three versions. The other two are housed in the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York and Badminton House in England. The third version, kept in the collection of Italian collector Vincenzo Giustiniani, was acquired by Alexander I in 1808. The Lute Player can be found in Room 232 of the New Hermitage.

8.Diego Velazquez. The Lunch, 1617 Catherine the Great had it ,so it dates back to the early days of the museum, although there is still some debate about its true authorship. This chef d'oeuvre by the Spanish master is on display in Room 239 of the New Hermitage.

9. Peter Paul Rubens. Perseus Releases Andromeda, 1622. This masterpiece, dedicated to the ancient Greek myth of Perseus, it is exhibited in Room 247 of the New Hermitage.

10. The Peacock Clock Automaton. London, 1770s This luxurious mechanical clock made by English jeweler James Cox was ordered for Catherine the Great by her courtier (and favorite) Prince Potemkin.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text:
The Hermitage is one of the most famous museums of the world, so there is no point to write about the beautiful art, gorgeous architecture or the pompous furniture.
What you need to bring is a lot of time, as it is a huge palace. The ball room on the first floor is 1000 square meters in size alone, to give you some point of reference.
When you enter, you can buy tickets at the machines in the yard, and leave your coats and bags in the cloak room for free.
Every room has an attendant, who will answer any questions and can give you some directions in multiple languages.
Plan your route ahead, as some expositions close earlier than others, and you don't want to miss out, just because you were too captivated by a room or statue or painting.
There is a cafe inside next to the gift shop, so you don't have to worry about drink or snacks.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: The Hermitage Museum in St Petersburg is one of best museums in Europe. Housed in the winter palace of the erstwhile Czar of Russia, the museum has some of the best art and artifact from this part of the world. Primarily, it showcases the old collection of over 3 million pieces making it the second largest museum in the world. A must do in St Petersburg.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text:
The Hermitage Museum in St. Petersburg is an unparalleled treasure trove of art and history, a must-visit for any cultural enthusiast. Spanning vast corridors and countless rooms, it's imperative to allocate a full day for this immersive experience. The sheer scale and richness of the exhibits demand time and attention to truly appreciate.

One crucial tip: ensure you're well-nourished before embarking on this artistic journey. The museum doesn't offer on-site dining options, and the extensive exploration can be quite energy-consuming.

From the opulent Winter Palace to the awe-inspiring art collections, every corner reveals a piece of human creativity and history. Don't rush through; take your time to absorb the magnificence. The Hermitage is not just a museum; it's a testament to the depth and breadth of human achievement.

Visitors seeking an unforgettable encounter with art and history should prioritize the Hermitage. Plan ahead, indulge in a hearty meal, and be prepared to be transported through centuries of human creativity and ingenuity. This museum is a true marvel, deserving of every moment you invest.

title: State Hermitage Museum

They will also work with small groups to have private tours even at night.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: The Palace complex of the State Hermitage is situated on the banks of Neva river, having amazing Baroque architecture, with wonderful collection of paintings and artefacts. Winter Palace, small Hermitage, Old Hermitage, New Hermitage, General Staff building and Alexander Column are part of complex. Sections of Italian Art, English Art, Russian Art, Flemish Art, French Art etc., Classical antiquity, Treasure Galleries, Numismatics, Jordan stair case, Peter Hall (small throne room, War gallery, Peacock clock, Leonardo da Vinci room, Large Throne room, Gold drawing room, Italian sky light hall, the Crouching Boy of Michelangelo, 19th century B.C. statue etc. are must watch. Needs minimum 4-5 hours to visit main areas of the complex and 6 pm is the closing time of Museum.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: The place is marvellous even if you are not a history lover. Outstanding artwork and historical facts will surely move you. A very large museum infact its the second largest in the world. But I would suggest to buy the ticket with your card from the ticket machine just outside the entry and get inside without any queue. Wish you all a very happy visit.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text:
The second largest museum in the world is spectacular not just for its collection but also for the splendid rooms showcasing the glory days of the Tsardom in Russia. Needless to say, it is usually packed with people but fortunately does not feel stuffy.
You will not be able to see most of the collection in a single day. I suggest visitors to look up the must see exhibits before visiting the museum. It would be a pity if you do not see the Malachite room, Kolyvan vase (biggest and spectacular at 20 tonnes), Da Vinci room and the main staircase.
I entered the museum at 11AM in the morning and left at 1800 in the evening. I didn't have to stand in line for the ticket because I stayed at the official hermitage hotel which has a machine to buy tickets in its lobby. From the long lines that I saw on Saturday morning, I suggest buying a ticket beforehand to gain time and also peace of mind. However, please keep in mind that the tickets purchased online will cost more.
Carry a water bottle with you (the security at the gate let me pass with one water bottle) and take regular breaks with at least one long break for lunch. The cafe food was average and overpriced but is better than staying hungry. I am not sure if you are allowed to carry your own food but who wants to eat food while appreciating art anyways?
The art pieces from the modern era can be found in the new general staff building on the Eastern side of the square. You will get lost many times but consider that a part of the adventure of hermitage.
If you have the time and can afford it, try breaking up the visits in two or three sessions rather than a one day marathon. (I did the latter)

title: State Hermitage Museum
text: The State Hermitage Museum is definitely worth a visit. This world-class museum is absolutely large and houses beautiful antique collections from around the world. The museum building itself is stunning. However, it was a little crowded when I visited. I recommend setting aside at least half a day for your visit.

title: State Hermitage Museum
text:
The Western European Art collection includes European paintings, sculpture, and applied art from the 13th to the 20th centuries. It is displayed, in about 120 rooms, on the first and second floor of the four main buildings. Drawings and prints are displayed in temporary exhibitions.

Egyptian antiquities Since 1940, the Egyptian collection, dating back to 1852 and including the former Castiglione Collection, has occupied a large hall on the ground floor in the eastern part of the Winter Palace. It serves as a passage to the exhibition of Classical Antiquities. A modest collection of the culture of Ancient Mesopotamia, including a number of Assyrian reliefs from Babylon, Dur-Sharrukin and Nimrud, is located in the same part of the building.

Italian Renaissance
The rooms on the first floor of the Old Hermitage were designed by Andrei Stakenschneider in revival styles in between 1851 and 1860, although the design survives only in some of them. They feature works of Italian Renaissance artists, including Giorgione, Titian, Veronese, as well as Benois Madonna and Madonna Litta attributed to Leonardo da Vinci or his school.

The Italian Renaissance galleries continues in the eastern wing of the New Hermitage with paintings, sculpture, majolica and tapestry from Italy of the 15th–16th centuries, including Conestabile Madonna and Madonna with Beardless St. Joseph by Raphael. The gallery known as the Raphael Loggias, designed by Giacomo Quarenghi and painted by Cristopher Unterberger and his workshop in the 1780s as a replication of the loggia in the Apostolic Palace in Rome frescoed by Raphael, runs along the eastern facade.
