title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: I had a truly unpleasant experience. I had high expectations for this famous museum in Spain, so I visited with high expectations. I researched in advance and found out that the museum offered luggage storage. So, I stood in line to check my luggage. I was sure everyone else in front of me was already checking theirs. When it was my turn, I checked to see if luggage storage was free. However, they (one Asian woman in particular) said they could only store my jacket and not my luggage, even though my bag was relatively small. I wonder if it was because I'm an Asian woman that I couldn't check my luggage. I didn't want to cause a scene, so I didn't use the luggage storage service, but it's unfortunate that something like this happened at one of Spain's most renowned museums.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
It was just an average museum, nothing special, certainly not better than the famous art museums in big cities. It was annoying that photography wasn’t allowed; I honestly don’t see the reason for that. It also felt like I was being watched the whole time, which was really uncomfortable. The atmosphere felt quite low-class. On top of that, I’d bought a small painting and had to leave it in the cloakroom, so I couldn’t properly enjoy the museum because I had to go back to the entrance to collect it.

The overall atmosphere felt surprisingly low-class, driven more by rigid control than genuine respect for visitors.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Queue to get in was too big to bother with, so we got a poor coffee round the corner and 3 vouchers to use the toilet. Can't take coffee in but could have drunk them and gone in on the voucher. So we went to the Retiro for sunset instead.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
This was by far the worst museum experience I have had in Europe.

It all started right after buying the ticket. I had to stand in a security line for about 20 minutes. The process was extremely slow and very poorly organized, with no clear flow or guidance.

After entering, I tried to find a restroom. It was not near the entrance and not even clearly marked. It turned out to be on another floor, which I had to walk through almost entirely, without any proper signs. When I finally found it, only two toilet stalls were working. Two stalls in the largest museum in Madrid is simply unacceptable.

Then I went to the cloakroom, only to be told that there was no space left and they refused to take my jacket. I had to carry it with me the entire time, which made the visit uncomfortable and frustrating.

On top of everything else, photography is completely forbidden throughout the entire museum. And if you do not realize it immediately, staff will explain it to you in a very unpleasant and rude manner, even when you calmly say that you did not understand that photography is prohibited in all halls without exception.

Overall, the experience was stressful, poorly managed, and unwelcoming. Instead of enjoying the art, I felt constantly annoyed and uncomfortable. I would not recommend this museum to anyone.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
While this is one of three art museums making up the Madrid "Golden Triangle of Art", along with Thyssen-Bornemisza Museum and  Museo Reina Sofia, I found it a disappointment.
Went at 6pm on Thursday for the free entry which applies for the last two hours (Thesday to Friday 6pm to 8pm).
Museum mainly contains Spanish Masters and is spread out over ~90 rooms.
A officious entry process requiring separate ticket collection, ticket checking and then Security is ridiculous for free entry.
I didn't find any of tge artwork particularly inspiring so I'll just say I'm glad I didn't pay 15€ to enter!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Controversially I'm not giving 5. Compared to the Thyssen the art is second rate. I can't add photos because you can't take photos. The best thing is Goya's Black paintings, which are showstopping. Worst cafe too. No alternative milk and outrageous prices even for a museum cafe.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Full of great exhibits and paintings, including many famous works. Photos aren’t allowed, and some staff members can come across as rude. Not many of the staff speak English, even though it’s a very popular tourist attraction and often overcrowded. The museum is huge and can feel chaotic, making it difficult to navigate without a guide.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Great art but they need to get their ticketing and entry system figured out. Even though I had purchased a ticket for an 11:30 entry. After waiting in three lines (one to get a paper ticket, the second to actually enter the museum, the third to go thru metal deti) I finally got into the museum at 12:25. It's a great museum but I won't return.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Huge and impressive—best for art lovers. At least 5 hours needed to explore properly; with just 2, you’ll be rushing. Crowded and a bit stressful, especially at peak times. Entry is €15, which is fair for what you get. The line was very long at opening, but moved fast (about 10 minutes). Security checks are quick; audio guide pickup takes a few minutes. Free online guide is fine for quick visits, but the €5 version is better for deeper insight.

Fully wheelchair-accessible, with a special guide option. There are rest areas and a cafeteria for breaks—very needed in such a big space. Toilets are as expected: often crowded, but usable. Third-floor toilets are quieter and wheelchair-friendly. No photos allowed. The museum shop is nice but overpriced, and not everything feels worth it.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: HUGE MUSEUM. YOU WILL GET LOST. WORTH IT TO CHECK OUT BUT THE OVERALL WORKS THERE ARE VERY REPETITIVE. STILL GOOD TO VISIT THOUGH. (FYI: YOU’RE NOT ALLOWED TO TAKE PHOTOS INSIDE - THEY ARE VERY STRICT WITH THIS RULE.)

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
I believe the museum management should reconsider the current policy regarding photo and video recording on mobile phones. In many leading museums around the world, photographing artworks for personal use (without flash) is allowed. This helps promote art and generates additional interest in museum collections. In this museum, such photography is not allowed, which feels like an outdated practice compared to international standards.

In addition, today there are many technologies that allow people to take photos discreetly: camera glasses, mini cameras, and other devices. As a result, banning regular phone photography does not really solve the problem of protecting images, but instead creates a situation where ordinary visitors may feel like they are doing something illegal or taking photos “like thieves.”

I would also like to mention the cloakroom situation. Formally it exists, but it is extremely small and does not match the visitor flow. As a result, guests experience inconvenience, especially during colder seasons when outerwear takes up more space.

Overall, the museum makes a strong impression thanks to its collection and the quality of its exhibitions. However, reconsidering these two issues would significantly improve visitor comfort and the overall experience.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
In Europe, writer Honoré de Balzac feared each photo peeled away ghostly layers of the body. Seems like similar believe is held by administration of this museum as photos are not permitted(not just flash photography). On top of it museum café serves nespresso which in my opinion should be illegal for anyone still having taste buds.

Collection of paintings is great.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: It is a lovely, small, enclosed park, but the botanical exhibitions are nothing unusual—a nice place to relax and walk around. There could be more options to sit down, relax, and take in the views. It felt more like a market for various plants than a botanical garden.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: It was good and interesting, but it has nothing to do with Prada, it is quite random I would say but definitely a place you could visit when you are in Milan

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Obviously the art is incredible and you can’t mis the Prado. I’m giving 3 stars because I think it isn’t run very well. First, the entrance is weirdly hard to find and there is poor signage generally. This extends to their no photo policy, which is stupid and unnecessary in 2026, but if you’re going to do it have good signage and don’t have rude guards running around snapping at every other guest in the museum every 3 seconds. Also, their website is slow and terrible.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Personally, I’m not a big fan of cubism — it’s an art style I find difficult to understand.
However, the museum is very rich and worth visiting.

Important: entry is free during the last two hours of the day, but the queue becomes extremely long. I recommend arriving at least thirty minutes before the free-entry time begins

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: This is one of the many museums I've visited in the world, with a rich collection, but unfortunately, photography is not allowed. Many staff members patrol and monitor everyone. If you take a picture, someone will rush over and stop you. I don't understand why we can take pictures freely at the Louvre, where the collection is more valuable. The exhibits at the Musée d'Orsay were more beautiful to me; I even took many photos with the works. At the MoMA Museum of Contemporary Art in New York, staff pulled me to a spot in front of Van Gogh's Starry Night and took my picture. My experience at the Prado Museum was terrible; it was extremely crowded, and the staff treated visitors like thieves, monitoring them. It was very unpleasant.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Undoubtedly, a world class museum. The queue was long on the day we visited (late July), but the staff was very efficient in getting us through the ticket office and through the doors. The downside is that one cannot take photos inside the museum. I expected for them to tell me I could take a few snaps as long as I didn't use a flash; once inside, I noticed a person trying to take a photo and a man running up to him like a traffic warden from a corner shouting, 'no photos!'
For a fee of €15 per visitor, they should be a little flexible about photography, but of course, they wish to cash in on their €20 guide book and other smaller guides to more specific collections.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Uptight museum where you can not take pictures or anything. I think the Louvre in Paris is more chill. If you are a real art enthusiast or a passionate historian, then you should check it out, vast collections. But I would not go back again !

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
We visited Museo del Prado, and it is truly one of the best museums I have ever been to. The collection is extraordinary, especially the masterpieces by Velázquez, Goya, and El Greco. Walking through the galleries feels like moving through centuries of European history. The layout is elegant, the lighting highlights the artworks beautifully, and the overall atmosphere is impressive and inspiring.

The museum is very well organized, and despite being busy, it does not feel chaotic. Everything is clearly labeled, and you can easily spend hours exploring without getting bored. It is absolutely a must visit in Madrid.

Negative feedback: they require you to leave your backpack in the cloakroom, which can be slightly inconvenient. Also, photography is not allowed inside the galleries, so you cannot take pictures of the artworks. While understandable for preservation reasons, it can be disappointing if you like capturing memories.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: 7/10 experience, better to book your ticket in advance to skip the lines especially in weekends days , asked for the audio guide if you like , there is cafe with different brunches you can enjoy some snacks , gift shop

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: A must see in Madrid. The seasonal exhibits are of great quality. Photo and/or video are not permitted which makes the visit more enjoyable, although it should be mentioned more clearly (didn’t really see any visible signs about it). The artwork labels could be a little bigger or the font bolder, other than that, we really enjoyed our visit.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Art museum with lots of artwork, some sculptures. Lots of art are religious theme. No pictures are allowed to take inside the museum. There are guard in every section area to ensure no touching nor photos. Museum and art lovers can easily spend half to a day here. Worth a visit.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Before coming to the museum, I recommend buying tickets in advance to avoid any problems. There are paid guided tours for around 20 euros and other free group tours. The museum is incredible, with a huge number of paintings, all of them Spanish. For an optimal visit, book tickets on their website to avoid queues. Consider adding audiology or guided tours to better appreciate the galleries.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Famous museum to visit but remember no camera photo allowed.  Even in London big famous museum we allow to take picture for memory. Some nice art to see if you really into art anyway.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: I have give 5 stars due to number of art pieces but it is rather overwhelming. Audio guide should be better - not to explain what you see but what is behind the painting and what was artist's motivation. So, audio guide was confusing and not so useful to me. It requires whole day to be visited or to be visited in a more than a single day. Not so suited for children if not on school excursion.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: I would sumarize my experience and also advice for people who wants to visit this muzeum. first of all you cant take any photo which made me lower my rating by one since i travel there from far and bought expensive tickets i wanted to have something for memories. Another downside is that there is usually huge queve for toilet and u will need probably 6 hours to see everything there. Also there is huge control for entrance so dont take anything what you will not need. For the museum itself it has generally huge collection of pictures from velasquez, rubens, goya.etc i was a little bit dissapointed that they didnt have anything from picasso. there is also some statues and old things from pottery or glass, coins. etc. From the positives i would say a lot of beautiful artwork, really nice organised maps and possibility to buy audio tickets and really good restaurant downstairs. Iam also adding few picture which i silently made there.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Incredible museum with an impressive and extensive collection. You need several hours to do it justice! Another fantastic thing is that students get FREE entry.
4 stars because I found staff to be very rude. The no photography rule is not clearly signposted before entering the collection so staff instead are hounding people left right and centre when they inevitably take photos. Given this is an usual rule compared to other art museums I’d suggest staff should mention this when entering. Also the cloakroom was inexplicably not accepting coats and umbrellas despite there being plenty of free space. Given it was a rainy day this meant I (and everyone else in the museum) was left to carry around wet weather gear, coats and umbrellas all day in the museum…

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: It is museum mostly filled with paintings. I thing the best exhibition has Goya. Not very interesting for children though. But it is worth seeing while in Madrid.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
It is one of the biggest museums among others. A lot of collections on paintings, related to Jesus and Christianity.

A slightly down side is the types of collections are not broad enough. But you will still be immersed in many rooms and artifacts that would take your whole day to just glide through.

You cannot take photos instead though.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
It’s a great museum. They house many of art history’s greatest masterpieces, like Bosch’s Garden of Earthly Delights. The singular most popular attraction is Las Meninas by Diego Valezquez.

The museum was very large, almost too large for me. I especially liked the gallery with Goya’s paintings during the dark period, for example Saturn devouring his son.

They did not allow pictures, which is why I took off one star. Nonetheless, I managed to sneak in a few photos.

Overall, great. The statue of Valezquez next to the museum is a great place to take selfies too.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: It's free after 6pm weekday. You should get there about 5pm for the free line. It's a big and 3-floors museo, so it might take 1 hours at least. You shouldn't forget visit the shopping inside of the museo. There is really good something you can buy. In the other hand, you can make friend when you wait for entering :) Lastly, I am not sure if it's best in Madrid, so just popular. Enjoy!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Lovely museum in which you can easily spend four to six hours. Wonderful artworks of some of the greats in their respective fields, for example Brueghel, are on display. The temporary exhibitions are also worth paying a little extra for. The museum is, however, very crowded. My girlfriend does not do well with crowded and busy places so we left after two hours. I would definitely take this into account.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Museum is huge and mostly exhibitions contain paintings, so don't expect other forms of art too much. Tickets are not too expensive for the quality of art; there is an option with free entrance at a specific time. Bad things are security and chaos with rooms.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Proud, massive collection of paintings dedicated especially to Spanish masters. Nice layout, lighting is amazing. Requires some.dedicated time as the number of paintings is huge.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Started queuing at 525pm in the rain, received the ticket at 6.06pm. Then go through security, and optional coat/bag storage. If you’re rushing for time, prioritise the 1st floor (get the brochure and ask counter for advice). Otherwise, I believe it would take up to 3 hrs for the whole museum.

Floor mapping can be confusing, no pictures allowed. Well worth the free visit!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: The most famous, the most visited (more than a million people a year) and the largest museum in Madrid. I recommend buying tickets in advance so you don't spend too much on the entrance to the museum. The museum primarily contains paintings by famous Spanish painters (Velázquez, El Greco, Mannings, Goya, etc.) and international painters (such as Rubens). You need 2-3 hours for the tour. What irritates me the most is the forbidden photography (which is not even the case in the Vatican museums) and I will never be able to understand what is achieved by this.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: The Museo del Prado was nice but didn’t quite blow me away. The art collection is impressive, but the experience was a bit dampened by how crowded it was. I went around midday, and in retrospect, I wish I had gone first thing in the morning to avoid the long line and packed galleries. That said, it’s still a must-visit if you’re in Madrid and interested in classic European art—just be strategic about when you go!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
The museum is huge and you really get everything from the money (15€/person), you are going to need about 4-5 hours if you want to see everything and go slowly through every room, there are 3 floors. It is the kind of museum where you would like to see and admire everything, not just one famous painting or sculpture.
There is a coffee area which is expensive but that is to be expected in a museum or tourist attraction.
It is very wheelchair accessible and they have special tours.

It is very crowded so it is better to buy tickets online in advance, although it is not cheaper, just faster. We went at about 11am and did not wait anything to enter, the staff was very fast and responsive so the queue moved fast.

The only really big drawback is that you cannot take photos. I expected to not be allowed to take photos with the flash on, but not to be completely forbidden. They are very strict about this rule and I dont really understand why.

The souvenir shop is very expensive but that, again, is to be expected. We got postcards for the memory only.

Overall the museum is really worth visiting.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: The Prado Museum is worth visiting, keep in mind that you may need to wait in line for a short period of time prior to entering. It has a large array of mostly classical artworks. I enjoyed seeing it, though a lot is religion based and/or of wealthy individuals. It is still worth the visit, at least once.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: This is a great gallery for understanding the art, history and culture of Madrid. There’s a lot of famous pieces, a lot of the pieces steeped in religion, which makes sense due to Spains history. it will take about 2 hours to see most of it, but after 5/6 it’s free entry and closes at 7 so for those on a budget it’s a good option. It was busy for the free entry but the line moved fast and had enough time to see a fair bit.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: This is a must see for first timers to Madrid. Unlike other museums, you aren't allowed to take photos. Tickets are €15 at the museum door, be careful if purchasing tickets online, as some websites look authentic but are charging way above the cost price. We visited in January, and it was not busy. There is alot to see, so allow plenty of time.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: This is probably a must for most people interested in old art when visiting Madrid. A bit off the natural tourist areas, but nothing that a Bolt ride or a brisk walk can't solve. The queuing is a bit confusing, we stood for a while in one line before being transferred to another entrance. Overall the queues was shortest on the side away form the ticket booth. The museum itself has everything you wish when coming to old masters. Even one of the early Mona Lisas. After a while some of the rooms blur into each other, there are so many paintings with similar motives. After a couple of hours we felt it was enough so we more or less just briefly passed the last rooms. Cafeteria inside full of people. The line to the ladies room inside the entrance requires patience. Better find ladies room on another level of the museum. Be sure to buy the Prado guide, it is almost a necessary complement. And no photography is allowed with or without flash. Sadly this makes the staff more or less a police force than anything else.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: This is the place for old paintings and sculpture and you will like it if that is your thing. We much preferred the Reina Sophia. However, the Prado does have many of the famous Spanish painters including a large number of Goyas. It was a Sunday when we went and it was quite crowded and hot. Worth a visit if you like that sort of art.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: “The Prado Museum is Madrid’s artistic treasure and one of the most important museums in the world. Founded in 1819 under King Ferdinand VII, it showcases iconic works by Velázquez, Goya, El Greco, and Rubens. Designed by Juan de Villanueva, the neoclassical building itself is a masterpiece. Every room reveals centuries of European art and royal history. Admission is around €15, with free entry during late-afternoon hours—an unmissable experience for art lovers visiting Madrid.”

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: 200 years later Goya continues to inspire around the world and is especially relevant these days. Here's a sticker seen on a utility post in Tucson Arizona USA:

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
A beautiful and huge museum full of classic art masterpieces.
The audio guides are good, but booking a tour guide is a better option due the size of the museum.

Will come again whenever back in Madrid.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: A great museum with rich collections. Worth visiting while in Madrid!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: A haven for art lovers, the Prado Museum houses an exceptional collection. Be sure to have enough time to fully appreciate it. It’s best to book your visit in advance and consider taking a guided tour for a richer experience. However, the museum map is also very helpful for navigating the halls. Photography is not permitted and this rule is strictly enforced.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: A magnificent museum, house to famous paintings especially Goya. Absolutely worth the visit, the paintings are beautiful and about three levels of the museum to explore. There's free entry to the museum two hours before closing everyday. Best to be there earlier as the queue gets very long.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: A magnificent, sensational, very rich collection of art by old masters. The Prado is the Prado; there is no other museum like it in the world. It is worth seeing.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: A must see if you are fan of arts. Recommend to buy tickets upfront so you won’t wait on any queues. We went on Sunday and entered immediately. Café is cute and nice for a short refreshment.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: A must see Museum! Their copy of the Mona Lisa is better than the one at the Louvre! And here it is not behind glass so you can see it better!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: A must see smaller museum (compared to the Louvre) filled with masterpieces but El Greco and many other artists. Spend an afternoon here. No photos allowed inside.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: A must visit museum for sure. Had a wonderful time and an audio guide is definitely needed to stay aware of the time especially if you’re limited. Only sad part was you can’t take photos but on the plus that allows you to focus on the artwork and your time at the museum

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: A must visit place! This museum is very impressive and is one of the best art museums I’ve ever been to. It has so many different paintings and a lot of them are incredibly famous and well known. Also there are many sculptures, and I think the oldest one I saw was a huge bust of a horse which dates back to like 500 BC. There is just so much to see in this museum and everything is well planned out. Literal MUST VISIT!!! A key highlight for me was seeing the Prado Mona Lisa, that was just incredible seeing it up close. There’s also lots of bathrooms around the museum and there’s a cafe with a very extensive menu with seating both inside and outside. I bought a fruit cup and it was delicious. Unfortunately photos are strictly forbidden throughout all the exhibitions.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: A must-see for all cultural visitors to Madrid. It is definitely among the top 10 art museums in the world. It is recommended to visit in small portions, 2-3 hours long, over several days.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: A must-see venue if you're visiting Madrid. The collection is vast but well organised. You can see the paintings of great masters like Goya, Caravaggio, Rubens, Titian and Bosch, amongst others.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
A transformational experience. To see
Such greatness - the best humanity has to offer. Velásquez obviously a highlight. Caravaggio took my breath away. Audio guides very helpful. Staff good. Crowded yes, but still space to see the art. Pre buying tickets definitely a pro move but won’t help avoid a bit of a line.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
A very good art museum, the Goya's dark paintings are really great, seeing "Saturn" was great experience.
It's not allowed to take photos inside the museum.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Absolutely beautiful museum, and easy to navigate. The couple of times I've been to Madrid, coming here is a must. They have a fantastic Goya exhibit, with 'Saturn Devouring His Son'. Really worthwhile place to go.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Absolutely impressive.
Seeing all these masterpieces in real life is mind-blowing.
A must-visit museum – truly unforgettable experience.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Absolutely phenomenal, one of the best art galleries in the world, worth the price of admission for the works by Goya, Bruegel and Bosch alone! You should set aside a good few hours if you wish to put a dent in seeing the collection here, there is so much to see, and realistically you probably won't see it all in one visit. Be prepared to queue on entry and for crowds inside, but it's worth it. Photography is not permitted and they enforce this rather strictly.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Amazing experience! The museum is huge. Although the free hours for admission are 6pm-8pm, it’s a massive, lengthy line that can take between 30 minutes and an hour wait time.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Amazing museum! The waiting time is at least 20 min on a weekend just to get in. Worth getting the audio guide. Some amazing paintings and it's really well organised.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: An absolutely stupendous museum for classical pictures and sculptures. The collection of Goya and Velasquez paintings is unsurpassed. There is also many other famous Spanish and European artists, including Ruben’s  El Greco  and other well known luminaries of the art world. Photography is not permitted inside the collection, which is a good thing as it makes you focus on the art itself. I must visit if you’re in Madrid.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: An excellent museum with a vast grounds and numerous works of art. It gets crowded, so it's worth buying tickets in advance. The optimal time to spend there is about 3-4 hours, but it's also worth considering that the museum is visited by many people, so it is better to plan your visit in advance and allocate plenty of time for it

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
An important museum for Madrid and Spain in general. Every capital should have such an art gallery. Big, beautiful, epic and picturesque. And after 18:00 free entry, for this special thanks, I have not seen such in any museum. Also, photography and video shooting are prohibited, this is strictly controlled and you will not be allowed to take anything, but there is a logical reason for this.

Recommended.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Another amazing experience at one of our favourite museum in Europe. Bosch , Goya , Velazquez, el Greco , Ruben …. We had an amazing private tour guide and I highly recommend it!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: As usual, an unforgettable cultural experience. Yet another oportunity to learn and admire beauty.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Astonishing. Breathtaking. This is one of the world's top art museums. You must see. Buy tickets in advance and check on free admission dates and times. Best to plan for a whole day.  Guided tours available and are worth the price. Allow plenty of time for security line, especially if you have a timed tour. Nice cafe inside can provide snacks, drinks or full meal. If you've never been go,

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Awesome place, free from 6pm-8pm however the lines can get really long.
Definitely need longer than 2 hours, it took us roughly 30mins to get from the back of the line to the front to get in. A lot of history in here, do take your time.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Beautiful museum. We skipped the audio tour, but I’d highly recommend it—there’s so much to take in that it can feel overwhelming without guidance. I also learned that two versions of the Mona Lisa were created at the same time, and the earlier version is housed here (something I had no idea about!). It’s fascinating to see how it differs subtly from the version at the Louvre.

No photos are allowed so I will leave you with a picture of the outside of the museum 🙂

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Beautiful! On par with the museums in Paris. The masterpieces were exciting to see in real-life. The museum’s architecture is magnificent.
Downside: no photos allowed inside. I was able to sneak one at the entrance. 🤭

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Beautiful!!! Great collection of the most famous art pieces in the world. Take at least the audio guide for more value. We had the English one and you can choose a series of tours. 1, 2, 3 hours etc. Very handy. First minutes had to get used to the user interface and the mapping of the museum and the rooms but after that it was easy.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Big museum! Must visit! Too many painting to see. 15euro.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Can’t really see everything in the same day! Great collection!
For free tickets queue at least an hour before entrance.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
classical paintings are endless
coudn't even find out the right exit

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: El Prado means The Meadow, based on its location from centuries ago. Houses royal collections, not to be missed. Plan ahead on which masterpieces you want to take in else you'd need to afford 12 hours to see everything. Free admission in the evenings daily, but unless you queue 45-60 mins beforehand, it will take up to an hour to get in. If you're short on time, recommend hiring a licensed guide to take you thru the key pieces, roughly one hour minimum. Well worth it! Enjoy the privilege of viewing these amazing works under one roof!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Goya's "Saturn Devouring His Son." I've always been a huge fan of the story, the painting, and surely the artist. However, I never thought about what it would feel like to be standing 2 meters away from it, especially that it's the original, of course!

Museo del Prado, is one fantastic experience to leave you absolutely awe-inspired for days! With it's 100 rooms and 1800 paintings and works of art, you walk out of the museum as the most emotional version of yourself, and that's an understatement.

I was with a big group and we only got to see 25% of the museum. Thank goodness we got to see the dark phase paintings of Goya. I was able to stand in the middle of that room and zone out from all the voices and chatter around me, to simply think of what it must've been like for Goya to paint such images and visions on the walls of his house.

The statues and sculptures are divine artistic expression. The details, are godly. The fact that not a single piece on display is a replica, but all are originals. I am at a loss of words in describing the experience.

Book that museum ticket. Plan to spend an entire day there. If you are with a group, make sure they're agile and interested. Go in, admire, gaze, breathe in all that history and art. Enjoy every second, and thank me later!

FYI, pictures are not allowed in the museum. I only got to snap these 4 pictures before one of the gallery employees there saw me and instructed me not to. I had to stop then and there, once I learned of this.

text:

It’s amazing that you get to be super close to huge paintings. There was one particular hallway filled with the biggest paintings I’d ever seen.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Madrid is worth visiting for many reasons, but the most important one, in my opinion, is the Prado Museum.

I was genuinely excited about going. We booked our tickets online two weeks in advance—though, honestly, that wasn’t really necessary. Near the entrance, there’s a bust of Francisco Goya, a subtle hint of what’s waiting for you inside. The museum is huge, so you need at least 4 hours just to see the highlights. It has three levels – ground floor, first and second.

To describe everything we saw, and to properly express how amazed I was, I’d probably need to write a whole novel. But instead, let me just give you a quick rundown of what to pay attention to.

In the section with Dutch art, you’ll first find the brilliant Bosch and his masterpieces: The Garden of Earthly Delights, The Adoration of the Magi, and The Haywain. Then come Van der Weyden and the timeless Pieter Bruegel the Elder. Anthony Van Dyck and Rubens complete this group of Dutch masters.

From the German Renaissance, there’s Albrecht Dürer with his famous self-portrait and the powerful paintings of Adam and Eve.

The Italian collection is beautifully represented by Renaissance masters: Fra Angelico, Mantegna, Botticelli, and Raphael with the stunning Holy Family with a Lamb and The Cardinal. Naturally, there’s also Titian, Tintoretto, and my personal favorite—Caravaggio, with David with the Head of Goliath.

And finally, the Spanish painters.

El Greco—real name Domenikos Theotokopoulos. I can’t decide what I like more: his religious works bursting with color, his striking portraits (like The Nobleman with His Hand on His Chest), or the mysterious Fable.

There’s also Jusepe de Ribera and Francisco de Zurbarán, who didn’t quite blow me away—maybe just because I wasn’t that familiar with his work before.

Diego Velázquez is probably the most represented artist in the Prado. I honestly don’t even know what to say—his works speak for themselves. Just look, ask questions, and enjoy. The Crucified Christ, Mars, Aesop, The Buffoon El Primo, and portraits of Philip IV spanning thirty years…

In front of The Surrender of Breda, we saw a group of kids with a teacher giving a lecture about the historical event and Velázquez himself. Beautiful moment.

And the painting that left me speechless: Las Meninas. I’d seen it countless times in reproductions and documentaries and was never particularly moved. I thought I "understood" it. But seeing it in person? A completely different experience. I was blown away—and still am. It’s a masterpiece you simply have to see with your own eyes.
And finally, like the crown jewel of the entire collection – Francisco Goya!

A true genius, an artist ahead of his time. When the Prado Museum opened in 1819, Goya was still alive and actually witnessed three of his paintings being displayed at the grand opening.

Among his masterpieces, you’ll find The Naked Maja, The Execution of the Rebels, The Fight Against the Mamelukes, and his striking self-portrait.

But for me, the biggest impression came from the so-called "dark room" – where his late-period works, known as The Black Paintings, are displayed. Saturn Devouring His Son, Witches’ Sabbath, A Pilgrimage to San Isidro... and The Drowning Dog – what an incredible piece. It feels so modern, like something painted yesterday in a New York studio (and it’s much bigger than I expected!).

To wrap it up: if I ever return to Madrid and had just one day to spend, I wouldn't hesitate—I’d go straight back to the Prado.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Madrid, Prado, Museo Nacional del Prado, is the main Spanish national art museum, located in central Madrid. It houses collections of European art, dating from the 12.th century to the early 20.th century, based on the former Spanish royal collection, and the single best collection of Spanish art. Founded as a museum of paintings and sculpture in: 1819, it also contains important collections of other types of works. The numerous works by: Francisco Goya, Hieronymus Bosch, El Greco, Peter Rubens, Titian, Tintoretto, Rembrandt, Diego Velázquez.
The collection currently comprises around: 8,200 drawings, 7,600 paintings, 4,800 prints, and 1,000 sculptures, in addition to many other works of art and historic documents.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
My husband and I visited The Prado on a Wednesday in late March 2025 during our honeymoon. We initially tried for one of the free evening slots but the line was literally wrapped around the building. Buying tickets online was easy and we added an audio guide. We spent over 3 hours wandering before we left to find dinner.

The audio guide was nice, but we ended up mainly using the app it came with as a reference to ensure we hit the main spots. I visited the Louvre years ago and the Prado is absolutely in the same level.

Be sure to take advantage of the coat check (or lockers), use a bathroom as soon as you see one (some were under reno), and go all the way to the top. The "treasures" attic room was very different from the rest.

Overall, we had fun finding artists we knew and a lot more we didn't. My husband liked to spot the dogs in painting corners, if you have kids or ADHD people to keep entertained.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
The Museo del Prado is an absolute treasure trove, especially if you’re a lover of Baroque art. With masterpieces from Velázquez, Rubens, Caravaggio, and Rembrandt, you could easily spend hours admiring the depth, drama, and emotion of this period.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
The Museo del Prado is one of the most impressive art museums in the world and a true jewel of Madrid. Its vast collection features masterpieces by Velázquez, Goya, El Greco, Rubens, and Titian, making it a dream for art lovers. Highlights like “Las Meninas” and Goya’s “The Third of May 1808” are unforgettable.

The museum’s elegant architecture, calm atmosphere, and beautifully curated galleries make exploring its halls an inspiring experience. Everything is well organized, with friendly staff, helpful guides, and a great café and gift shop.

Located near Retiro Park and Paseo del Prado, it’s part of Madrid’s famous art triangle. A visit to the Prado is a journey through centuries of creativity — absolutely unmissable for anyone visiting Spain.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
The Museo del Prado is, quite simply, one of the greatest museums in Europe and an essential cultural experience in Madrid. Spread across six expansive floors, it houses an extraordinary collection that feels both monumental and deeply human, offering a powerful journey through the artistic and historical development of Spain.

What makes the Prado truly exceptional is how its collection reflects the evolution of Spanish identity. Through masterpieces spanning centuries, the museum traces Spain’s transformation from an Arab-ruled nation to one developing its own distinct culture, power, and artistic voice. This narrative unfolds seamlessly as you move through the galleries, giving context and meaning to each period rather than presenting art in isolation.

The breadth and quality of the collection are remarkable. Iconic works by Velázquez, Goya, El Greco, Zurbarán, and Ribera sit alongside masterpieces from other European centers, creating a rich dialogue between Spain and the wider artistic world. Each room feels thoughtfully curated, allowing visitors to appreciate both the technical brilliance and emotional depth of the works.

Despite its scale, the Prado never feels overwhelming. The layout is intuitive, the lighting superb, and the atmosphere calm and reverent, inviting visitors to linger, reflect, and truly absorb the art. It is a museum that rewards both first-time visitors and seasoned art lovers.

The Museo del Prado is not simply a collection of paintings; it is a visual history of a nation—its struggles, triumphs, and soul. Profound, moving, and endlessly fascinating, it stands confidently among Europe’s very finest museums.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: The Museo Nacional del Prado is one of those places that truly earns its reputation — a vast, magnificent collection that feels like walking through the pages of art history. From Velázquez and Goya to Titian and Rubens, every gallery reveals another masterpiece. That said, it can definitely feel overwhelming, with rooms branching into more rooms and a layout that’s easy to get lost in — but that’s part of its charm and grandeur. We visited on Spain’s National Day when entry was free, and while the queue took about 30 minutes, it moved steadily. Even on regular free-admission days, expect a wait, so plan accordingly. Once inside, the experience is nothing short of breathtaking — an essential stop for anyone visiting Madrid, even if it takes a little patience to get in. Truly a world class museum.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: The Prado hit me harder than I expected, not just the scale but the emotion. You wander from Velázquez to Goya and realize you are walking through centuries of human feeling not just art. The light, the silence, the faces in the paintings - it is all strangely alive. You don’t leave smarter, you leave quieter and somehow more awake.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: The Prado is a must-see in Madrid! It houses an unparalleled collection of European masterpieces, including Velázquez’s Las Meninas and Goya’s Black Paintings. The sheer history and artistic beauty within its walls are breathtaking. A true cultural landmark no tourist should miss.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
The Prado is an incredible museum and a true highlight of Madrid. We hired a professional guide, which made the experience even better, there’s just so much to see that having context really brought the art to life.

The Goya collection was a standout for me, especially his later works when his mind and style shifted into darker, more haunting territory. It was fascinating and unforgettable.

One thing to note: photos aren’t allowed inside unless the walls are red, which was a bit of a bummer since you want to capture the experience, but it didn’t take away from how much I loved it.

Overall, the Prado is world-class and absolutely worth a visit.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: The Prado is an overwhelming place, so don't try to be a hero and see everything in one visit. Definitely buy tickets online beforehand because the physical line is brutal. I honestly recommend avoiding the free entry hours in the evening; you spend half the time queuing just to get shoved through crowded rooms. Make sure to find Goya’s Black Paintings on the ground floor, they stick with you way longer than the royal portraits upstairs.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: The Prado is my favorite museum in Madrid because of their well honed organization of tours, the museum layouts all of it. They are 5he oldest and that means they know how to treat the public. So many beautiful rooms, nooks and art to see. Go around 3 pm to see the Garden of Earthly Delights and then move on to the vast collection of some of the best art in the world. Incredible. Photos are not allowed so it allows you to just enjoy and absorb the art around you.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
The Prado is undeniably one of the greatest art museums in the world. The sheer collection of iconic masterpieces is breathtaking. Once you are inside, the atmosphere is surprisingly relaxing; the layout is easy to navigate, and it doesn't feel overwhelming despite the size of the collection.
Crucial Tips:
BUY TICKETS ONLINE: I cannot stress this enough. The line to buy tickets at the door was over an hour long. If you book ahead, you walk right in.
Get the Audio Guide: This is key. To truly appreciate the details of the paintings and sculptures, you need the context. Whether you get the audio device or join a guided tour, having that expert insight completely changes the experience.
Art Highlights:
"Las Meninas" by Velázquez: This was my absolute favorite. Seeing it in person is a different experience entirely—the scale, the perspective, and the way the figures seem to look back at you is mesmerizing.
Bosch’s "The Garden of Earthly Delights": (Added based on popular reviews) Make sure to stop by this triptych. The level of minute, bizarre detail is fascinating and you could stare at it for 20 minutes and still find new things.
Goya’s "Black Paintings": (Added based on popular reviews) These offer a dark, intense contrast to the rest of the museum and are a powerful emotional experience.
Final thought:
If you love art, this is a non-negotiable stop in Madrid. Just do yourself a favor: skip the queue by booking online and get the audio guide so you don't miss the hidden stories behind the canvas.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
The Prado Museum – A Must-Visit in Madrid!

If you’re in Madrid, the Prado Museum is one of the places you must visit. It houses a unique collection of masterpieces, including works by Velázquez, Goya, Rubens, Bosch, and other great artists. The atmosphere is incredible, and the exhibition is truly impressive in scale and historical significance.

💡 Even Kids Will Find It Interesting!
The museum is a great place to introduce children to art and history. We recommend taking a guided tour to make the visit even more engaging and educational.

🎟 Tip: It’s best to buy tickets in advance to avoid long queues.

The only downside – it can be very crowded, especially during peak tourist seasons. However, this doesn’t take away from the experience of enjoying world-class art.

😄 Bonus: If you’re looking for Picasso, be ready for a surprise – there’s only one of his paintings here!

Highly recommended for everyone!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
The Prado Museum in Madrid is truly one of the best I have visited. It has countless masterpieces, and the one that impressed me the most was The Garden of Earthly Delights by Hieronymus Bosch, absolutely breathtaking to see in person. I highly recommend going early in the morning, around 9.30 before it opens, and on weekdays to avoid the biggest crowds. Buying your ticket in advance also helps, though some categories of visitors can get in for free.

Plan to spend at least three hours here because the collection is vast and full of treasures. I will definitely go back, it is an unforgettable experience. Fun fact: the museum was founded in 1819 and today it houses more than 8000 paintings, making it one of the greatest art museums in the world.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: The Prado Museum is a masterpiece itself, housing an extraordinary collection of European art. Velázquez, Goya, and El Greco define its soul. Walking its galleries feels like traveling through centuries of Spanish history, power, faith, and human emotion, rendered with unmatched technical brilliance that still resonates deeply today with visitors. But no photo in anyway.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: The Prado! Easy to walk and enjoy. Use the gree audio guides to experience the nuisance of each piece.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: This is the best museum I’ve ever been to. Easy in and out, do purchase tickets prior to entry. No long lines either. Every room was pristine and showcased all the art. Very impressive. Don’t miss this. So elegant. Cafe shops are great, with excellent selections. Don’t miss the gift shop, has so much to choose from in the way of gifts, books, magnets, etc.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
This museum has a wide range of Spanish Art Collections.

It's quite big with basement and multiple floors if you want to explore everything with details I'm pretty sure 5-6 hour's will be less.

Inside of museum it has cafeteria. It's better if you can buy tickets online rather waiting in lengthy line's.

It's the best collection of Goya. For exploring it you'll get a free guide as well.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: This Museum in just one word- A-M-A-Z-I-N-G.  I can't describe how gorgeous this museum is. I spent about 3 hours and it wasn’t enough to finish it all. The paintings are so real, the facial expressions, the bodies, the colors, everything on the paintings are majestic. You will also find beautiful sculptures with so much history. This museum has pieces of art more than 300 years old, it is unbelievable. You can easily transport yourself to other times by admiring these enormous paintings covering the walls from floor to ceiling. You must experience a visit to the beautiful "Museo National del Prado" Una belleza 🇪🇦

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
This museum is a must visit when in madrid. It offers a vast range of well known artists such as Titian, Bosch, Rubens, Rafael, Tintoretto. Don't be fooled by its appearance the museum is huge and you'll need several hours to fully experience it. All forms of artist are present from bronze statues to tapestries and of course rooms upon rooms of paintings. They have a strict no camera policy which is enforced in the majority of areas. Cafés are dotted throughout.

FOOD I sampled a courtyard cafe serving champagne and salads, both reasonably priced and delicious.

BAGS Anything A4 sized onwards is expected to be left in the free storage area.

TICKETS I would recommend buying in advance you'll save money but more importantly won't need to queue for 20-60 minutes. If you get there before 10am, the queues are less. Free entry is available for the last two hours of opening. Or for selected people like students, free. For private viewings if you have a group of 12 or more and want to pay €50 you can enjoy the musume preopening for 1 hour.

NOISE Noise levels are as expected. If sensitive to this ear plugs would be an advantage.

WHEELCHAIR FRIENDLY absolutely. You can even loan one. Many displays have induction loops fitted. There are lifts and ramps throughout.

ENTRANCE Head for the botanical gardens enterance. Opposite this is a large building (not that entrance). Make you way round to the back, this is where the ticket office is located. Prebooked tickets may need to be collected. The main entrance is shown in the picture below. You need to either go past the ticket booths or come down the side steps, opposite the church. On the left side of the building there's a small entrance too but this is for members only.

Enjoy!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
This museum was an unforgettable surprise. I mean for sure if you read the reviews and online opinions, the expectations must be very high, but at whatever level they were for us, they were surpassed. A few things of note:
*they really are free between 6 and 8PM every day. This may or may not be a good thing for you - if you'd like to have some space and avoid the stampede, it's probably best to avoid those times. However, in February, there was plenty of space to admire all the works of art
* there are many paintings, mostly renaissance. I'm no art critic, so I'll let you discover for yourself what and if you like
* there are high quality temporary exhibits
* top floor there is an exhibit of some gorgeous pieces of pottery, the Dauphin's collection. If you feel you had your fill of lights, colours and shadows, this is the floor for you. I liked the diversity and the exquisite workmanship

So overall, recommended. We visited twice, after the first day we were tired, but felt we left too much on the table. And we'll go again.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: This vast museum houses an impressive number of masterpieces, boasting extensive collections of art dating back to the 16th and 17th centuries. A guided tour is highly recommended to fully appreciate the depth and detail.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Truly, one of the great galleries of the world. Allow a minimum of three hours to visit, and this is a quickly as you could possibly do it, and that's only giving you a brief look. Be there for the opening, it's advisable to buy your tickets in advance, but tickets are available on the day.... DO NOT BUY TICKETS FROM A SECONDARY WEBSITE, YOU DON'T NEED TO.

There are a number of cafes within the museum, so take a break and a deep breath. If you are wondering about a visit, just come, you won't regret it.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Unbelievably diverse collection of the masters as well as less well known talents from across the world.  One day is too little to take all in!!!  And, don't even think of taking pictures.  They are serious about enforcing the rules.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Very big and beautiful museum. If you are interested in classical art, very recommended to go to this Museum! Its a very big Museum so usually it takes around 3-4 hours to check everything!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Very big museum, I gave up halfway, if you walk slow and look at everything it will take u 2-3 hours.
There is a gift shop the prices are a bit high but very cute stuff.
YOU CAN’T TAKE PICTURES IN THERE!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Very nice museum in Madrid.  Has some amazing pieces including Goya and Caravaggio.  A must see if you are visiting Madrid.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Very nice museum. At the end of the day you can enter for free. Just a tip!
Nice paintings!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Visited this place with guided tour from Viator. Had an amazing experience. Tour guide explained beautifully about spanish history along with paintings. I must recommend to have tour guide if you are art lover. (I am not the one and still enjoyed) kudos to those painters who have contributed to this work.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Visiting the Prado Museum in Madrid had been on my list for many years, and finally experiencing it in person was everything I hoped for and more! The galleries showcase an extraordinary collection of masterpieces, Velázquez, Goya, El Greco, and Rubens just to mention few. Works that define European art history and are presented with a real sense of grandeur.

At the heart of the collection is Velázquez' Las Meninas one of the most celebrated paintings in Western art. Equally compelling is the gallery of Goya's Black Paintings. I found it incredible that Goya in his depressed state painted directly on the walls of his home in the final years of his life and how they were transported. I was so thankful to have a very knowledgeable tour guide with me. Without  one I would have missed out on the history which is so much of what makes the Prado museum one of the world's great cultural institutions.

One important tip: buy tickets in advance. The lines can be long, and advance purchase not only guarantees entry but also saves valuable time. Ticket prices are reasonable given the rarity and importance of the collection. There is also a bag check at the entrance so its best to not bring any large backpacks or purses.

The museum enforces a strict no cameras or photography rule, which ensures that visitors are truly present with the art rather than distracted by screens. This policy helps maintain an atmosphere of quiet focus, allowing the works to speak for themselves. They do however allow photos in the lobby.

The museum store offers a wide range of art books, postcards, and gifts. There is also a cafe which is a great place to sit and unwind with a coffee and light snack.

If you are anywhere near Madrid and appreciate art -go!!!!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: We bought our tickets in advance and walked in without issue 15 minutes before our timed entry on Sunday morning.  Photography is not allowed so I'll just post exterior photos. The collection is fantastic with excellent explanations of each piece and information about the artists. We did not do the audio guide or a tour. We spent 4 hours in the museum and saw about 80% of the collection. The highlight was the Dauphin's treasure exhibition.  Don't miss it!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: We visited during the free admission hours in the evening. The line was long, but it was absolutely worth it. The museum’s collection of medieval oil paintings is unparalleled in scale—among the finest in the world—with many works truly worth seeing. If the collection included more modern art, the museum would be even more perfect.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: We visited the Prado on a day with a manageable queue, which was a relief since we had someone with mobility issues in our group. While photography is not allowed inside, we found ourselves fully immersed in the stunning artwork and atmosphere, making photos feel unnecessary. The museum's accessibility features, including seating areas and clear signage, added to our experience. We also appreciated the on-site café and gift shop, which offered a range of affordable options. Love the Prado.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Went on a Saturday afternoon. Queued and got tickets on the door much cheaper less than half price. Don't know if this was a one off offer or a normal option. This is more of an Art gallery with over 100 rooms.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Went on a Sunday, at 5pm, so I could get in for free. There was a long line but it moved quickly, in 20min I was inside!
Amazing collection of artworks, incredible things really!
I was there for 2 hours but I felt like it wasn't enough... Maybe 3/3h30m would be ideal.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text:
Went on a weekday in February. Didn't buy tickets in advance. No wait to get in.
Very large museum with a huge collection. You can spend many hours here. If you purchase the audio guide, you'll get a device with a map to help you navigate. The ear piece they provided with the audio guide device loops around one ear. It isn't super comfortable, and falls off easy. So, I recommend bringing your own wired ones, and see if it works (I didn't have one to try though).
A lot of student groups were visiting at the same time as me. They gather in front of certain art works making it hard for me to get close.
Also, the museum doesn't allow any photos of the artworks or even the description of the artworks. I took a few before I was told not to, and will keep those for personal memory only. (Attaching a few photos of the surrounding buildings, as well as one of a sculpture room window)

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: What can I say except it being worth every penny?! I like to take my time in museums, but this one was something else. I believe I may have been in there for 6 hours. Kept getting lost admiring all of the amazing art! Seeing Goya's Black Paintings was one of many highlights. Just remember, no photos! I definitely didn't get a telling off...

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: What might have been an everyday activity at the time, like Maecenas in the Roman Empire thousands of years ago, was merely a support activity for artists by the Spanish royal family, would one day become a cultural black hole that attracts tourists from all over the world.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Wonderful museum.  There is so much to take in that I recommend getting the audio  guide and following one of the recommended tours.  No photography is allowed- so you can admire the art without worrying about getting that great picture and fighting through crowds of selfy takers!

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Worth visiting. I visited in their free entry hours. It's very big and quite fascinating. I love the art from the original painters or you can say legends. You can find the artwork and painting from all the painters. The description about all the art will make you feel that your visiting that era/ generation. It's well managed. I will recommend you take out atleast 2 hours to visit this beautiful museum.

title: Museo Nacional del Prado
text: Wow one of the best in the world! In a way I love it even better than louvre probably because of my love for Goya, especially his black paintings. I hope to come back and study him and Bosch more

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
Given very positive reviews we came with expectations that were not fullfilled.
While the museum's collection is indeed sizeable, the exhibition lacks connections, story or shared idea allowing the visitor to understand and learn.
If the visitor is taken by seeing samurai blades and shogun's armors, they will be satisfied.
But if coming with a desire to learn key aspects of Japan's history, this museum will disappoint.
I think the museum from this century should have the ambition to give more.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text: A good place to visit when it's hot outside. Most items are Buddhist related pieces of artifacts. The kimono section was impressive.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
Review specific to the Hello Kitty Exhibit -

I arrived at around 9:45 AM and waited for two hours to get in. The exhibit itself was quite short, focusing on different types of merchandise, collaborations, and artworks featuring Hello Kitty.
However, it didn't cover the history or origins, which I felt would have made it much more interesting.

There was another 30-minute queue for the souvenir shop, where many items were already sold out before noon. Each shopper was limited to 10 minutes in the store, which was packed like sardines, with everyone pushing and jostling.

Overall, I'm not sure if this exhibit is truly worth the time unless you have plenty of free time on your hands.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
The exhibits were mostly artifacts with the 2D art mainly being scroll art. This is not inherently an issue since that was the most prevalent art form of the time periods covered, but I am more into paintings so it was less interesting to me.
BTW: If you are looking for Japanese paintings, I found the Tokyo MoMa to be excellent with art ranging from last 1800s to present day

title: Tokyo National Museum
text: A good way to see some history of Tokyo art.  Though it’s partially closed so not as good as it could be.  There are stamps, a gift shop, and lockers to make the trip easier.   One room with interactive technology that’s good for children.  The Asian galleries is also good.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text: A spacious museum. Loved the decors & the building for the Hello Kitty Exhibit looks really nice. We went on a weekend for the Hello Kitty Exhibit. We were shocked on how crowded it was. We queued for about 40 to 50 minutes  before being able to enter the event building. The lining up process was in order but once inside it was very disorganized. Despite the chaos, still enjoyed the exhibit to celebrate Hello Kitty. ❤

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
Admission ticket was quite pricey, although the was quite big with lots of places to roam around… my friend and I went at around 3:00 pm and I’d say it’s perfect since there aren’t much people in the area - perfect for taking nice photos and just to immerse yourself on the art that’s displayed.

Would probably visit again to see one more time, although I’d have to say that the entrance fee is again, expensive.

P.S. I’d like to say that the guard who was tall and wearing a black trench coat manning the traditional gate near Pic #2 around 4 pm yesterday is ikemen and lol, if ever you are reading this, please message or contact me 😆. I’d love to know you more!

title: Tokyo National Museum
text: Almost more than a day's worth of exhibits. Recommend going on a day with a later closing time. Some information was really hard to read with fading print. Ç

title: Tokyo National Museum
text: First thing you will see when entering the museum is that Japanese people love to visit museum. Very crowded and at some displays you have to line up to see them closely. However, it’s worth to visit.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
Great place to visit on a rainy day in Tokyo. There's so much history to view. The building itself is showing its age a bit, though. Its not as nice as the NYC Met or the Smithsonian. However the pieces within are fascinating if you're at all interested in Japanese history.

Be sure to buy tickets in advance. They have storage lockers to hold your backpack while you tour the museum. Be aware that some displays do not permit photos.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
I came to the Ueno area for a business trip and decided to stop by the Tokyo National Museum.
As always, it’s incredibly spacious with an impressive collection — there’s no way to see everything in just half a day.

If you really want to take your time and see all the exhibits, one full day probably isn’t enough. I’d love to come back again when I have more time.

I didn’t know this before, but some of the exhibits allow photography now, so I took a few pictures as souvenirs.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text: I enjoyed my time at the museum but given it was a holiday it was very busy. I was a bit surprised there was so much staff at the entry way but not throughout the main exhibits where there were crowds of folks struggling to herd into one room and then the other. It’s a historical site with lots to see so I patiently made me way through and even grabbed some noodles afterwards. Go with plenty of time and be aware it’s ¥1k per person typically which I feel is very reasonable. I spent four hours exploring and loved the hidden treasures and tea garden exhibits most. It’s a very well located museum near the JR by five minute walk so perfect for families who may be going to the art museum, zoo, or science museum as they’re all overlooking the same park. Be aware stamps are in the main hall and it’s a bit tedious with longer lines if you do decide to fill up your book!

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
If you’re interested in Japanese History the National Museum is full of information, artefacts and history to peek your interest.
The kids did get a little bored after a couple of hours, but it was a great option for a rainy Tokyo day. Just take note: On a rainy day it may take you a while to get in because the lines are HUGE!

title: Tokyo National Museum
text: It was good if you like musuems. They also sell beer and food. Also have free lockers. I forgot my top hat in one and when I came back it was labeled in a lost and found for me at the front desk. It was 1000 yen to get in. Less if your 70 years old, unfortunately my dad was 69 and 320 days.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text: It's a fairly small museum if you came expecting something like the Met or the British Museum. Contains art and artifacts from Japan and Asia.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text: It’s a very nice and well curated museum. However, it’s a “serious” museum as in that it doesn’t have anything for children.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text: Museum is nice, but it just seems to me there is not so much interesting items. But it shows interesing items from japan history.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text: Must visit place for get the awareness about tech and cultural history of Japan. There were limited items and comparing to other music this was quite small but important items were there. It was not crowded even in the weekend and ticket price was reasonable.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
Note: there are certain objects that are photos prohibited which has a “no photos” sign, so you should definitely check before taking one.

About the museum, just like every others this is where many national precious objects are displayed, but I do not think it worth the visit if you haven’t already been to other famous attractions around the city. There is one activity you could do in the left room of the souvenir shop, where you can use stamps to create an image.

The site comprises of a bunch of other museums so if you are interested in visiting these, you can spend the day wandering between them, but I believe tickets for each museums are bought individually .

This place is wheelchair friendly.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text: Really nice museum if you have a tight schedule. 2 hours should be enough to go through all exhibitions in the main building and give you a decent understanding of different eras in the history of Japan.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text: Solid overall not very large Museum I think one of the best things that this place offers is the interactive systems that allows for audio touring made for a lovely visit my only downside is a lot of the exhibits are closed should have checked I guess before I came but all in all educational

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
The Tokyo National Museum is one of the most impressive cultural institutions in Japan, and my visit there was both enjoyable and educational. The museum houses an incredible collection of artifacts, artworks, and historical pieces that tell the story of Japan’s rich heritage while also offering glimpses into broader Asian cultures. The highlight for me was the Japanese gallery, where beautifully preserved samurai armor, swords, and ukiyo-e prints were displayed with care. Each exhibit felt thoughtfully curated, and the information provided was detailed enough to spark curiosity without being overwhelming.

That said, the museum is quite large and the layout can feel a bit intimidating for first-time visitors. It is easy to spend hours wandering without covering everything, and I found myself wishing for clearer directional signage or suggested routes to help navigate. The lighting in some sections could also be improved to make the details of delicate objects more visible.

Despite those minor drawbacks, the museum is absolutely worth visiting. It offers a rare chance to connect deeply with Japanese history and culture in one place. Whether you are a history enthusiast or just curious about Japan, this museum delivers an enriching experience that lingers long after the visit.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text: The Tokyo National Museum, located in Ueno Park, features several impressive buildings showcasing a wide variety of exhibits. You’ll find clothing and textiles, ceramics, sculptures, and much more. However, if you’re not particularly interested in Japanese history and culture, parts of the museum might feel a bit less engaging. That said, be sure not to miss the beautiful garden behind the main building—it’s definitely worth a visit.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
This is the largest museum in Japan, and it truly lives up to its reputation with fascinating exhibitions. We visited on a Sunday, which was lively because there was also a fair with shops and food stalls. However, Sundays are very crowded, so moving around the museum can feel slow—if you prefer a calmer experience, I’d recommend visiting on a weekday.

What I especially liked about this museum is that it doesn’t just display art pieces, but also explains how they’re made and preserved. You can learn about restoration techniques, the making of traditional Japanese paper, the design of rooftop decorations, and more. It’s a unique approach that sets it apart from other museums.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
This was an awesome experience. Stood in line to purchase tickets, but it moved quickly.
There are 3 buildings, with the main shop
In the middle one. The artwork was beautiful and I was able to view artifacts from the 1at occupants of Japan. It is definintly worth a visit!

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
Very beautiful museum, lots to see and appreciate. Plenty fo space for walking and the use of lighting was exceptional. The temporary anime exhibit was particularly striking.

But, what I really wanted out of a museum with this title is to learn the history of Tokyo or even Japan as a whole. You do get pieces of that, but really what this feels like is an art gallery where each piece has a very brief description of its historical context. There's really just not enough to dig your teeth into to where you leave here feeling like you learned something new.

That being said, for the same reason I think it's a great museum for children, are completely uninitiated with Japanese culture, or those who don't like reading signs for four hours. This museum still gets my recommendation and is a solid way to spend a few hours in Tokyo!

PS: almost all of the signage was also in English and Korean. Anything that wasn't could easily be translated by your phone.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
Visited Tokyo National Museum after spending time at the science museum and art gallery, so unfortunately had limited time to explore properly. I purchased tickets for both the "Shin Japonism" video exhibition and the ukiyo-e display. The Shin Japonism video was frankly disappointing - extremely short, overly simplistic, and definitely not worth the additional fee. Since this is a temporary special exhibition that will rotate out, I'd strongly advise skipping it and saving your money for the permanent collections instead.

The museum itself is impressively vast, with different buildings housing various collections - the ukiyo-e exhibition runs in the Hyokeikan building while the treasure displays are in the sleek, modern Horyuji building. You'd easily need a full day to properly appreciate everything this complex has to offer. The permanent collections showcase Japan's cultural heritage beautifully, though as a foreign visitor, I found myself questioning whether some sections would resonate as deeply without more cultural context. Plan accordingly - this isn't a quick visit, and focus your budget on the core collections rather than potentially underwhelming special exhibitions.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
We went to 2 special exhibits and the general building. The first special exhibit, Ukioy-e-collaboration, was EXCEPTIONAL both in content and design/management. It was not crowded and the layout of the art works was such that you could see the works without problems.

The second special exhibit was about Tsutaya Jūzaburō:  Creative Visionary of Edo. This exhibit was a big disappointment. It was so crowded that we could not enjoy the works. There were long lines to see many works.

The final part of the exhibit was less crowded and more enjoyable.

We visited the first floor of the general Building and found it much less crowded. The archeological displays were good.

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
Well worth the visit and modest entry fee (which is valid for the whole day). The main building is dedicated to Japanese art and culture. An annex covers archaeology and history, while the modern Asian building included Buddhist sculpture, Silk road and some interesting Egyptian statues and an opened sarcophagus and mummy. There are gardens and a small pond at the back. There was a very interesting nutsuke display when we visited - 1st floor main building, at the top of the main stairs.
Lots to see, allow 2-3 hours for a quick whizz round, or at least a day to explore all the galleries properly (come and go with a date stamped ticket).
Sadly several of the really famous paintings are not on show for some reason... Hence 4-stars only.
Tickets can be bought online to save queueing.

January 2026

title: Tokyo National Museum
text:
Went here during Hello Kitty Exhibition on a weekend. Museum has good aesthetic inside and out. It's highly accessible via Ueno Park and Ueno station. There were lots of personnel to assist visitors for directions and inquiries (even in English).

Only con I think was the long line towards the gift shop at the end of exhibit. text:
