title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: The museum is beautiful, a little overwhelming to navigate, and was very crowded on the Monday morning we went. Seeing Starry Night, the Picasso room, and the Salvador Dali paintings in person was incredible, put the pushing and shoving to see them felt like it could have been controlled by the presence of a staff member at those pieces.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Listen. This is one of the greatest art museums in the world. Name a modern painting you like and it is probsbly here. Masterpiece after masterpiece with stunning special exhibits and incredible surprises of works by artists you dont know, early works by artists you do, and just spectacular curation throughout. The museum is so good it has fallen victim to itself. It is always packed to the gills. You sometimes have to physically push your way through to see what you want to see. The masses of people become exhausting and if the garden is closed, the museum does not have any thoughtfully designed spaces to stop and rest. I go to dozens of museums every year and this is the only one I ever go to and get museum fatigue. I keep going back becasue the art and the museum is fantastic but I always leave worn out, not refreshed like a museum should leave you.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Very Nice museum, Amazing Art colection, need to be patient for Security check and  always Crowded. 5 story building and not all Galleries Open. Overall rating 4 Star.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Beautiful museum. Got tickets ahead of time to avoid long lines. There is a security check upon entrance. Was on a Friday afternoon and extremely crowded. Was a bit disappointed because it was hard and almost impossible to see some of the more popular pieces of art up close because of the crowds. Huge gift shop that was also packed with long lines. If I were ever to go again I think I would try earlier in the day.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
Really enjoyed the museum
Had some great exhibits. Got to see the OG starry night painting. So much texture on that painting when you see images you assume it's flat, but live and in person it's incredible.

Had alot of different mediums on display.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
The Museum of Modern Art is one of the best art museums in the world, offering an outstanding collection that blends iconic masterpieces with innovative contemporary work. From Van Gogh’s Starry Night to pieces by Picasso, Warhol, and Monet, the exhibits are thoughtfully curated and beautifully displayed.

The galleries are spacious, easy to navigate, and consistently well-organized, making the experience enjoyable even during busy hours. The museum also features engaging rotating exhibitions, an excellent design store, and a peaceful outdoor sculpture garden that’s great for a break.

Whether you’re an art lover or just exploring New York City, MoMA delivers a memorable, world-class museum experience.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: The Museum has six floors. It’s convenient that there are elevators and escalators between all floors, and benches to sit and rest. The exhibitions are interesting and there are many rooms, but the navigation is confusing. It’s easy to get lost and hard to tell where you’ve already been. We entered some rooms more than once and weren’t always sure which direction to go. Clearer signage would make the visit much more comfortable.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: The MoMA had an impressive variety of modern art ranging from Starry Night to Andrew Warhal and everything between. Some of the flow of it made it a bit difficult to avoid missing any halls so make sure to grab a map.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
I was really impressed by MOMA. I'm not a modern/contemporary art type of person, but the quality is so high I was sold.
The 1880-1940s section was perfection for me. I didn't expect so much of this earlier work, and I was really pleased. The giant Monets from his later period were a sight to behold.
The rest of the gallery was very good as well, I was impressed by the amount of truly iconic pieces they have on display.
My only criticism is that it was far too busy. Quite uncomfortable at some points.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: While this museum is a classic destination in NYC with constantly changing exhibitions, it may not appeal to everyone due to its sometimes controversial art. Nevertheless, the museum itself is impressive, featuring a range of both famous and emerging artists. It's easy to navigate, and you don't need to spend a long time here. Ticket prices can vary, but purchasing them in advance through third-party websites like Booking.com can be advantageous. Overall, it’s a must-visit for anyone coming to NYC for the first time.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
I recommend starting at the top floor and working your way down. The top floor features some of the most iconic pieces, including works by Van Gogh, Monet, and Salvador Dalí. As you continue downward, you’ll come across pop art by artists like Andy Warhol.

Further down, things get a bit more experimental, with some installations that may leave you wondering, “Is this really art?” — including strange humming sounds and a video of an ant accompanied by loud clicking noises. It might not be for everyone and could be a bit off-putting for more traditional art lovers.

We also visited the café on the terrace, which was a great experience. Definitely worth checking out.

Overall you’ll leave with some amazing photos and will certainly be a fun activity for the whole day!

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
Maybe I have not enough convictions about modern art. Very little I find enthusiastic.

Don’t get me wrong I like Moma especially the 5th floor with the 1860+ paintings exhibits : Picasso, Toulouse Laitrec, Magritte, Gauguin, Dali etc. What a fantastic collection

Those make me thrills and a big envy to stay longer. And I believe I am not Ali e. Indeed this 5th floor is crowded while other floors are a bit of a desert. Wonder why

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: The facilities and systems are impressive. However, there are so many people entering at once that it’s difficult to tell whether you’re viewing an exhibit or just walking through a busy market. For highly popular works, it might make sense to set up a queueing system.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Loved how beautifully every artist was displayed. Was super busy and general bathrooms were difficult to access.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: some of the recent modern art, with digital medium or with a few stripes on a canvas etc., is impossible for me to understand, even the explanations seem superfluous. Modern art by Picasso on the principle of cubism or paintings by Van Gogh are beautiful and convey meaning at the same time and these are worth your time. a request to tourists- to not stand very close to the paintings, with so many people its not possible for museum staff to monitor that everybody respects the distance from paintings, specially when its super crowded.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Moma is truly an impressive museum. We had a blast - although for us, the six(changing exhibitions) and fifth floor were really the only interest. Amazing paintings, affordable admission fee

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: The collection is first-rate, including the world-famous Starry Night, and the flexible exhibition spaces on the 2F and 3F are also good. However, there is indeed a bit of a lack of guidance, and the items in the museum shop are somewhat more expensive compared with those at the MET

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: MoMA is always a treat and a weekday visit is absolutely the move — in within 10 minutes and enough breathing room to actually enjoy the art. The collection is iconic; seeing works by Picasso, Warhol, and Monet in person never gets old. The building itself is stunning too. Pricey admission but a world-class experience all around!

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
A good museum to visit if you have a discount for a ticket!
Wardrobe services could be improved as two people to operate such a big museum is not enough.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
Loved it. A great day out and I'd visit again. Extensive art journey, I understand the popularity, especially because of the location, making this a worthwhile city excursion.
Great gift/souvenir shop and very helpful and welcoming staff 🙂

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
One of my favorite museums in the world and I know many. I come here every 2 years aprox. This time I missed a lot of works that had been removed. I loved the Andy Warhol collection and now they only had a few cans of campbells. I’m chilean so I wanted to see the Roberto Marta paintings and they weren’t there anymore. And I’m not a fan but the most of Frida Kahlo works were removed and the only left is one of her family. And the furniture and architecture section were reduced as well. The monets were there but not all of them. A lot of blank spaces, less independent rooms. In the new things I found plastic arts and B&W photography.

In conclusion I left with the feeling of seen a 40% of the MoMa. It took me 1,5 hrs. And in the store most things you could find on Amazon and a few merch of Van Gogh starry night and Monet, but almost no more cool and fun MoMa merch.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
The art collection is, without a doubt, on par with New York's finest, and its quality is outstanding. However, the atmosphere for enjoying it falls short of what you'd find in places like the Musée de l'Orangerie or the Musée d'Orsay in Paris. The behavior of many visitors is a bit too casual and unfortunately disrupts the experience.
It’s a shame to see people using these incredible works of art as mere backdrops for "proof of visit" selfies. They tend to be loud and inconsiderate, and it’s disappointing to watch them see the paintings through their phone screens rather than with their own eyes. It makes it nearly impossible to truly appreciate the wonderful works on display.
On the fifth floor, there is a terrace café with a great view, but the menu is limited and a bit overpriced. The same view can be enjoyed from the large, full-length windows on the fourth floor, so it might be better to save your money and get coffee somewhere else.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Love the art gallery Moma. Helpful and friendly staff. The two top levels felt like no air con circulating and quite hot. Top tip arrive early as already a was a que 10.30am opening time. Definitely a must see while in NYC.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
Such a cool museum, especially as a Van Gogh and Klimt fan!

Wanted to note that though their website says you can check luggage for $6, their policy now is you can’t bring suitcases into the building at all. The security guard was helpful in recommending where we could store them elsewhere to still use our tickets, but would have been helpful to know in advance so we could have more time in the galleries!

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: MoMA is a must-visit in NYC. The art collection is incredible — from Van Gogh’s Starry Night to bold modern pieces that really make you think. The museum is well laid out, easy to explore, and the sculpture garden is a quiet gem in the city. Staff were helpful and everything felt smooth and organized. Even if you’re not an art expert, you’ll find something inspiring here. Totally worth the visit!

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
Visited the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in New York and had an incredible experience! Even if you’re not a huge art person, this place is absolutely worth the visit. The collection is massive and beautifully curated — from famous classics like Van Gogh’s Starry Night to amazing pieces by Picasso, Warhol, Monet, and Dali. Every floor has something unique to offer, from paintings and sculptures to modern design and digital exhibits.

The layout makes it easy to explore at your own pace, and the atmosphere is calm and inspiring. The staff are friendly and helpful if you need directions or recommendations. It’s also great that they have plenty of spots to sit and take it all in.

Whether you’re an art lover, a tourist, or just looking for something memorable to do in the city, MoMA is a must-visit. Give yourself at least a couple of hours — it’s an experience that really sticks with you.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Has become one of my favorite museums. They have an unbelievable collection. I couldn’t believe all the famous works of art and incredible artists they had on display. Starry Night is worth the visit alone.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: One of the best modern art museums in the world. What I love most is that the galleries change every time I visit, so it always feels fresh and exciting. At the same time, they still showcase the main iconic works from famous artists, which makes every visit worth it.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Oh what an experience! Indulged into Art and Architecture! From Stuff toys hanging from the ceiling to blur paintings that look like a picture on your phone to some intriguing videos it is a treat for the artist and collector. Takes a few hours to cover the whole place so don’t miss the cafe on the top floor!

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: What an amazing museum. Highly recommend it. Don't leave without leaving their store! It's full of cool funds and souvenirs.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Amazing, wonderful, breathtaking…be prepared with a clear head and patience to spend time with art. It is one of the best collections of art anywhere. Went on Friday at 3 and it wasn’t really crowded. I am not a fan of overly crowded tourist places (even though I have been to many) but this was not overcrowded. Cafe offered some food and drinks if you needed a break.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: MoMA is without a doubt the best museum in the world! It has everything from the world of art and to spend an entire day there is never enough. Your soul will leave elevated, enlightened and full of ideas at every visit. It’s huge and very well organized and it offers different membership for people who want to enjoy it all year round. The exhibits are constantly changing and the entire 3rd floor covers modern art from the ‘40s to the ‘70s. It’s definitely one of my favorite places in New York.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
Wasn't on the top of my list before the trip, but the MoMA was an incredible experience.  We timed it with an exhibit showcasing the works of Ruth Asawa (her painting of the Japanese internment camp her family was sent to in Arkansas was eye-opening for me, having grown up in Arkansas).

Getting to stare and soak in Van Gogh's "Starry Night" was a bucket-list moment, then strolling among Picasso's, Rothko's, Pollock's, Kahlo, Dali, Hopper, photos by Evans and Stieglitz, and countless other masters of the craft is a visually stunning and humbling way to spend the day.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: This was an excellent experience, I spent hours there, all was incredible. The restaurant was great too and even the store have a lot different options for gifts you can buy. I love this place, it’s nice to have introspection about yourself because you can feel the calm and the good energy there. The best place.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: MoMA is overwhelming in the best possible way. As a visitor, I felt how modern art, design, film, and photography are placed in constant dialogue with each other. The museum does not only show masterpieces – it actively shapes how we understand modern visual culture

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
MoMA is always such an inspiring experience. There were many people tho.. thought it would be pretty empty given it was a weekday afternoon. guess there were many travelers since it's last week of August/Labor Day weekend. I always love seeing the works of Edward Hopper — his art captures quiet, everyday moments so beautifully. Standing in front of Monet’s Water Lilies felt almost meditative, and Picasso’s pieces reminded me of how revolutionary his vision was. One of my personal highlights was seeing Klimt’s work up close, the details and colors are breathtaking in person.

The museum itself is so thoughtfully curated, and I always leave feeling like I’ve discovered something new, even with the most famous masterpieces and even i visit multiple times a year. Truly a place where you can spend hours just soaking in the creativity and brilliance of so many iconic artists.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: MoMA has been on my bucket list for years.  Quintessential NYC experience.  van Gogh's Starry Night and more, full-on Monet Water Lilies experience, Pollock, Rothko, O'Keefe, Warhol...all the icons and more..exceptional immersive pieces as well.  $30 for entry, very reasonable.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
This museum is gorgeous. I came to see a specific piece by Van Gogh and was not disappointed. There is a queue for The Starry Night, which wasn’t as intense as the Mona Lisa at The Lourve, and everyone was patient and didn’t spend too much time for their photos.

A must visit when you are in NYC.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
Definitely a great museum to visit and explore. Getting your tickets is fairly easy as you can get them online, or get them there which is to the left after you enter. After than go to the very right and there should be an escalator that brings you upstairs where they will scan your ticket prior to you entering.

I’m definitely not an art connoisseur but the main reason I came here is because I wanted to see paintings from Van Gogh, Picasso, Dali and Monet (all floor 5) which are all artists that I heard about as early as elementary. How often do you get a chance to see a piece of art that has been through the test of time and still maintains its significance.

Although those paintings are my main reason for coming here, I did enjoy going through the other pieces of art. The thing that is really entertaining about this museum is that they have so many different arts in different mediums as each room has its own style and continually progresses. You aren’t stuck in one genre such as renaissance where everything begins to look the same.

Overall the experience took me about 1hr 50min with me going through everything but also kind of speeding through some pieces that I had no interest in.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
Spent part of my day here and still didn’t get to look at everything! This museum is giant and maze like with tons of art but such a good experience.
The capsule home exhibition they have was the main reason behind our visit, and then we spent another 2.5 hours looking at the 5th and 4th floors. There’s truly so much to see and this is not a one day visit by any means.
The museum can get very busy and some popular pieces like the starry night might be harder to get to. The notecards and information is all simple, easy to understand and super informative. It is a very popular museum but you can tell great care and love goes into every exhibit.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
What a breathtaking, beautiful & peaceful museum. Every art tells a story and every corner it’s just immaculate! The staffs were friendly, helpful and very nice and the museum itself looked very organized & easy to follow.

Also, don’t get me started on their little outside area that they have, such a beautiful spot to just chill & take a moment to relax.

I definitely recommend getting tickets online ahead of time and get there early as well! 10/10 experience, I will definitely be coming back 🖼️.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Visited here on a Wednesday and because it’s such a large building it didn’t seem crowded, but there was a line for the coat and bag check which is a nice feature. You can look, watch, observe and even touch some art pieces, without having to carry your coat and maybe bags if you’ve been shopping already. I learned that art really is in the eye of the beholder and can be in many forms. ANYTHING can be art! I started at the top on the 6th floor and worked my way down. They do have elevators for accessibility and they have a cafe that serves food and beverages. I didn’t eat there, I was overwhelmed by the art. They tell you only pictures, no fancy cameras and no videos. I spent well over 3 hours here just looking and reading about artist and their works. Some things I walked right by, others caught my eye. Some artists encourage you to be a part of their art others are hands off. Some warn you that you might be misted with water. Oh and some tickets give you admission to other sights to see in the city or a discount to see other sights.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
I absolutely love MoMA, I could easily spend the whole day here and still not feel like it’s enough. The collection is incredible, and every floor has something iconic.

Some tip here, get there early and line up before they open, then go straight to Van Gogh’s The Starry Night first while it’s still less crowded. However, in general it didn’t feel too busy, which made the visit even more enjoyable.

If you’re short on time, here’s my must-see list:
•	Yves Klein’s Blue Monochrome (Gallery 405)
•	Van Gogh’s The Starry Night & Olive Trees (Gallery 501)
•	Henri Matisse’s The Dance (Gallery 506)
•	Andy Warhol’s Campbell’s Soup Cans (Gallery 412)
•	Picasso’s Les Demoiselles d’Avignon (Gallery 502)
•	Picasso’s Girl Before a Mirror (Gallery 517)
•	Picasso’s Three Musicians (Gallery 513)
•	Klimt’s The Park & Hope II (Floor 5)
•	Dalí’s The Persistence of Memory (Gallery 517)
•	Frida Kahlo’s Fulang-Chang and I & Self-Portrait (Galleries 517 & 521)
•	Monet’s Water Lilies & Agapanthus (Gallery 515)

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
MoMA is a fantastic museum with an outstanding collection of modern art. You can see many iconic works by artists like Van Gogh and Picasso, along with a wide range of influential modern and contemporary pieces. The museum store is excellent, and both the café and ticketing process are well organized and smooth.

One small suggestion would be to provide maps at multiple spots inside the museum, as it’s easy to miss sections without one. Overall, a must-visit museum in New York for anyone interested in modern art.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
Museum of Modern Art was an absolutely amazing experience. The museum is super clean, very well organized, and the staff was incredibly helpful and kind. There is so much to see — you can easily spend hours there and still feel inspired.

The only downside was the cafeteria situation. There is only food service on the second floor, and both cafeterias had very long lines. Since the museum is huge, it would be great to have vending machines with water and beverages on each floor.

Overall, a wonderful experience and definitely worth visiting. I would happily come back again.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
Amazing experience, very well organized, with online guide for everything that's must see and it was a breeze to go through it. We only got time for the 5th and 4th floor. But if you're into visual art I consider that to be the bare minimum for you to visit if like us you're in a rush.

Originals of Stary Night with the hand strokes visible and self portrait of Van Gogh as well as other unique creations from likes of Dali, Picasso, Friday Kahlo and much more

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
I visited The Met on a Friday evening and had an outstanding experience. The Temple of Dendur looked especially stunning lit up at night — a perfect backdrop for exploring the museum after hours. The Arms and Armor collection was a major highlight, showcasing incredible craftsmanship and history. I also enjoyed the Greek and Asian galleries, each filled with striking pieces, and the European collection, which offered impressive depth and variety.

I spent about three hours there and quickly realized it wasn’t enough. The Met’s scale and quality make it easy to lose track of time, and I’m already planning a return visit to see more.

A must-visit for anyone who appreciates art, culture, or world-class museum experiences.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
We had an amazing visit to Museum of Modern Art with our family of four.

One of the best parts was that the kids were free, which makes such a difference when you’re visiting major attractions in NYC. It made the whole experience feel accessible and easy.

Seeing The Starry Night in person was a highlight. No photo really captures how powerful it feels standing in front of it. Our kids were excited to see a painting they actually recognized, and that made it even more fun.

The museum is well organized, easy to navigate, and there’s a great mix of iconic pieces and contemporary works. We spent a few hours there and easily could have stayed longer.

Definitely worth visiting, especially if you’re traveling as a family.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Great place to take the family, go on a date, or just go solo. Staff are very helpful and friendly. Good atmophere and plenty of activities and things to see. If you get hungry, they have a nice coffee bar and cafe that serve a vairety of options.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: What is there to say, it’s MOMA. Easily spent a whole day here, slowly perusing the 6 floors of galleries. Particularly enjoyed the Ruth Asawa exhibit that is currently ongoing, as well as Wilfredo Lam’s artwork. Also really appreciated the creative corner, where you can sit down in a quiet area with street views while you doodle and contribute some art of your own to the space. The extensive gift store has so much variety for getting yourself a little memento to remember your visit.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: My favorite museum in the city. I recommend starting on the top floor and then making your way down. It does get quite busy on the weekends and the best time to come is about an hour before closing on a weekday. It almost feels like you own the museum during those times!

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
I visited The Museum of Modern Art on 23 November and the experience turned out better than expected. The mix of classics and bold modern pieces keeps you engaged, from iconic paintings to sculptures that actually make you stop for a second. Some sections felt a little crowded, but moving around the place is still easy.
My favorite part was seeing completely different styles share the same space and still feel connected. If you enjoy art and want a break from the usual tourist routine, this spot is definitely worth your time.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Absolutely delighted about our visit and experience at the MoMA. We visited for the family fest and it was great! So much for the children to do, many more for the adults to see. The wait times for some exhibitions were a little long but after 4pm it was much easier. The staff was always very helpful and friendly.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Excellent museum with notable pieces across decades. There is such a vast variety of works that I'd recommend visiting even if you aren't a huge modern art fan. I would definitely plan to spend at least 1.5 hours here but preferably more. In my opinion, the MoMA is best experienced when you have plenty of time to savor and appreciate its incredible collection. The museum is reasonably crowded during peak hours but not unbearably so. Certain famous pieces always draw large crowds.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
Floor 5 is where you want to go. That’s where the legends live: Van Gogh’s Starry Night, Monet’s Water Lilies, Dali’s Persistence of Memory, Matisse, Pollock, Warhol, the list goes on. Seeing the real paint, the texture, the human effort behind each stroke is something no screen or print can capture.

The traffic flow inside can feel a bit confusing compared to other museums, but honestly, if that’s my biggest critique, that’s a win.

The attached restaurant is surprisingly good, clean, quick, and fairly priced, and the massive gift shop is worth its own visit. If you’re hunting for unique art-inspired gifts or design items, this is the place.

This isn’t just a must-visit museum; it’s the museum for anyone who longs for the salons of old, who keeps art as their phone wallpaper, who dreams in baroque and modern hues alike. Go here. Now.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
Visiting the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in New York is an unforgettable experience. From the moment you enter, the museum feels alive — a dynamic space where art, history, and imagination intersect. The permanent collection is extraordinary, featuring iconic works by Van Gogh, Picasso, Matisse, Dalí, and Warhol. Walking through these galleries is like traveling through time, yet the ever-changing installations ensure each visit feels fresh and surprising.

What sets MoMA apart is its commitment to both tradition and innovation. While the classic masterpieces captivate, the contemporary exhibitions push boundaries and invite reflection. Recent shows, from cutting-edge photography to thought-provoking design exhibitions, reveal new perspectives and voices, proving that MoMA isn’t just preserving history — it’s shaping the future of art.

The museum itself is a masterpiece of design. Open, light-filled spaces make exploring the galleries a joy, while thoughtful architecture creates natural pauses for reflection. Beyond the art, MoMA offers guided tours, digital visitor guides, a charming café, and a design store with unique finds. For those seeking deeper engagement, the library and archives are invaluable resources.

Ultimately, MoMA delivers a rich, immersive, and profoundly human experience. Whether you are an art enthusiast or simply curious about the creative world, every corner of the museum inspires awe, curiosity, and connection. It’s a place where classic works and contemporary experiments coexist seamlessly, offering a journey that is at once timeless and thrillingly current. Five stars without hesitation.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: It was an amazing gallery with a variety of pieces. It is pretty crowded on the popular pieces. But this definitely worth checking out. Children that are 16 and under are able to be admitted for free.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: This venture was on my 'Bucket List'.   It was great.   I loved every minute of it.   The art was wonderful and was all I thought it would be.    If you go, make a point of thanking the staff in each gallery.    I found they were all really nice and few of the visitors talked to them.   The food on the balcony cafe was nice and great staff.... But like most of NY it was expensive.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Pro Tip: If you want cheaper tickets, skip the official website! Buy them from third-party sites like Agoda Booking.com etc. The official price there is $30 but I was lucky and only paid $18 on Agoda. By the way the museum is fantastic, featuring lots of works by renowned artists. If you're not sure where to begin you can scan a barcode which opens a link showing the recommended art you should check out that day

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: There is so much to experience, that more than one/two visits are warranted. I was fortunate to take a 6-week art class there so my personal experience is a little different than what others may have. They do offer little portable stools so you can sit and really take in the grandeur and information of a particular artist. I'd really recommend taking the time to read the little signs and placards of any artist you may be interested in. It really helps you to grasp the artist concept for any particular piece. To me putting eyes on Claude Monet's Water Lillies was the highlight of my visit. Absolutely Epic!!!

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
Amazing place to visit with so many incredible pieces of art. I would check if there is a specific piece you want to see, some are not always on display but there is more than enough to view.

I did some research before I went to make sure I knew where the main pieces I wanted to see were which helped as I got to them before they got too busy.

I would recommend at least a few hours for a visit but you could easily spend a day here.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
The Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in NYC, founded 1929 by Rockefeller, Bliss, and Sullivan, is a leading institution for modern & contemporary art (1880s-present) with vast collections in Painting & Sculpture, Architecture & Design, Film, Photography, Prints, & more, offering exhibitions, films, talks, dining, and shopping, open daily with late hours Fridays, and specific free admission for NY residents.

Key Details:

Mission: To help people understand and enjoy modern and contemporary art, with a focus on education, collecting, and exhibitions.

Collection: Over 200,000 works, including paintings, sculptures, drawings, films, architectural models, design objects, and millions of film stills, featuring artists like Van Gogh, Picasso, Warhol, and Pollock.

Departments: Curatorial departments cover Painting & Sculpture, Architecture & Design, Film, Photography, Prints & Illustrated Books, and Video.

Location: West 53rd Street in Manhattan, New York City.

Visitor Info: Open 10:30 AM–5:30 PM (late Fridays); offers tours, dining, shopping, and free admission for NY State residents.

What to Expect:

Art: Masterpieces from the late 19th century to today, with rotating exhibitions.

Events: Film screenings, performances, art-making workshops, and talks.

Experiences: Guided tours, restaurants, and shops.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: This was my first time going to the MOMA and I fell in love! I took my kids with me and my daughter told me this time was better for her than the first time. The art is absolutely beautiful. I felt bad for one of the security guards during one of the exhibits because people kept touching the art and she looked very stressed. It was a bit crowded in certain rooms but I loved the experience. My son loved the colors.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
So crowded today at MoMA — not just the main lobby, but the café and every gallery as well.
From the turnout alone, you could say their marketing and exhibition programming are very successful.
Worth visiting, but come early or on a weekday if you prefer a quieter experience.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: The MoMA is a great chance to see modern art classics in person, even if you’re not an expert. The whole place feels modern, bright, and easy to walk through, making the visit really enjoyable. And the museum shop is fantastic — you can even take home prints and other items!

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: This is an absolute gem of a museum, very well located. Grab a bagel and coffee before you go in because you’re going to be there for many many hours. I wish I’d done my research well beforehand because I had no idea what I was getting into. If you’re in a time crunch just don’t go because you can spend an entire day on one floor alone! This place and all that it offers is massive!! You will need as much time as you can afford. An entire day would not be enough if you’re an art enthusiast or adjacent. Everyone should go here once! Thoroughly inspiring.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Attended recently to see the Hilma af Klint exhibit with my friend, a guided tour for Latino and Hispanic Heritage month, and for my first exhibit opening for Ruth Asawa. It was sort of like a party but it was also just nice to see the exhibit early. There was a dj and drinks and seasoned walnuts I think, and the outdoors area was beautiful at night. The guide was knowledgeable and provided great insight for the pieces we saw. Entry was easy every time, there’s rarely a long wait

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: Always worth a visit. The only downside is the number of people in there at the same time, which can alter the experience. I was there during a big rainstorm, so people probably stayed way longer than the usual average. But most Collections galleries were still tolerable!

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: I was there yesterday, December 19th, and I thought it was fantastic because I was able to see works by Picasso, Monet, Van Gogh, Diego Rivera, and other famous artists. I was especially impressed by the sculptures of the Japanese artist Ruth Asawa, as she uses techniques that resemble swarms, which makes these sculptures very delicate, as if they could fly on their own, especially since some were suspended in the air.  It's definitely a great museum to visit in New York.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
The Museum of Modern Art is an absolute must if you appreciate creativity in all its forms. The collection is incredible, with iconic works you recognize instantly alongside pieces that challenge you to slow down and really think about what you’re seeing. It’s the kind of place where every floor feels completely different, but all of it works together.

What stood out most was how accessible the museum feels, even with world-famous art on the walls. Whether it’s painting, sculpture, photography, film, or design, everything is curated in a way that invites curiosity instead of intimidation. You can spend time with a single piece or move through entire sections and still feel like you’re getting something meaningful out of it.

We visited in January, which made the experience even better. The crowds were manageable, giving us space to take things in without being rushed. Being able to stand in front of major works and actually absorb them—without constantly stepping aside for foot traffic—made a huge difference.

MoMA isn’t just about looking at art; it’s about ideas, perspective, and how creativity reflects the world we live in. Whether you’re deeply into art or just want to experience something inspiring, this museum delivers. Easily one of the highlights of our time in New York.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
The Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) is an absolute must-visit for anyone who appreciates creativity, innovation, and powerful artistic expression. From the moment you walk in, the space feels thoughtfully designed — modern, spacious, and perfectly curated to let the artwork truly shine.
The collection is extraordinary. Seeing iconic masterpieces in person is an unforgettable experience, but what makes MoMA special is how it balances world-famous works with contemporary and experimental pieces. Every gallery feels intentional, telling a story about how modern art has evolved over time. The mix of paintings, sculptures, photography, film, and design keeps the experience dynamic and engaging throughout.
The layout is easy to navigate, and each floor offers something different, so it never feels repetitive. I especially appreciated the natural lighting in many of the galleries, which enhances the viewing experience. The museum staff are professional and helpful, always ready to guide visitors if needed.
Beyond the art itself, the overall atmosphere is inspiring. It’s a place where you can take your time, reflect, and truly immerse yourself in creativity. The museum shop is also worth visiting, offering unique art-inspired gifts and books.
Whether you’re an art enthusiast or just curious, MoMA delivers a five-star cultural experience that stays with you long after you leave. 🎨✨

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
MoMA: A Portal Through Shadows — Toward Light

Walking through MoMA is like tracing the psychological contour of modern civilization.
You begin as an observer — but level by level, you’re pulled deeper into a living map of rupture, reflection, and encoded meaning.

Many pieces express unresolved mental and societal fractures — trauma, dislocation, fragmentation — yet all through the lens of creative intelligence.
There’s a brilliance in how pain is shaped into presence, silence into form.

Still, I left with a question:
What happens when art stops orbiting around wounds… and begins projecting futures?

What if the next evolution of art is not only to reflect what is, but to construct what could be?

I long for works that dare to envision forward — where trauma is acknowledged, but not centered.
Where beauty, clarity, and transcendence take the lead.

Because we are not just a species healing.
We are a species evolving.

✨ A deeply thoughtful and layered museum experience. MoMA challenges the mind — and invites a question: what comes after ?

text:
Absolutely breathtaking. The Museum of Modern Art is a place where you don’t just view art—you feel it. Room after room is filled with works that changed the course of art history and continue to inspire the world.

We saw masterpieces by Van Gogh, Monet, Klimt, Picasso, Dali, and Warhol—icons in every sense. Standing in front of pieces like The Starry Night or The Persistence of Memory is surreal, like stepping into the pages of an art textbook or a dream.

The layout is bright, modern, and spacious—allowing you to breathe, linger, and let each work sink in. It’s a place where time slows down and creativity surrounds you.

If you have even the slightest love for art, MoMA is a must. One of the most beautiful and inspiring places in New York City.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
Located in Midtown Manhattan, the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) is one of the most influential modern and contemporary art museums in the world. Its collection is both iconic and expansive, featuring works by artists such as Van Gogh, Picasso, Warhol, Matisse, and Kahlo—alongside photography, film, design, and new media.

Visitors can see legendary pieces like Starry Night, Les Demoiselles d’Avignon, and Campbell’s Soup Cans, while also discovering rotating exhibitions that spotlight emerging artists and timely themes. The museum’s design is sleek and open, making it easy to explore at your own pace, whether you’re staying for an hour or an afternoon.

In addition to the art, MoMA offers a serene sculpture garden, a bookstore filled with design-forward items, and a café for a quick break. The atmosphere is lively but focused, attracting everyone from art lovers and students to casual visitors curious about modern culture.

Whether you’re deeply into modern art or just looking for a cultural stop in Midtown, MoMA delivers an essential New York museum experience that’s both thought-provoking and visually striking.

The collection of modernist classics is particularly impressive, alongside the fresh, thought-provoking exhibitions from emerging artists. The museum space is thoughtfully designed, allowing you to fully immerse yourself in each artwork while maintaining a seamless flow through the entire exhibit.

The staff is incredibly welcoming and ready to assist with any questions, and the overall atmosphere creates a perfect escape from the hustle and bustle of the city.

If you’re seeking inspiration, new ideas, and a true aesthetic experience — MoMA is a must-visi

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
The Metropolitan Museum of Art is one of the most impressive museums in the world and a must-visit in New York City. The collection is incredibly diverse, covering thousands of years of history, from ancient civilizations to modern masterpieces. Every gallery feels thoughtfully curated and rich in detail, offering something fascinating for every visitor.

The museum’s atmosphere is both inspiring and welcoming, whether you are an art expert or simply exploring out of curiosity. Highlights include the European paintings, the Egyptian collection, and the beautifully designed period rooms. The size of the museum allows you to spend hours discovering new artworks with every visit.

Overall, The Met offers an unforgettable cultural experience. It is not just a museum, but a journey through human creativity, history, and imagination. Highly recommended for anyone visiting New York.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
If you’re even remotely into art—or just curious about what all the fuss is about—MoMA is a must. From the moment you step in, you’re hit with this wave of creativity that’s both grounding and otherworldly. But let’s talk about the real star of the show: Picasso.

The Picasso collection is nothing short of a visual masterclass. You don’t just see the evolution of an artist—you feel it. From the early blue period pieces dripping with melancholy to the fragmented genius of Cubism, it’s like walking through the restless mind of someone who literally changed how we see the world.

Each piece pulls you in. The colors, the chaos, the clarity—it’s an emotional rollercoaster, in the best way. Even if you’ve seen Picasso in books or online, nothing prepares you for the real thing. Standing in front of Les Demoiselles d’Avignon or Girl before a Mirror hits different. It’s raw. It’s unsettling. It’s brilliant.

The layout is intuitive, the lighting is perfect, and the staff are both helpful and low-key. It never feels rushed or overwhelming. Just the right amount of “wow” with enough space to breathe and reflect.

Whether you’re an art aficionado or a first-timer, MoMA’s Picasso collection is worth the visit alone. But lucky for you, it’s surrounded by works from Van Gogh, Matisse, Rothko, and Warhol too. Total jackpot.

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
We visited in July.

MoMA is an incredible experience for anyone interested in modern and contemporary art. The collection is vast and diverse, featuring masterpieces by artists like Van Gogh, Warhol, Picasso, and Pollock, alongside thought-provoking installations and photography.

The layout is easy to navigate, and the audio guides add a lot of context and insight to the exhibits. The staff were friendly and helpful, and the museum atmosphere felt vibrant yet contemplative.

Highlights for me included Van Gogh’s Starry Night, the sculpture garden, and the rotating special exhibitions, which always bring fresh perspectives.

The cafe and museum shop are also worth a visit — great spots to rest and pick up unique art-related gifts.

Tips:
- Buy tickets online in advance to skip lines.
- Allocate at least 2–3 hours to explore fully.
- Wear comfortable shoes — there’s a lot of ground to cover!
- Check their website for current exhibitions and events.
- Connect to the wifi and listen about each piece of art.

Overall, MoMA is an inspiring place that celebrates creativity and innovation in art — highly recommended for visitors to NYC!

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text:
MoMA
Overwhelming. Marvelous. Beautiful. Magical.

Navigating the galleries was easy, but the sheer number of visitors on that rainy day made the experience a bit overwhelming, and definitely not how I imagined spending my birthday, surrounded by strangers. I wanted to take in everything the museum had to offer, but it felt impossible. Still, I left knowing I’ll be back again (probably averaging over 20 visits this year).

You honestly need an entire day to explore it all, from the galleries to the two cafes inside, and The Modern—a two-Michelin-starred restaurant nestled next door. I kicked off my birthday there with a light brunch and cocktails with a friend before heading to the third floor of the museum to see and be art. By the time I was ready to leave, I had planned to stop by The Terrace Café for another cocktail, but between the crowd and a 20-minute wait, I let it go.

There’s no wonder MoMA is hailed as the most influential museum of modern and contemporary art. It showcases an incredible variety: design, architecture, sculpture, fashion, photography, film, illustrated books, and electronic media. It houses works by art legends—Warhol, Picasso, Van Gogh, Kahlo and many more provocative creators, with new artists as well. Some pieces I got lost in, others I snapped quickly to revisit later.

Next time, I’ll start from the top floor and work my way down—my usual museum ritual. There are plenty of places to sit and just let the art wash over you. If you love modern art and design, this is the place. Highly recommend visiting on your next trip to NYC.

I bought my ticket online and there was no line getting into the building or checking into the museum. The wait line for cost check in was quite a stretch though. I had to use the bathroom, it was clean and artsy with modern design. The bookstore had so many gifts, books and more!

#MoMA #WhattodoinNYC

title: The Museum of Modern Art
text: This is one of the premier art museums in the United States, renowned for its exceptional collection of modern and contemporary masterpieces. Highlights include iconic works by Claude Monet, Vincent van Gogh, and Paul Cézanne, with some of the most celebrated pieces on display—such as The Starry Night, Olive Trees, and Monet’s Water Lilies. The museum is centrally and conveniently located at the intersection of Fifth Avenue and 53rd Street in New York City, with easy access via the orange subway lines (B, D, F, and M). Its world-class exhibitions, dynamic programming, and architecturally striking galleries make it a must-visit destination for art lovers and tourists alike.

title: The National Gallery
text:
Shortly after New Year, I visited the National Gallery in London with my relatives — my sister and her husband, who were visiting England for the first time. Unfortunately, this visit turned into a very unpleasant and humiliating experience.
When we entered the gallery, we wanted to take a photo together. I intended to use a selfie stick. We briefly placed our jackets on a bench in the main hall while I was trying to set up my phone. Suddenly, a male member of staff rushed toward us from behind and shouted something very loudly. I did not understand what he said. Thinking that perhaps the issue was about leaving jackets on the bench, we immediately picked them up.
After that, I continued adjusting my phone on the selfie stick so we could take one photo together. At that moment, the same staff member rushed at us again, this time shouting aggressively and very rudely, telling us that selfie sticks were not allowed.
I have no problem with rules. If selfie sticks are not permitted, I fully respect that. However, the way this rule was communicated was completely unacceptable. This man was extremely aggressive, loud, and rude. He could have politely explained the rule, especially considering that this is a world-famous museum visited by tourists from all over the world — many of whom do not speak English fluently or at all. Not everyone will understand something shouted at them once, from behind, in a hostile tone.
I felt deeply embarrassed and ashamed in front of my relatives, who were visiting England for the first time and were shocked to experience such aggression in a museum.
Two years ago, I visited this same gallery with my sister, and both we and other visitors used selfie sticks without any comments from staff. If the rules have changed since then — or even if they existed before — visitors deserve to have them explained calmly and respectfully.
I even approached this staff member to ask a simple question: what is the difference between holding the phone on a selfie stick or holding it in my hand to take a selfie? He gave no clear answer, kept walking away, and behaved as if I had to chase him just to get an explanation.
I later spoke to a manager, explained the situation, and pointed out this staff member (whose photo I am attaching below). I asked whether such aggressive behaviour towards visitors is considered normal in this museum.
Museums should be places of culture, respect, and education. Staff members represent the values of the institution. Unfortunately, this individual showed a complete lack of professionalism, politeness, and basic human respect. I truly hope this behaviour is addressed so that other visitors do not have to go through a similar experience.

This incident completely ruined our day from the very beginning. What was supposed to be a pleasant cultural visit turned into stress and discomfort. After this aggressive interaction, the mood was gone, and it was very hard to enjoy the gallery or the rest of our day in London.

title: The National Gallery
text:
The National Gallery is a cornerstone of global art and a non-negotiable London highlight. Its free, encyclopedic collection in Trafalgar Square lets you stand inches from works by Van Gogh, da Vinci, Turner, and Rembrandt—a overwhelming privilege. The key to a rewarding visit, however, is strategy over stamina.

My strong advice is to target your interests ruthlessly. The gallery is arranged chronologically; decide if you're here for the Italian Renaissance, the Dutch Golden Age, or the French Impressionists. Use the free Highlights Trail map for a superb 90-minute curated route. Never go at midday on a weekend; instead, visit at opening time or on a Friday evening for relative calm. While entry to the permanent collection is free, the paid exhibitions are consistently world-class and worth budgeting for.

Ultimately, this is a museum to be enjoyed in focused bursts. Don't try to "complete" it. Choose a wing, immerse yourself, and then relax in the café overlooking Trafalgar Square. It’s an institution that democratizes genius, offering a direct, powerful connection to centuries of human creativity. With a simple plan, it becomes not just awe-inspiring, but personally enriching.

title: The National Gallery
text:
The National Gallery is truly one of London’s crown jewels. Located right in Trafalgar Square, it’s home to an incredible collection of paintings that span centuries, from the early Renaissance to the Impressionists and beyond. And amazingly, entry to the main collection is completely free!

Walking through the grand halls, you get to see masterpieces from legends like Van Gogh, Monet, Rembrandt, Turner, Botticelli, and so many more. Each room feels like stepping into a different era of art history, and the layout makes it easy to wander, discover, and lose track of time.

The building itself is stunning, both outside and inside, with beautiful architecture and plenty of places to sit and reflect. The staff are friendly, knowledgeable, and always happy to help if you’re looking for a specific painting or artist.

The National Gallery also offers excellent temporary exhibitions (some ticketed), and their gift shop and cafes are well worth a stop. It's a wonderful experience whether you're an art lover or just curious about seeing some of the most famous paintings in the world up close.

If you’re in London, visiting The National Gallery is absolutely essential — inspiring, calming, and unforgettable.

title: The National Gallery
text:
The National Gallery is absolutely breathtaking. The level of detail in the paintings is astonishing — many of them feel more like high-resolution photographs than paintings. When you zoom in and really observe, you notice textures, facial expressions, reflections, landscapes, and tiny architectural details that are simply unreal.
The galleries are spacious, beautifully curated, and very well organized. You can easily spend hours inside without feeling overwhelmed. It was great to see many school groups as well — this is exactly the kind of place where art, history, and education come together.
The landscapes, mountains, castles, animals, and human faces show an incredible level of skill and patience. You can clearly tell that these works took enormous time, dedication, and mastery to complete.
A world-class museum, completely free, and one of the finest cultural experiences in London. A must-visit for anyone who appreciates art, history, and true craftsmanship.

title: The National Gallery

During my visit, I explored several different exhibitions. Each of these exhibitions showcased remarkable artworks that captured my attention. The displayed works were highly diverse, encompassing a blend of styles and various artistic periods. Viewing these beautiful pieces provided an inspiring and unforgettable experience.

The National Gallery in London is a place that every art enthusiast should visit at least once. Its inspiring atmosphere and valuable artworks create a lasting impression in the minds of all visitors.

title: The National Gallery
text:
I’m  back! What a great place to revisit! The National Gallery truly is one of those gems that deserves more than just a quick walk-through. The depth of the collection, the historical richness, and the generosity of making it free for everyone.
The fact that photography is allowed makes it even better! You can take a piece of the experience with you and reflect on it later (or share it with others who haven’t yet had the chance to visit). The archives, especially, are underrated. It’s not just about the iconic paintings; it's about the journey through centuries of art and culture.
If you’re into history and art, this gallery is a must to visit.

title: The National Gallery
text:
Amazing experience at The National Gallery!

Visited The National Gallery in Trafalgar Square and it was absolutely fantastic. The collection is world-class — from iconic masterpieces by Van Gogh, Da Vinci, and Turner to lesser-known gems that are equally captivating. The galleries are well laid-out and easy to navigate, with clear information on each piece. I appreciated the free admission, which makes this incredible museum accessible to everyone.

The lighting and display really showcase the art beautifully, and there are plenty of benches to sit and absorb the atmosphere. Staff were friendly and helpful whenever I had questions. If you love art or just want to spend a few hours surrounded by beauty and history, this is a must-visit in London!

Would definitely recommend to friends and family — unforgettable experience! 🎨👏

title: The National Gallery
text:
It is easy to forget how many recognisable masterpieces are in this gallery, but everywhere you turn there is one! From Holbein’s “The Ambassadors” to Stubbs’ “Whistlejacket” and Van Dyke’s King Charles I, to Van Eyk and paintings of Cardinal Richelieu.

And then there is the “Fighting Fighting Temeraire”, Britain’s favourite painting…

title: The National Gallery
text: The National Gallery is one of the most impressive museums in London and an absolute must for anyone who appreciates art, history or culture. The collection is incredible, with world-famous masterpieces that you’ve seen in books and online, but experiencing them in person is on a completely different level. Seeing works like Van Gogh, Monet and so many iconic paintings up close makes the visit feel truly special. The museum is well organized, easy to navigate and free to enter, which makes it even better. It’s important that you book your ticket online in advance, because it’s one of the most famous and touristic museums in the city. It’s a perfect place to spend a few hours exploring, slowing down and really taking in the art, especially if you enjoy meaningful experiences while traveling. Loved it so much and couldn’t recommend more. 10/10 experience, for sure!

I wasn't prepared for the emotional reaction I experienced with some of the paintings. I've dismissed a lot of it in the passed, having seen pictures or footage of some pieces, but actually being in front of it is a very different experience. And now I get it. I highly recommend this.

title: The National Gallery
text:
🖼️ My Visit to The National Gallery, Trafalgar Square, London

My visit to The National Gallery in Trafalgar Square was an inspiring journey through centuries of European art. Founded in 1824, the gallery houses one of the world’s greatest art collections, featuring masterpieces by Da Vinci, Van Gogh, Monet, Rembrandt, Raphael, and Turner. Each room offers a window into different art movements, from the Renaissance to Impressionism.

The gallery’s architecture itself is stunning, and its location overlooking Trafalgar Square adds to the experience. Entry is free, making it an accessible cultural highlight for all visitors.

Visitor Tips: Allow at least 2 hours to explore. Photography is allowed in most areas (without flash). Visit early morning or late afternoon for a quieter experience, and don’t miss the gift shop and café inside.

How to Reach: The gallery is located at Trafalgar Square, London WC2N 5DN, easily reached by Charing Cross, Leicester Square, or Piccadilly Circus Underground stations.

Overall, The National Gallery is a must-visit for art lovers — a peaceful escape in the heart of London filled with creativity, history, and timeless beauty.

title: The National Gallery
text:
My visit to the National Gallery was nothing short of unforgettable. Walking through its galleries, I felt a genuine sense of awe at being surrounded by such a powerful collection of masterpieces. To stand before Van Gogh’s expressive brushwork, Monet’s shifting light and Turner’s atmospheric drama was both humbling and exhilarating. I felt as if the paintings were breathing alongside us.

I loved the curation of the artworks; the light, the space, the quiet reverence of the galleries combined to create an atmosphere that feels immersive and deeply moving. I found myself lingering longer than expected, caught between admiration and reflection.

For me, the National Gallery was a sanctuary of creativity and history, a place where art touches something profoundly human.

text: The world’s greatest painting gallery. All of the great masters are here, represented most often by a stone cold masterpiece—Caravaggio’s Supper at Emmaus, Titian’s Poesie, Veronese’s Darius Before Alexander, Parmigianino’s Vision of St Jerome, del Piombo’s Lazarus… it just goes on and on and on, in the best possible way. Equally as impressive as the paintings is the ease in which everyone waltzes in and out of the museum, which is always free. The relaxed, casual, open and friendly atmosphere sets it apart from most other museums—it feels like you’re wandering about a giant living room filled with centuries of exquisite art. The recent rehang of the paintings is a smashing success, and the newly refurbished Sainsbury Wing now offers more space and updated facilities. An unparalleled immersive experience in the river of European painting. Unmissable.

title: The National Gallery
text: The National Gallery was one of my favorite places in London! As an artist and history enthusiast, I was very pleased with the varied collection of pieces in this museum. From Van Gogh to Rembrandt, this space has something for everyone! I visited on a Sunday two and a half hours before closing time, and while it was busy, there were no entry lines and the exhibitions were very easy to walk through. Collections were divided by time period and movement, making it easy to understand even for people who are not familiar with art history. All the pieces were perfectly labeled and displayed, portraying the cleanliness and professionalism of the organization. The staff was very friendly, and the guards kept everything in order without being rude or disrespectful. When closing time was approaching (around 15 to 10 minutes before), everyone was asked to walk towards the main entrance, getting everyone out of the establishment by 6:00pm on the dot. On another hand, I was very surprised that the entrance was free for everyone, given that I have visited poorer museums that I have had to pay a good amount of money for. Overall, I loved this museum and will definitely come back when I visit London again!

title: The National Gallery
text:
The National Gallery is not a single building but a fascinating, evolving structure that tells a story of architectural compromise, ambition, and adaptation over two centuries. Located prominently on the north side of Trafalgar Square, its architecture presents a powerful contrast between Neoclassical tradition and Postmodern modernism.

The Wilkins Building: Neoclassical Grandeur
The main, central building, known as the Wilkins Building, is the iconic face of the National Gallery, designed by William Wilkins and opened in 1838.

Appearance and Style: It is a prime example of Greek Revival architecture, featuring a long, relatively low facade topped by a triangular pediment and flanked by impressive Corinthian columns. It was intended to be a "temple to art," giving the new national collection a prestigious public home accessible to all social classes.

The Compromise: The design was notoriously criticized upon opening. Wilkins was forced to compromise due to budgetary constraints and the requirement to incorporate an existing barracks building behind it, which led to a structure that many contemporaries found too shallow and lacking the grandeur of the Louvre or other European galleries. The columns themselves were salvaged from the demolished Carlton House.

Adaptation and Expansion: The building has been continually expanded over the decades to house the growing collection. Notable additions include the central Barry Rooms (1872–1876), designed by E. M. Barry, which added a beautiful central octagonal dome, and other extensions that have subtly expanded the gallery's footprint while retaining the Neoclassical feel of the main public spaces.

The Sainsbury Wing: Postmodern Intervention
The most significant architectural addition is the Sainsbury Wing, which opened in 1991 on the western side of the complex.

Context and Controversy: The initial design for an extension was famously rejected after Prince Charles described it as a "monstrous carbuncle." The final, Grade I-listed design was done by the American architectural pioneers Robert Venturi and Denise Scott Brown, masters of Postmodern architecture.

Postmodern Dialogue: The Sainsbury Wing deliberately uses the same Neoclassical vocabulary—limestone cladding, columns, and cornices—as the Wilkins Building, but with postmodern inflection. It mimics the style but uses a different, more spare grammar. For instance, columns appear where they are not structurally necessary, reinforcing a feeling of weight and anticipation.

Interior Experience: The interior was specifically designed to house the gallery's early Renaissance paintings (13th to 16th centuries). The galleries feature white walls and grey pietra serena sandstone columns, deliberately evoking the ambiance of a Florentine Renaissance church or palazzo. The architects created a sense of procession, leading visitors from a more compressed entrance hall (recently brightened in a 2024 refurbishment by Selldorf Architects) up a grand staircase and into the beautifully lit exhibition rooms.

title: The National Gallery
text: The National Gallery is one of the world’s great cultural treasures, home to masterpieces that shaped the course of Western art and, with it, Western thought. Walking through its halls feels like traveling through centuries of creativity, from the Renaissance to Impressionism, with works by legends such as Van Gogh, Da Vinci, Turner, and countless others. Beyond the sheer beauty of the collection, the Gallery offers a profound reminder of how art reflects and influences the values, struggles, and dreams of society. The building itself is magnificent, right in Trafalgar Square, and admission is free—making some of humanity’s greatest achievements accessible to all. An absolute gem, both for art lovers and anyone seeking to understand the roots of Western culture.

title: The National Gallery
text:
My visit to the National Gallery was on 15/11/2025 (Monday), after lunch with the intention of spending maybe a couple of hours. Boy was I wrong, I spent +4 hours and it wasn't enough to see everything! At some point I'll have to come back to view the 19th-20th collection.

And did I mention it's FREE ENTRY? But of course donations are always welcome. Some tips to maximise your time there:

• Pay for the rental museum guide headset, it's worth its weight in gold. This way you won't miss all the historical information and interesting side stories about the paintings. This will also help you decide your route, because you can choose to see the paintings chronologically or based on style.

• Because of the museum audio guide, I decided these are my favourites paintings so far: St Catherine of Alexandria by Rafael (1507), St Ursula by French painter Claude Gellée (1604/5), Venus and Mars (1485).

• Don't forget to pay attention to the architecture if the weather is good, and go out and spend enough time to appreciate the different parts of the building exteriors too:

- For the indoor experience start with the main centrepiece of classical architecture represented by the William Wilkins building (1838).

- Then there's the grand museum expansion by Edward Barry in the form of Greek cross plan interpreted in the Neo-Renaissance style (1872-1876).

- There are also Victorian era and Beaux Arts rooms/galleries.

- Finally there's a 20thC extension in late modernist architectural style, including the Sainsbury Wing by Robert Venturi and Denise Scott Brown.

title: The National Gallery
text:
The National Gallery in London is an unparalleled celebration of art, boasting an impressive collection of over 2,000 Western European paintings from the 13th to the 19th centuries. Located in the heart of Trafalgar Square, the gallery features masterpieces by renowned artists such as Van Gogh, Da Vinci, Botticelli, and Turner. The layout of the gallery is both accessible and immersive, offering visitors a chance to journey through the evolution of art styles and techniques across centuries.

Admission is free, which makes it an even more remarkable cultural gem. Whether you're an art connoisseur or a casual visitor, the experience of standing before iconic works like Van Gogh's *Sunflowers* or Turner’s *The Fighting Temeraire* is truly awe-inspiring. The gallery also provides educational resources, temporary exhibitions, and a charming café to round out the experience.

title: The National Gallery
text:
🌟 First Impressions & Highlights
Walking up to The National Gallery on Trafalgar Square itself feels momentous. The grand façade, the sense of history, the buzz of the city around you — you already know you’re about to see something special.
Inside, the collection is jaw‑dropping. Paintings span centuries (from the 13th to the 19th), so you get everything from early masters up through romantic and impressionist works. Big names you expect to see are all there: Van Gogh, Turner, Caravaggio, Rembrandt, etc. Every gallery has gems, not just a few show pieces. Experiencing the “greats” in person — the brushwork, the scale, the colors — is something photos or books don’t fully capture.
One of the newer highlights is the Sainsbury Wing (recently renovated), which feels brighter, more accessible, with better lighting for many of the Renaissance pieces. The way artworks are hung now helps you see connections between artists, styles, subjects. It doesn’t feel like just randomly assembled rooms; there’s a flow, a narrative you can follow.
Also, even though many famous works are free to see (the permanent collection), some special exhibitions do cost — and they’re usually well curated, well presented.
👍 What Works Really Well
Diversity & depth of the collection. Whether you're into early Renaissance, landscapes, portraits or late‑19th century, there’s a lot to admire.
Quality of display. The renovated wings (especially Sainsbury) make a difference: the lighting, spacing, overall ambience feel carefully considered.
Location & accessibility. Right in central London, easy to reach. Because entry to the main collection is free, it’s very visitor‑friendly.
Atmosphere. There’s a peacefulness in many rooms, even though it's a major gallery. When you find a quiet spot, the experience becomes contemplative, almost meditative.

The layout is user-friendly, making it easy to navigate through the various exhibits.

Spending time in the gallery feels enriching—the artwork is stunning, and the audio guides provide insightful context that enhances the experience. Additionally, the square's lively atmosphere, with its street performers and nearby restaurants, makes it a great spot to take a break after exploring the gallery.

title: The National Gallery
text: The National Gallery in London is located at the heart of Trafalgar Square, one of the city’s most iconic public spaces, distinguished by its fountains, Nelson’s Column, and the monumental statues of four black lions. The gallery stands close to the National Portrait Gallery, but the two institutions are distinct. While the National Portrait Gallery is dedicated to portraits of historically significant British figures—including an exceptional collection of Tudor portraits of Henry VIII, Anne Boleyn, Mary I, and Elizabeth I—the National Gallery focuses on a broader artistic tradition. It houses one of the world’s most comprehensive collections of European painting, spanning from the late Middle Ages to the early 20th century. Its collection includes masterpieces by Leonardo da Vinci, Raphael, Michelangelo, Rembrandt, Monet, Cézanne, Picasso, and Van Gogh. Among its most celebrated works are Van Gogh’s Sunflowers and Van Gogh’s Chair, Monet’s Water-Lilies, Leonardo da Vinci’s Virgin of the Rocks and The Virgin and Child with Saint Anne and Saint John the Baptist, Raphael’s The Madonna of the Pinks and The Ansidei Madonna, and Michelangelo’s The Entombment. The National Gallery is not only a museum but also a cultural institution, offering free access to its permanent collection, making some of the greatest works of Western art accessible to all.
